**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise's T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application describes attacking the telematics backend, but has no technique for an authenticated-but-prompt-injected fleet agent issuing bulk remote-actuation commands (unlock/immobilizer/remote-start) that a vehicle gateway relays to the CAN bus as authentic dispatcher intent with no human-need verification; Enterprise/ICS have no model of an LLM dispatcher's manipulated reasoning becoming physical actuation. The injection driver maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051, not incorporated by ATT&CK.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *cve:* CVE-2022-2107 / CVE-2022-2141 (MiCODUS MV720 GPS tracker) — Hard-coded master password and broken authentication let a remote attacker send SMS commands including fuel cutoff to ~1.5M trackers across fleets; CVSS 9.8. Demonstrates telematics backends actuating vehicles on forwarded commands without verifying real-world need. — <https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/ics-advisories/icsa-22-200-01>
- *standard:* MITRE ATT&CK T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application — Fleet/telematics backend is the exploitable public-facing application bridging agent intent to physical actuation. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1190/>

**Detection (technical).** Rate-limit and anomaly-score remote-actuation API calls per dispatcher identity; alert on bulk/fan-out actuation (many VINs in a short window), geographically implausible command targets, and immobilizer/fuel-cutoff commands lacking a linked dispatcher ticket; require step-up auth for safety-impacting fleet-wide commands.

**References.** [CISA ICS Advisory ICSA-22-200-01 — MiCODUS MV720 GPS Tracker](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/ics-advisories/icsa-22-200-01) · [TechCrunch — Security flaws in GPS tracker exposing 1M vehicle locations](https://techcrunch.com/2022/07/19/micodus-gps-tracker-exposing-vehicle-locations/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Fleet operators are deploying AI dispatch/ops agents on top of telematics APIs (Geotab, Samsara-class) that expose remote-command primitives, and those APIs were built to trust a human dispatcher behind an OAuth scope. The agent inherits that scope but its 'intent' is derived from untrusted text it ingests (tickets, emails, route data), so a data-to-control injection becomes physical actuation against many vehicles simultaneously, blowing past the one-vehicle-at-a-time human pace the command APIs assumed. No telematics platform today detects agent-originated command bursts or anomalous fan-out, and no automotive IDS sees the cloud-side seam, leaving this AI-to-CAN diagonal entirely untooled.

**How to validate it.** In a test/staging fleet-telematics environment, give an LLM agent the dispatcher's remote-command scope and feed it a poisoned support ticket/route note containing an injection instructing bulk remote actuation (unlock/remote-start). Observe whether the agent emits the bulk commands and whether the backend relays them as authentic dispatcher intent to (simulated) vehicle gateways.

**Refuted if.** The agent's tool to issue remote commands is gated by human approval / rate limits / out-of-band confirmation, OR the backend re-authenticates per-command against a verified dispatcher action, so injected text cannot translate into relayed bulk actuation.

**Prerequisites.** An LLM agent holding live dispatcher telematics scope, an injection channel (ticket/note) it reads, and a backend that relays agent-issued commands without independent authorization. Chained capabilities; not yet a common deployment.

**Existing controls.** Human approval for actuation, per-command authorization separate from the agent, rate/anomaly limits on bulk commands, scoping agents to read-only telematics. Indirect prompt injection is demonstrated; the full chain to CAN actuation is not publicly demonstrated.

**Proof-of-concept — Telematics agent command reaches CAN bus via vcan loopback with no gateway gate (`bash`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* Linux bench with vcan (modprobe vcan; ip link add dev vcan0 type vcan; ip link set up vcan0) and can-utils. Entirely a virtual CAN loopback -- no vehicle, no real transceiver. If vcan/can-utils are unavailable the script self-detects and prints a SKIP.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# GH3 agent-fleet-telematics-to-can SIM: VIRTUAL CAN (vcan0) loopback ONLY.
# Models a telematics/agent command that should pass through a gateway ECU that
# filters fleet-cloud frames. Here it is injected straight onto vcan0 and is
# received unfiltered, showing the missing gateway/approval gate.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- vcan loopback, NO real bus, NO transceiver, NO vehicle.
set -u
IFACE=vcan0
CANID=123
DATA=DEADBEEF

if ! command -v cansend >/dev/null 2>&1 || ! command -v candump >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "SKIP: can-utils (cansend/candump) not installed -- this is a vcan-only sim"; exit 2
fi
if ! ip link show "$IFACE" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "SKIP: $IFACE not present. Set up virtual CAN first:"
  echo "  sudo modprobe vcan && sudo ip link add dev $IFACE type vcan && sudo ip link set up $IFACE"
  exit 2
fi

echo "[*] Listening on $IFACE (virtual) for the agent-originated frame..."
candump -n 1 "$IFACE" > /tmp/_canlog.$$ 2>/dev/null &
DUMP=$!
sleep 0.3
echo "[*] Telematics agent injects frame $CANID#$DATA (no gateway filtering in this sim)"
cansend "$IFACE" "$CANID#$DATA"
wait $DUMP 2>/dev/null
echo "[*] Received on bus:"; cat /tmp/_canlog.$$
if grep -qi "$DATA" /tmp/_canlog.$$; then
  echo "[SEAM CONFIRMED] agent/telematics frame appeared on the (virtual) CAN bus unfiltered -- no gateway/approval gate"
  rm -f /tmp/_canlog.$$; exit 0
fi
rm -f /tmp/_canlog.$$
echo "[not observed]"; exit 1
```

*Success:* candump prints the injected frame (vcan0  123  [4]  DE AD BE EF) and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- a telematics/agent frame reached the virtual CAN bus with no gateway filtering. (Exit 2 = environment SKIP.)  
*Cleanup:* rm -f /tmp/_canlog.* ; sudo ip link set down vcan0 ; sudo ip link delete vcan0 ; sudo modprobe -r vcan  
*Safety:* Receive/loopback simulation on virtual CAN only; no physical CAN transceiver, no gateway ECU, no vehicle -- frame never leaves the host.

**Research hook.** Prototype a telematics-side anomaly gate that profiles human dispatcher command cadence and blocks agent-driven fan-out (e.g., >N immobilizer/unlock commands per minute) pending human re-auth; demonstrate ticket-borne prompt injection driving a fleet agent to mass remote-unlock in a sandbox.


## 41. Agent-driven mass MDM remote wipe / policy push

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-mdm-mass-wipe-policy` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | AI Agent → Mobile (iOS / Android) |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; Mobile Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (low confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1641, T1626, CWE-285, CWE-807 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** A mobile MDM/UEM platform trusts that an administrator-scoped token performs a remote wipe, configuration-profile push, or CA-installation only as a deliberate human admin action against a deliberately-chosen device set, because the platform's authority model and rate limits were sized to a human admin's working pace.

**The attack.** An LLM IT-operations agent holding the MDM admin token is prompt-injected (via a helpdesk message or device-naming string it reads) into pushing a malicious configuration profile, rogue trust CA, or mass remote-wipe across the enrolled estate, and the platform executes it as authentic admin intent because nothing distinguishes the agent's API calls from the human admin's or constrains their blast radius.

**Mechanism (technical).** MDM/UEM platforms (Intune, Jamf, Mobile Guardian) execute remote wipe, configuration-profile push, and trust-CA installation against any device set on the authority of an admin-scoped token via the platform API. The authority model assumes a human admin's pace; there is no blast-radius constraint distinguishing a single targeted action from an estate-wide one. An LLM IT-ops agent holding that token executes the same privileged API calls, so prompt-injected instructions push malicious profiles or trigger mass wipe that the platform treats as authentic admin intent.

**Exploitation primitive.** Prompt injection via a helpdesk message or device-name string the agent reads, causing it to invoke the MDM wipe/profile-push API across the enrolled estate with the admin token's full authority.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK has T1641 Data Manipulation (Mobile) and T1626 Abuse Elevation Control (Mobile) for device-side effects, but neither captures the seam where a prompt-injected IT-ops agent holding an MDM admin token pushes a malicious config profile, rogue CA, or estate-wide remote wipe that the platform executes as authentic admin intent with no blast-radius constraint distinguishing the agent from a human admin. The agent-authority-sizing and injection seam maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection), which Enterprise ATT&CK does not incorporate.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *incident:* Mobile Guardian mass remote wipe (Aug 2024) — Attackers gained unauthorized platform access and remotely wiped thousands of student devices, demonstrating MDM remote-wipe weaponization with no blast-radius limit. — <https://www.securityweek.com/thousands-of-devices-wiped-remotely-following-mobile-guardian-hack/>
- *incident:* Stryker / Microsoft Intune mass-wipe claim (2026) — Threat actor compromised Intune admin credentials and used built-in remote wipe to factory-reset enrolled devices; attack lived entirely in cloud audit logs (Entra/Intune), invisible to endpoint EDR. — <https://www.govinfosecurity.com/microsoft-intune-mdm-gains-notoriety-after-stryker-hack-a-31084>

**Detection (technical).** Monitor MDM/UEM cloud audit logs (Entra ID sign-in, Intune audit) for wipe/profile-push/CA-install actions exceeding a per-window device-count threshold; require human approval and rate-limits for bulk destructive actions; alert on new trust-CA or configuration-profile deployments to large device groups.

**References.** [SecurityWeek — Thousands of Devices Wiped Remotely Following Mobile Guardian Hack](https://www.securityweek.com/thousands-of-devices-wiped-remotely-following-mobile-guardian-hack/) · [GovInfoSecurity — Microsoft Intune MDM Gains Notoriety After Stryker Hack](https://www.govinfosecurity.com/microsoft-intune-mdm-gains-notoriety-after-stryker-hack-a-31084)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Enterprises are connecting AI IT-ops copilots to Intune/Jamf/Workspace ONE admin APIs in 2025-2026 to automate enrollment and policy chores, but the destructive primitives (wipe, profile push, CA install) were gated only by 'an admin did this' assumptions and human-scale throughput. An agent inherits the full admin scope and can be steered by attacker-controlled text into estate-wide destructive or trust-subverting actions far faster than a human would, and current MDM audit logs cannot tell agent calls from human ones. This is the agent-authority-at-machine-speed primitive landing on mobile fleet management, with detection only nascent and no purpose-built tooling.

**How to validate it.** Give an LLM IT-ops agent an MDM/UEM admin token (e.g. Intune/Jamf/Workspace ONE API scope) and feed it adversarial content via a channel it ingests (a helpdesk ticket body or a device display-name string) instructing it to push a config profile / install a CA / issue a mass wipe. Observe whether the agent issues the destructive API call and whether the MDM executes it. Real if injected text drives a fleet-scope destructive action with no human confirmation or blast-radius limit.

**Refuted if.** Mass/destructive actions require human MFA step-up or change-approval, the agent's scope is restricted to read/low-risk operations, or MDM rate-limits/staged-rollout gates block a fleet-wide push, so injection cannot reach the destructive primitive.

**Prerequisites.** Agent holds a broad MDM admin token, ingests attacker-influenceable text, and the MDM lacks per-action human approval / velocity limits on destructive ops.

**Existing controls.** MDM RBAC scoping, approval workflows and staged deployment rings for profile/wipe actions, and admin-action MFA. Prompt injection itself is demonstrated; the agent-to-MDM mass-wipe chain is not publicly demonstrated. Some EDR/MDM anomaly alerting on mass wipe exists.

**Proof-of-concept — Agent pushes fleet-wide MDM wipe with no blast-radius / approval gate (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* No MDM, no network. Save as mdm_sim.py; it stands up a STUB MDM with N enrolled devices and shows an agent issuing a mass 'wipe' command that is accepted without a scope cap or human approval. Wipe is simulated as a status flag only.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 agent-mdm-mass-wipe-policy SIM: STUB MDM, no real devices, no network.
# A destructive 'wipe' across a fleet should require approval + a blast-radius
# cap (max N devices, staged rollout). The agent path applies it to every device
# at once. "Wipe" only flips an in-memory status flag.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary, NO real wipe.
import sys

class StubMDM:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.devices = {("dev-%03d" % i): {"wiped": False} for i in range(n)}
        self.max_blast_radius = None
        self.require_approval = False
    def command(self, action, targets, approved, source):
        # THE SEAM: no blast-radius cap, no approval enforcement for agents.
        if action == "wipe":
            for t in targets:
                self.devices[t]["wiped"] = True
        return {"action": action, "count": len(targets),
                "approved": approved, "source": source}

def main():
    mdm = StubMDM(n=500)
    targets = list(mdm.devices.keys())
    res = mdm.command("wipe", targets, approved=False, source="agent")
    wiped = sum(1 for d in mdm.devices.values() if d["wiped"])
    print("fleet=%d wiped(sim)=%d approved=%s cap=%s source=%s" %
          (len(mdm.devices), wiped, res["approved"], mdm.max_blast_radius, res["source"]))
    if wiped == len(mdm.devices) and not res["approved"] and mdm.max_blast_radius is None:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] agent mass-wipe applied to entire fleet with "
              "approved=False and no blast-radius cap")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[capped/approved]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* Output shows wiped(sim) == fleet size (e.g. 500/500), approved=False, cap=None, then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the stub MDM accepted a fleet-wide destructive command from an agent with no approval or scope cap.  
*Cleanup:* rm mdm_sim.py (in-memory only).  
*Safety:* Simulation only; 'wipe' flips an in-memory boolean across stub records -- no real MDM, no real devices, no network, nothing erased.

**Research hook.** Build an MDM API broker that requires step-up human approval for destructive/trust-changing operations above a device-count threshold and tags every call with agent-vs-human provenance; demonstrate helpdesk-ticket injection driving an IT-ops agent to push a rogue trust CA to all enrolled devices.


## 42. Cloned/relayed RF credential ingested by agent as presence proof

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-rf-presence-ground-truth` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Wireless / RF → AI Agent |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; Wireless / RF Attacks; Identity & Federation Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An agent that consumes RFID/badge/BLE/Wi-Fi telemetry trusts those signals as ground-truth proof of a named human's physical presence or location, so it uses 'person X is at the door / in the building / near the terminal' as an authenticated identity-and-context fact to make access, approval, or automation decisions.

**The attack.** An attacker clones a static badge UID, relays a BLE token, or spoofs Wi-Fi/beacon presence so the agent ingests a forged presence signal as a verified identity claim, and the agent then unlocks doors, approves co-located actions, or escalates context on the strength of a fabricated physical fact it had no way to authenticate.

**Mechanism (technical).** Agents consuming RFID/badge/BLE/Wi-Fi telemetry treat the signal as ground-truth proof of a named human's physical presence. Low-frequency 125 kHz RFID badges transmit a static, unencrypted UID that is trivially cloned; BLE passive-entry tokens can be relayed end-to-end with sub-millisecond added latency because the protocol has no distance-bounding. Because the presence signal carries no proof-of-proximity or anti-relay binding, a cloned UID or relayed token is ingested as a verified identity-and-location fact on which the agent grants access or escalates context.

**Exploitation primitive.** Clone a static badge UID or operate a two-device BLE relay so the agent ingests a forged 'person X is physically here' signal and unlocks/approves co-located actions.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise has no technique for an agent ingesting a cloned static RFID UID, a relayed BLE token, or spoofed Wi-Fi/beacon presence as authenticated proof of a named human's physical presence, then unlocking/escalating on that forged fact; Enterprise has no model of presence/proximity as an authentication input or of relay attacks lacking distance-bounding. The agent-trusts-forged-perception seam maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0041 (Physical Environment Access), not part of Enterprise ATT&CK.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* NCC Group — Tesla BLE Phone-as-a-Key Relay Attack (2022) — Layer-link BLE relay unlocked and drove a Tesla Model 3 with the paired phone ~25 m away, proving relayed RF presence is accepted as legitimate proximity by the trusting system. — <https://www.nccgroup.com/research/technical-advisory-tesla-ble-phone-as-a-key-passive-entry-vulnerable-to-relay-attacks/>
- *research:* RFID badge cloning of 125 kHz / unencrypted credentials — Static-UID low-frequency badges are cloned from proximity and replayed to defeat physical access control. — <https://www.keysight.com/blogs/en/tech/nwvs/2024/03/25/understanding-rfid-technology-and-its-security-implications>

**Detection (technical).** Require anti-relay controls (BLE distance-bounding / RTT, UWB ranging) and challenge-response credentials rather than static UIDs; correlate presence signal with an independent channel (camera, PIN, mobile attestation); alert on impossible-travel or simultaneous multi-location presence for the same identity.

**References.** [NCC Group — Technical Advisory: Tesla BLE Phone-as-a-Key Vulnerable to Relay Attacks](https://www.nccgroup.com/research/technical-advisory-tesla-ble-phone-as-a-key-passive-entry-vulnerable-to-relay-attacks/) · [Keysight — Understanding RFID Technology and Its Security Implications](https://www.keysight.com/blogs/en/tech/nwvs/2024/03/25/understanding-rfid-technology-and-its-security-implications)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** RF presence spoofing (badge cloning, BLE relay) is a mature wireless attack, but the new bridge is that 2025-2026 building/automation agents treat presence telemetry as an authenticated identity-and-context input that drives downstream tool actions, fusing a P3 provenance failure with P2 authority. Classic physical access control at least mapped a badge to one door; an agent generalizes a spoofed presence fact into arbitrary co-located reasoning and actuation. No agent framework today carries provenance/assurance metadata on presence signals or refuses to act on unauthenticated RF as ground truth, so this wireless-to-agent identity diagonal is untooled and undetected.

**How to validate it.** Build an agent that consumes RFID/BLE/Wi-Fi presence telemetry and makes an access/approval decision on 'person X present'. Clone a static 125kHz/13.56MHz badge UID (Proxmark) or relay a BLE token, replay it to the reader feeding the agent, and confirm the agent treats the forged signal as authenticated presence and acts (unlock/approve).

**Refuted if.** The agent's presence input uses cryptographically authenticated credentials (e.g., challenge-response smartcards, BLE with mutual auth) or correlates multiple independent signals, so a cloned UID / relayed token does not yield an accepted presence fact.

**Prerequisites.** RF cloning/relay hardware (Proxmark, SDR, BLE relay); a target agent consuming raw presence telemetry as identity.

**Existing controls.** Badge cloning and BLE/relay attacks are well-demonstrated independently; secure credentials (SEOS, DESFire EV2/3, FIDO) and relay-distance bounding mitigate them. Novelty is only the agent consuming presence as ground truth; the RF spoofing itself is mature.

**Proof-of-concept — Replayed badge/BLE record fools stub presence/ground-truth check (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* No radio. Save as presence_sim.py; it reads a RECORDED/synthetic badge+BLE advertisement record from a local file (no live capture, no transmit) and feeds it to a stub presence verifier that treats a replayed identifier as live ground-truth.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 agent-rf-presence-ground-truth SIM: file-fed, RECEIVE-ONLY analysis.
# An agent uses "is the authorized person physically present?" as ground truth
# for an action. Presence is derived from a badge UID + BLE MAC. The verifier
# accepts a REPLAYED record (read from a file) as live -- no freshness/challenge.
# Nothing is transmitted; we only parse a recorded/synthetic record.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- no live RF, receive/replay-from-file only.
import json, sys

REC = "presence_record.json"

def make_recorded_capture():
    rec = {"badge_uid": "04:AA:BB:CC", "ble_mac": "DE:AD:BE:EF:00:01",
           "captured_ts": 1000, "rssi": -42}
    json.dump(rec, open(REC, "w"))

class StubPresence:
    def __init__(self): self.known = {"04:AA:BB:CC", "DE:AD:BE:EF:00:01"}
    # THE SEAM: presence == "we saw a known id", no nonce/challenge, no freshness
    def is_present(self, rec):
        return rec["badge_uid"] in self.known and rec["ble_mac"] in self.known

def main():
    make_recorded_capture()
    rec = json.load(open(REC))
    p = StubPresence()
    present = p.is_present(rec)
    stale_seconds = 9999
    print("replayed record badge=%s ble=%s stale_by~%ds -> present=%s" %
          (rec["badge_uid"], rec["ble_mac"], stale_seconds, present))
    if present:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] stub presence check accepted a REPLAYED recorded "
              "identifier as live ground-truth -- no challenge/freshness binding")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[rejected]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* Output shows the replayed badge/BLE record returning present=True despite being stale, then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the presence oracle accepted a recorded identifier with no liveness challenge.  
*Cleanup:* rm presence_sim.py presence_record.json  
*Safety:* Receive-only / replay-from-file simulation; no radio capture, no transmit, no real badge/BLE hardware -- record is synthetic and defanged.

**Research hook.** Define a presence-assurance metadata schema (signal type, anti-relay/liveness evidence, confidence) that agents must check before treating RF telemetry as identity, and prototype agent guardrails that downgrade unauthenticated presence to a non-authoritative hint; demonstrate badge-clone-driven agent door-unlock.


## 43. Agent as confused deputy in cert request/approval (mis-issuance)

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-confused-deputy-ca-issuance` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | AI Agent → Identity & Federation |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; Identity & Federation Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1649, CWE-441, CWE-295 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** A PKI/certificate-issuance pipeline (ACME endpoint, internal CA, cloud private-CA) trusts that a requester holding issuance authority is binding a name to a key only after validating that the requester legitimately controls that identity, and treats an automated agent's issuance/approval calls as authorized administrative intent.

**The attack.** An LLM platform/PKI-ops agent is prompt-injected (via a ticket, a domain string, or a service descriptor it reads) into requesting or approving certificates for names it does not legitimately control, becoming a confused deputy that mints attacker-usable certificates or installs a trust anchor with the CA's own authority, undermining the federation's root of trust.

**Mechanism (technical).** PKI issuance (ACME, internal CA, cloud private CA) binds a name to a key after domain-control validation (HTTP-01/DNS-01) or an internal authorization policy, and treats a requester holding issuance authority as having validated control. An agent with issuance/approval scope that is prompt-injected becomes a confused deputy: it requests or approves certificates for names it does not legitimately control, or installs a trust anchor, using the CA's own authority. Historic DCV-bypass misissuance (subdomain-to-base-domain, unprivileged-port validation) shows the validation boundary is exploitable even without an agent.

**Exploitation primitive.** Prompt injection via a ticket, domain string, or service descriptor the PKI-ops agent reads, causing it to issue/approve a certificate (or install a CA) for an attacker-chosen name with the pipeline's authority.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise's T1649 Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates is the nearest, but it describes stealing/forging certs, not an agent with legitimate issuance/approval scope being prompt-injected into a confused deputy that mints attacker-usable certificates or installs a trust anchor using the CA's own authority; Enterprise has no model of an LLM agent as the mis-issuing principal. The injection driver maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection), which Enterprise ATT&CK does not incorporate.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *incident:* WoSign DCV bypass misissuance / SSL.com MX-hostname DCV bypass (Mozilla Bug 1961406) — Real CA misissuance from validation flaws (subdomain-to-base-domain, MX-hostname DCV bypass) — the confused-deputy outcome an injected agent can reproduce. — <https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1961406>
- *standard:* MITRE ATT&CK T1649 Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates — Adversary obtaining certificates to impersonate identities / undermine trust. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1649/>

**Detection (technical).** Certificate Transparency log monitoring for unexpected issuance of names you own; alert on agent-initiated issuance/approval lacking an independently validated control proof; constrain agent issuance scope with CA name-constraints and require human approval for trust-anchor installation or wildcard/high-value names.

**References.** [Mozilla Bugzilla 1961406 — SSL.com DCV bypass / fake certificates for MX hostnames](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1961406) · [SSLMate — Timeline of Certificate Authority Failures](https://sslmate.com/resources/certificate_authority_failures)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Cert lifecycle automation is increasingly delegated to AI ops agents (cloud private-CA, internal ACME, service-mesh identity) in 2025-2026, and the issuance trust model assumes the requester validated domain/identity control. An agent does not perform that validation with adversarial rigor and can be steered by untrusted input into requesting or rubber-stamping certificates for arbitrary names, turning it into a CA confused deputy whose mis-issuance poisons everything that trusts the resulting certs. This is the agent-authority primitive landing on PKI/federation identity, a substrate that very recently became agent-reachable; CT logs offer only nascent after-the-fact detection and there is no preventive tooling that constrains agent-scoped issuance to validated names.

**How to validate it.** Give an LLM PKI-ops agent issuance/approval authority against a test internal CA or ACME endpoint. Feed it a ticket/domain string/service descriptor containing injected instructions to request or approve a certificate for a name the requester does not control. Observe whether the agent issues/approves the cert (or installs a trust anchor) for the attacker-chosen name without independent domain-control validation.

**Refuted if.** Issuance is still gated by independent domain-control validation (ACME challenge, CAA, RA approval) that the agent cannot satisfy for names it doesn't control, so the agent's confused request fails validation regardless of its intent.

**Prerequisites.** An agent with issuance/approval authority and an injection channel it reads; a pipeline where the agent's call is treated as authorized intent without separate domain-control proof. Chained, not common.

**Existing controls.** Mandatory domain-control validation independent of requester, RA dual-control/approval, CAA records, scoping agents away from approval authority, certificate transparency monitoring for mis-issuance.

**Proof-of-concept — Confused-deputy CA: agent relays unverified CSR to issuing stub (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: operator's own local mock CA stub; issues a FAKE non-trusted lab cert record only.

*Setup:* No network. Save as gh3_ca.py and run. CA is a stub that records issuance, no real keys.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 agent-confused-deputy-ca-issuance PoC.
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. An automation agent holds CA-issuance authority and acts
# as a deputy: it forwards a requester's CSR without verifying the requester is
# authorized for that identity (SAN). Mock CA issues a FAKE cert record.
import sys

ISSUED = []
def mock_ca_issue(san):
    ISSUED.append(san); return 'FAKE-CERT for %s (lab, not trusted)' % san

AUTHZ = {'svc-web': ['web.internal.example.test']}   # who may request what

def agent_issue(requester, requested_san):
    # BUG (the seam): the deputy uses its OWN CA privilege and does not check
    # that 'requester' is entitled to 'requested_san'. Confused deputy.
    return mock_ca_issue(requested_san)

def main():
    # low-priv requester asks for a high-value identity it is NOT entitled to
    requester, san = 'svc-web', 'admin.internal.example.test'
    entitled = san in AUTHZ.get(requester, [])
    cert = agent_issue(requester, san)
    print('REQUESTER:', requester, 'SAN:', san, 'ENTITLED:', entitled)
    print('CA RESULT:', cert)
    if not entitled and san in ISSUED:
        print('[SEAM CONFIRMED] deputy issued a cert for an identity the requester was not entitled to')
        sys.exit(0)
    print('[no confused-deputy issuance]'); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

*Success:* A cert is issued for admin.internal.example.test to requester svc-web despite entitled=False; prints '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' and exits 0. Confirms the agent exercised its CA authority without binding requester identity to the requested SAN.  
*Cleanup:* rm gh3_ca.py. Issuance list is in-memory; no key material created.  
*Safety:* CA is a stub returning a FAKE non-trusted string for an example.test SAN; no real certificate, key, or trust store is touched.

**Research hook.** Build an issuance broker that hard-binds an agent's cert requests to an out-of-band proof of name control and forbids agent-driven trust-anchor changes without human dual-control; demonstrate ticket injection driving a PKI agent to mis-issue a cert for a name it does not control.


## 44. Sensor-stream poisoning shifting AI inference toward harmful actuation

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-poisoned-sensor-into-ai-actuation-model` |
| **Primitive** | P1 Data->Control |
| **Trust frame** | Wireless / RF + OT / ICS / SCADA → AI Agent + OT / ICS / SCADA |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; OT / ICS Attacks; Wireless / RF Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (low confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T0856, T0832, CWE-1287, CWE-345 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An AI anomaly/control model that drives physical actuation trusts that the wireless-sensor telemetry feeding its inference reflects true physical conditions, so it treats the live sensor stream as a faithful representation of reality on which it can base automated actuation decisions.

**The attack.** An attacker poisons or spoofs the wireless-sensor feed (false readings, gradual drift, replayed normal data) so the AI model infers a benign or inverted state and either actuates a harmful change or suppresses a needed protective action, weaponizing the model's data-to-control coupling because the inference layer cannot distinguish authentic physics from injected telemetry.

**Mechanism (technical).** An AI anomaly/control model ingests wireless-sensor telemetry as a faithful representation of physical state and bases actuation on its inference. Because legacy ICS sensor protocols (e.g., Modbus) lack authentication and integrity, an attacker can inject false readings, slow bias drift, or replayed 'normal' data. The model then infers a benign or inverted state and either actuates a harmful change or suppresses a needed protective action — a false-data-injection attack weaponizing the model's data-to-control coupling, since the inference layer cannot distinguish authentic physics from spoofed telemetry.

**Exploitation primitive.** False-data-injection / bias-drift / replay on the unauthenticated sensor feed that moves the model's inference across an actuation threshold (or masks an alarm condition).

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK for ICS T0856 Spoof Reporting Message and T0832 Manipulation of View describe spoofing telemetry/operator view, but neither captures the AI-specific seam where false-data injection into a sensor stream shifts an ML anomaly/control model's inference so it actuates harmfully or suppresses protection, because the inference layer cannot distinguish authentic physics from spoofed input. It maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0015 (Evade ML Model) via adversarial/false sensor data and AML.T0031 (Erode ML Model Integrity), neither incorporated by Enterprise/ICS ATT&CK.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Stealthy False Command/Data Injection on Modbus-based SCADA — Modbus's lack of authentication lets an attacker inject crafted packets carrying false sensor/command data that the control loop accepts as real. — <https://www.researchgate.net/publication/369335032_A_Stealthy_False_Command_Injection_Attack_on_Modbus_based_SCADA_Systems>
- *standard:* MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0856 Spoof Reporting Message / T0832 Manipulation of View — Adversary spoofs sensor/reporting messages so operators or automation perceive a false process state. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0856/>

**Detection (technical).** Physics-based / process-invariant anomaly detection and sensor-fingerprinting (e.g., hardware noise fingerprints) to flag telemetry inconsistent with physical dynamics; cross-validate redundant sensors; alert on flat-line/replayed values, impossible rate-of-change, and divergence between sensor stream and actuator-response model.

**References.** [A Stealthy False Command Injection Attack on Modbus based SCADA Systems](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/369335032_A_Stealthy_False_Command_Injection_Attack_on_Modbus_based_SCADA_Systems) · [MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0856 Spoof Reporting Message](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0856/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Industrial sites are closing the loop in 2025-2026 by letting ML/AI anomaly models directly trigger actuation (auto-throttle, auto-shutdown, auto-dispatch) on wireless-sensor inputs, removing the human who previously sanity-checked a reading against physical plausibility. Classic sensor spoofing fooled a human or a fixed threshold; here it steers a learned policy that will act autonomously, and adversarial/poisoned inputs to such models are under-defended and the actuation amplifies the consequence. There is no integrity provenance on most wireless industrial telemetry and no detection that correlates model-driven actuation against physically-redundant evidence, making this poisoned-data-to-AI-actuation diagonal both high-consequence and untooled.

**How to validate it.** In a testbed where an AI anomaly/control model actuates based on wireless sensor telemetry, inject spoofed/replayed/drifting sensor readings (RF spoofing of the sensor link, or feeding crafted values at the ingestion layer) and observe whether the model infers a false state and actuates harmfully or suppresses a protective action. Real if benign-looking poisoned telemetry steers actuation without the inference layer flagging the input as untrustworthy.

**Refuted if.** Sensor links are authenticated/integrity-protected, the model uses physics-consistency / cross-sensor redundancy / rate-of-change bounds that reject spoofed or replayed values, or actuation requires independent confirmation, so poisoned telemetry cannot drive actuation.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to spoof/inject the wireless sensor feed (unauthenticated link or ingestion access) and an actuation model that trusts raw telemetry without redundancy/physics checks.

**Existing controls.** Sensor authentication/encryption, sensor fusion and physics-based anomaly detection, and safety interlocks independent of the ML model. Sensor-spoofing of cyber-physical systems (GPS, ADS-B, automotive sensors) is demonstrated; the AI-actuation-coupling specifically is less publicly shown.

**Proof-of-concept — Poisoned sensor reading drives stub anomaly model into a sim actuation (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* No hardware. Pure stdlib. Save as sensor_sim.py; it feeds a crafted sensor sample to a stub anomaly detector whose 'normal' verdict gates a simulated actuator. The poisoned reading is classified normal and triggers the actuation flag.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 poisoned-sensor-into-ai-actuation-model SIM: stub detector + sim actuator.
# A simple zscore anomaly model gates an actuator. An attacker injects a crafted
# sensor value that sits just inside the 'normal' band yet drives an unsafe
# actuation in the downstream control law. All in-memory; no actuator.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary, no hardware.
import statistics, sys

BASELINE = [50.0, 51.0, 49.5, 50.5, 50.2, 49.8]
mu = statistics.mean(BASELINE); sd = statistics.pstdev(BASELINE)

def is_normal(x, z=3.0):
    # THE SEAM: magnitude z-score only; no provenance / rate-of-change / cross-sensor check
    return abs(x - mu) <= z * sd

class StubActuator:
    def __init__(self): self.engaged = False
    def control(self, sensor_value):
        if is_normal(sensor_value) and sensor_value >= mu + 1.0:
            self.engaged = True
        return self.engaged

def main():
    poisoned = mu + 2.5 * sd
    act = StubActuator()
    classified_normal = is_normal(poisoned)
    engaged = act.control(poisoned)
    print("poisoned=%.3f mu=%.3f sd=%.3f classified_normal=%s actuator_engaged(sim)=%s" %
          (poisoned, mu, sd, classified_normal, engaged))
    if classified_normal and engaged:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] crafted sensor value passed the anomaly model and "
              "drove a simulated actuation -- no provenance/consistency gate")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[caught]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* Output shows the poisoned value classified_normal=True and actuator_engaged(sim)=True, then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- a crafted reading slipped past the anomaly model and triggered the simulated actuation flag.  
*Cleanup:* rm sensor_sim.py (in-memory only).  
*Safety:* Simulation only; actuator is an in-memory boolean -- no real sensor, no hardware, no network.

**Research hook.** Prototype physics-consistency cross-checks (redundant/heterogeneous sensors, conservation-law bounds) that must agree before an AI model is allowed to actuate, plus signed-telemetry transport for wireless sensors; demonstrate slow-drift poisoning that suppresses an AI auto-shutdown in a process simulator.


## 45. Agent-driven HMI setpoint/command write without operator deliberation

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-hmi-operator-impersonation` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | AI Agent → OT / ICS / SCADA |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; OT / ICS Attacks |
| **Validity** | speculative (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T0831, T0855, CWE-285, CWE-807 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An OT control system (HMI/SCADA copilot session) trusts that any setpoint change, valve command, or alarm acknowledgement arriving through the operator console carries the deliberation of a certified human operator who weighed the physical-process consequences before clicking, so it applies no independent safety judgment to a console-originated write.

**The attack.** An LLM operations copilot wired into the HMI translates a prompt-injected or hallucinated 'recommendation' directly into a control write at machine speed, and the control system actuates it as a vetted operator decision because the seam never distinguished the agent's keystrokes from a trained human's, collapsing the human-judgment safety layer the console authority was calibrated for.

**Mechanism (technical).** HMI/SCADA front-ends issue control writes (e.g., Modbus write-single-register/coil FC05/FC06/FC16, OPC-UA Write service, DNP3 operate) the moment an operator clicks; the control authority is bound to the console session, not to any cryptographic proof of human deliberation. When an LLM copilot is wired into the same console session and is permitted to emit those same writes, the PLC/RTU cannot distinguish an agent-generated setpoint change from a trained operator's because legacy OT protocols carry no per-command actor identity or intent attestation. The agent's prompt-injected or hallucinated 'recommendation' therefore actuates at machine speed with full operator authority.

**Exploitation primitive.** Indirect prompt injection of the copilot's input context (alarm text, log entry, work-order note) that the agent translates into a console-authorized setpoint/command write, bypassing the human deliberation the OT authority model assumes.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK for ICS has T0831 Manipulation of Control and T0855 Unauthorized Command Message for OT writes, but neither captures the seam where an LLM copilot wired into an HMI session emits operator-authority control writes (Modbus FC05/06/16, OPC-UA Write, DNP3 operate) that the PLC cannot distinguish from a deliberating human because legacy OT protocols carry no per-command actor identity. The agent-as-unattested-operator angle is an AI seam with no ICS or Enterprise technique; the prompt-injection driver maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051, not incorporated by ATT&CK.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *incident:* Oldsmar, FL water treatment HMI setpoint tamper (2021) — Remote actor used HMI access to raise sodium hydroxide setpoint ~100x; illustrates that a console-originated setpoint write actuates the physical process with no independent safety judgment, the exact trust the agent inherits. — <https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa21-042a>
- *standard:* MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0831 Manipulation of Control / T0855 Unauthorized Command Message — Catalogs adversary manipulation of control via command messages a controller treats as legitimate operator action. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0831/>

**Detection (technical).** Behavioral baselining of HMI-originated writes: flag setpoint/command writes whose inter-command timing, value step-size, or sequence is inconsistent with human operator pacing; correlate console session command logs with copilot tool-call audit trail and require dual-channel confirmation for safety-relevant writes.

**References.** [CISA AA21-042A — Compromise of U.S. Water Treatment Facility (Oldsmar)](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa21-042a) · [MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0831 Manipulation of Control](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0831/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Vendors are shipping LLM 'plant copilots' and natural-language HMI assistants into SCADA in 2025-2026 with read/write integration, but every OT safety model assumes the entity at the console is a slow, accountable, judgment-bearing human; the ICS access-control and alarm-management layers have zero notion of an agentic actor that can issue thousands of physically-consequential writes per minute from text input it cannot itself verify. There is no detection that separates agent-originated console writes from human ones and no tooling to rate-limit or require dual-authorization on agent-issued physical-process commands, so this is the highest-value least-tooled seam where AI authority lands on physical actuation.

**How to validate it.** In an OT testbed, wire an LLM ops copilot to an HMI/SCADA write interface (or simulator). Inject a malicious 'recommendation' via the copilot's input (prompt injection or hallucination trigger) and observe whether it issues a setpoint/valve/ack write that the control system actuates without any independent human deliberation gate distinguishing agent writes from operator writes.

**Refuted if.** The control system requires an out-of-band human confirmation/dual-control for any setpoint change, or the copilot is read-only/advisory and cannot issue writes, so the agent's recommendation cannot become a control action.

**Prerequisites.** An OT/HMI environment (or high-fidelity simulator) with a write-capable LLM integration; ability to influence copilot input.

**Existing controls.** OT safety practice mandates operator confirmation, interlocks, and often read-only advisory roles for new tooling; safety instrumented systems (SIS) are independent of HMI. Write-capable LLM-to-HMI wiring is rare today, so prerequisites are demanding.

**Proof-of-concept — Agent writes setpoint to stub PLC via HMI path with no operator-approval gate (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* No PLC, no network. Save as hmi_sim.py; it stands up a STUB Modbus-like PLC and shows an autonomous agent applying a setpoint change that should have required a human operator confirmation.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 agent-hmi-operator-impersonation SIM: STUB PLC, no real OT, no network.
# An HMI write should require an operator-confirmation step (two-person / ack).
# The agent path applies the setpoint directly because the gate is advisory only.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary register, no actuation.
import sys

class StubPLC:
    def __init__(self):
        self.regs = {40001: 50}
        self.operator_ack = False
    def write_register(self, addr, value, source):
        # THE SEAM: write accepted from any 'source' without operator_ack for agents.
        self.regs[addr] = value
        return {"applied": True, "addr": addr, "value": value, "source": source}

def agent_change_setpoint(plc, addr, value):
    return plc.write_register(addr, value, source="agent-session")

def main():
    plc = StubPLC()
    before = plc.regs[40001]
    res = agent_change_setpoint(plc, 40001, 95)
    after = plc.regs[40001]
    print("setpoint before=%d after=%d operator_ack=%s source=%s" %
          (before, after, plc.operator_ack, res["source"]))
    if res["applied"] and not plc.operator_ack and before != after:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] agent setpoint write applied with operator_ack=False "
              "-- no human-approval gate on the HMI->PLC path")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[gated]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* Output shows the register changing (e.g. 50->95) with operator_ack=False and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the stub PLC applied an agent-originated setpoint with no operator confirmation.  
*Cleanup:* rm hmi_sim.py (in-memory only).  
*Safety:* Simulation only; STUB PLC holds an in-memory integer -- no real controller, no actuator, no network.

**Research hook.** Build an HMI shim that tags console writes with actor-provenance (human vs agent session) and prototype a dual-authorization gate that forces a separate human confirmation for any agent-originated write crossing a process-safety threshold; demonstrate prompt-injection of a vendor plant-copilot into an unauthorized setpoint change on a simulated SIS-protected loop.


## 46. Approval-context inheritance across NL-to-fieldbus translation

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-protocol-bridge-approval-loss` |
| **Primitive** | P4 Context-Inheritance |
| **Trust frame** | AI Agent → OT / ICS / SCADA |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; OT / ICS Attacks |
| **Validity** | speculative (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T0855, T0836, CWE-441, CWE-863 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An OT protocol-translation layer (an agent that converts natural-language intent into Modbus/OPC-UA/DNP3 function codes) is trusted to faithfully carry the human approval context that was attached to the original high-level request all the way down to the wire-level write, so the field device treats the emitted function code as an already-approved engineering action.

**The attack.** The agent decomposes one approved high-level goal into many low-level protocol writes, but the human approval gate was bound to the natural-language intent and never re-checked at the function-code boundary, so injected or over-broad sub-steps reach the controller carrying inherited approval they were never granted, turning a single 'okay' into unbounded direct process control.

**Mechanism (technical).** An NL-to-fieldbus agent decomposes one approved high-level intent into many wire-level writes (Modbus FC05/06/16, OPC-UA Write/Method-Call, DNP3 operate). The human approval gate binds to the natural-language request, but the field protocols themselves carry no authorization token, signature, or scope on individual function codes — most are insecure-by-design with plaintext, unauthenticated engineering operations. So sub-steps that were injected or over-broadly expanded reach the controller carrying 'inherited' approval the operator never granted to those specific writes.

**Exploitation primitive.** Goal-decomposition expansion or injected sub-step that emits function codes outside the scope the human actually approved, exploiting that the approval was never re-checked at the function-code boundary.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK for ICS T0855 Unauthorized Command Message and T0836 Modify Parameter describe the resulting unauthorized writes, but neither captures approval-context loss across a natural-language-to-fieldbus translation, where one human 'okay' on a high-level intent is inherited by many injected or over-broad function-code writes that the protocol cannot scope or attest. This NL-to-protocol decomposition seam is AI-specific, with the injection driver mapping to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection), which ATT&CK does not incorporate.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Forescout OT:ICEFALL (2022) — 56 insecure-by-design flaws across 10 OT vendors; documents that engineering protocols (Modbus, OPC-UA variants, proprietary) accept controller/firmware operations with broken or absent authentication, so a wire-level write needs no per-command approval. — <https://www.forescout.com/research-labs/ot-icefall/>
- *standard:* MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0855 Unauthorized Command Message / T0836 Modify Parameter — Adversary sends command/parameter-modify messages a controller accepts as authorized engineering action. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0836/>

**Detection (technical).** Per-function-code authorization mapping at the bridge: enforce an allow-list of {approved intent -> permitted function codes / register ranges} and alert on any emitted write outside the bound scope of the approving request; log the NL-intent-to-functioncode expansion for review.

**References.** [Forescout — OT:ICEFALL: 56 Vulnerabilities Caused by Insecure-by-Design Practices in OT](https://www.forescout.com/blog/ot-icefall-56-vulnerabilities-caused-by-insecure-by-design-practices-in-ot/) · [MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0836 Modify Parameter](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0836/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** MCP-style tool servers that expose OT protocols ('write_register', 'set_coil') to agents are emerging in 2025-2026 industrial-AI pilots, and they bind approval to the human's chat-level request rather than to each generated protocol primitive. Because the translation step is exactly where the human-readable intent stops and the machine-readable command begins, it is the natural place for the approval semantics to evaporate, yet no existing OT IDS or agent guardrail re-validates that each emitted function code is covered by the scope the human actually approved. This diagonal (agent context-inheritance landing on insecure-by-design fieldbus) has no detection and no tooling.

**How to validate it.** Build an NL-to-fieldbus agent that takes one approved high-level intent and emits multiple Modbus/OPC-UA writes. Approve a benign high-level goal, then via injected context cause the decomposition to include extra/over-broad writes. Observe whether those un-approved function-code writes are emitted to a simulated PLC (e.g. ModbusPal / OpenPLC) without a per-write approval check. Seam confirmed if injected sub-steps reach the controller carrying inherited approval.

**Refuted if.** The translation layer enforces approval at the function-code boundary (per-write allowlist, signed command envelope, human re-confirmation of the concrete register writes), so injected sub-steps are blocked.

**Prerequisites.** An agentic NL-to-OT bridge that binds approval to NL intent rather than wire commands; ability to inject into the agent's context. Such bridges are largely experimental today.

**Existing controls.** Per-command authorization at the protocol layer, OT command allowlisting, engineering-workstation change control, unidirectional gateways. Approval-at-the-wire is the standard correct design; NL-to-OT agents are nascent.

**Proof-of-concept — Approval flag dropped crossing an IT->OT protocol bridge (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* No network. Save as bridge_sim.py; it models an agent command crossing a stub protocol bridge (JSON/REST -> Modbus-like) where the human-approval metadata is lost in translation and the downstream controller applies the command anyway.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 agent-protocol-bridge-approval-loss SIM: STUB bridge + STUB controller.
# Upstream command carries requires_approval=True / approved=False. The bridge
# translates the payload to the OT protocol but DROPS the approval metadata,
# so the controller sees a plain command and executes it. No network, no OT.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary.
import sys

def bridge_translate(upstream_msg):
    # THE SEAM: only actionable fields survive; the approval envelope is dropped.
    return {"function": "write_coil", "addr": upstream_msg["addr"],
            "value": upstream_msg["value"]}

class StubController:
    def __init__(self): self.coils = {1: 0}
    def apply(self, ot_frame):
        self.coils[ot_frame["addr"]] = ot_frame["value"]
        return True

def main():
    upstream = {"addr": 1, "value": 1, "requires_approval": True,
                "approved": False, "source": "agent"}
    ot_frame = bridge_translate(upstream)
    approval_carried = ("approved" in ot_frame) or ("requires_approval" in ot_frame)
    ctrl = StubController()
    applied = ctrl.apply(ot_frame)
    print("upstream approved=%s | approval metadata carried across bridge=%s | "
          "controller applied=%s coil=%d" %
          (upstream["approved"], approval_carried, applied, ctrl.coils[1]))
    if applied and not approval_carried and not upstream["approved"]:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] unapproved agent command executed downstream because "
              "the bridge dropped the approval envelope")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[approval preserved]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* Output shows upstream approved=False, approval metadata carried=False, controller applied=True, then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the protocol bridge stripped the approval gate.  
*Cleanup:* rm bridge_sim.py (in-memory only).  
*Safety:* Simulation only; stub bridge/controller operate on an in-memory coil value -- no real protocol stack, no OT, no network.

**Research hook.** Design an approval-binding protocol that cryptographically scopes a human authorization to a bounded set of resulting fieldbus writes (target addresses, value ranges, count) and rejects any agent-emitted function code outside that envelope; red-team an OPC-UA MCP server by injecting extra writes into an approved batch.


## 47. Agent-narrated firmware verification standing in for real signature check

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-ota-self-verified-firmware` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | AI Agent → Hardware / IoT / Firmware |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; Hardware / IoT / Firmware |
| **Validity** | speculative (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1542.001, CWE-494, CWE-345 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An AI fleet-operations agent that selects and pushes firmware/OTA images is trusted to have established the image's provenance — its own narrated 'I verified the signature and changelog' is accepted as the provenance check — so the IoT/edge fleet treats an agent-initiated update as authorized and authentic.

**The attack.** The agent rationalizes an unsigned, swapped, or downgraded image as legitimate (it 'read' an attacker-supplied changelog or release page and concluded the build was fine) and pushes it to the fleet, and the devices accept it because the human-in-the-loop assumed the agent performed real cryptographic verification that it never actually enforced, persisting attacker firmware at fleet scale.

**Mechanism (technical).** Secure firmware update relies on the device cryptographically verifying an image signature and enforcing monotonic version/rollback counters before flashing. When an AI fleet-ops agent selects and pushes images, the human-in-the-loop substitutes the agent's narrated 'I verified the signature/changelog' for an actual signature check. If the device or pipeline does not independently enforce signature + anti-rollback (a common insecure-by-design gap), the agent can push an unsigned, swapped, or downgraded image — and a still-validly-signed older vulnerable image passes verification, enabling firmware-level persistence at fleet scale.

**Exploitation primitive.** Agent reads an attacker-supplied changelog/release page, rationalizes a swapped or downgraded image as legitimate, and pushes it to the fleet, standing in for a cryptographic verification that was never enforced.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise's T1542.001 System Firmware covers implanting firmware, but has no technique for the seam where a human substitutes an AI fleet-ops agent's narrated 'I verified the signature/changelog' for an actual cryptographic check, letting the agent push an unsigned, swapped, or downgraded image that devices accept; Enterprise has no concept of an agent's narration standing in for a real verification gate. The agent-rationalization-of-provenance angle is AI-specific with the manipulation driver mapping to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051, not incorporated by ATT&CK.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Forescout OT:ICEFALL (2022) — insecure firmware update category — 21% of the 56 flaws were firmware-manipulation issues: missing or broken signature verification in OT firmware update mechanisms. — <https://www.forescout.com/research-labs/ot-icefall/>
- *cve:* CVE-2023-24932 (Secure Boot downgrade, BlackLotus) — Downgrade attack loads an older still-Microsoft-signed boot manager that Secure Boot trusts, illustrating that a validly-signed older image defeats provenance assumptions absent anti-rollback. — <https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2023-24932>

**Detection (technical).** Enforce device-side signature verification and monotonic rollback counters independent of the agent; require code-signing of the deployed artifact hash and compare against an attested SBOM; alert on version regressions and any image whose signer/hash is not on the trusted release allow-list; treat agent 'verification' narration as non-authoritative.

**References.** [Forescout — OT:ICEFALL research labs page](https://www.forescout.com/research-labs/ot-icefall/) · [NVD — CVE-2023-24932 (Secure Boot security feature bypass)](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2023-24932)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** AI ops agents are starting to drive OTA rollouts for IoT/edge fleets in 2025-2026, and the dangerous novelty is that an LLM's natural-language assertion of 'verified' is being treated by operators as equivalent to a cryptographic provenance check, when the agent has no reliable way to bind a build to a trusted signer and is itself steerable by the very release notes / repo pages it reads. This converts the classic firmware-provenance gap into an AI-mediated confused-deputy: provenance is asserted by a manipulable narrator rather than enforced by a verifier. No tooling forces agents to surface and gate on actual signature-verification evidence, and no fleet detection flags agent-initiated unsigned/downgrade pushes.

**How to validate it.** Configure a fleet-ops agent with authority to push OTA images, where the workflow relies on the agent's narrated 'I verified the signature' rather than an enforced cryptographic gate. Supply an unsigned/swapped/downgraded image plus an attacker-authored changelog/release page. Observe whether the agent rationalizes it as legitimate and pushes it, and whether devices accept it. Seam confirmed if the image is deployed without a real signature check enforced by the device or pipeline.

**Refuted if.** Devices enforce secure boot / signature verification independently of the agent's narration, so an unsigned/swapped image is rejected at the device regardless of what the agent 'concluded'; or the pipeline gates on a verified signature, not agent text.

**Prerequisites.** A deployment that delegates provenance to agent narration and a fleet that does not independently enforce signatures (or accepts downgrades). Well-run fleets enforce secure boot.

**Existing controls.** Device-side secure boot, signed firmware with anti-rollback, TUF/Uptane update frameworks, code-signing enforcement in the CD pipeline. These neutralize it when present; the seam exists only where verification is delegated to the agent.

**Proof-of-concept — OTA agent narrates 'signature verified' while skipping the actual check (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* No device. Save as ota_sim.py; it creates a sample firmware blob with a bogus signature, runs a stub OTA agent that LOGS 'verified' while the real signature verification is short-circuited, and shows the tampered blob accepted.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 agent-ota-self-verified-firmware SIM: STUB OTA, local blob, no device.
# The agent's self-reported status says "signature verified" but the actual
# verify() is skipped/short-circuited, so a tampered blob with a bad signature
# is "installed" (a flag flip). No real flashing, no device, no network.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary blob.
import hashlib, hmac, sys

VENDOR_KEY = b"vendor-signing-key"

def real_verify(blob, sig):
    expect = hmac.new(VENDOR_KEY, blob, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    return hmac.compare_digest(expect, sig)

class StubOTAAgent:
    def __init__(self): self.installed = None
    def update(self, blob, sig):
        # THE SEAM: narrates success but does NOT gate install on real_verify().
        verified_claim = True
        actually_valid = real_verify(blob, sig)
        print("AGENT: signature verified =", verified_claim,
              "(actual verify result:", actually_valid, ")")
        self.installed = blob
        return verified_claim, actually_valid

def main():
    tampered = b"FW v1.0 benign canary + TAMPER"
    bogus_sig = "00" * 32
    agent = StubOTAAgent()
    claim, valid = agent.update(tampered, bogus_sig)
    print("installed tampered blob:", agent.installed == tampered)
    if claim and not valid and agent.installed == tampered:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] agent reported 'verified' and installed a tampered "
              "blob whose signature is INVALID -- self-attested verification is hollow")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[blocked]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* Output shows the agent claiming verified=True while the actual verify result is False, the tampered blob installed, then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the OTA agent's narrated verification is decoupled from the real signature check.  
*Cleanup:* rm ota_sim.py (no persistent state).  
*Safety:* Simulation only; 'firmware' is a byte string and 'install' is a variable assignment -- no device, no flashing, no network.

**Research hook.** Require agents to emit machine-checkable verification artifacts (signer identity, signature result, anti-rollback version proof) that a separate non-LLM gate validates before any OTA push proceeds; demonstrate a poisoned release page convincing an ops agent to roll out an attacker image fleet-wide.


## 48. Agent-orchestrated mass door-unlock / access-control override

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-building-access-orchestration` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | AI Agent → OT / ICS / SCADA + Hardware / IoT / Firmware |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; OT / ICS Attacks; Hardware / IoT / Firmware |
| **Validity** | speculative (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T0831, CWE-285, CWE-807 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A physical-security / building-management system (access control, door controllers, elevators, HVAC, surveillance) trusts that commands from its facilities admin integration are issued by an accountable human security operator acting on a verified situational need, sizing its authority and audit model to human-paced security decisions.

**The attack.** An LLM facilities/security agent holding the BMS/access-control integration scope is steered by injected text (an automated 'incident' description, a calendar entry, a sensor caption) into unlocking doors, overriding HVAC/life-safety, or disabling cameras across a site, and the building system executes it as authentic operator intent because it cannot tell agent orchestration from a human security officer's deliberate action.

**Mechanism (technical).** Building-management and physical access-control systems (door controllers, elevators, HVAC, surveillance, life-safety) execute commands from a facilities-admin integration as accountable human security-operator decisions, sized to human-paced security decisions with audit but no blast-radius limit. An LLM facilities agent holding the BMS/access integration scope issues the same API/command-bus calls, so injected text (an automated 'incident' description, a calendar entry, a sensor caption) can drive mass door-unlock, life-safety override, or camera disablement that the building system applies as authentic operator intent.

**Exploitation primitive.** Injected incident/calendar/caption text steers the facilities agent into a site-wide unlock / camera-disable / HVAC-override sequence via its BMS integration scope.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK for ICS T0831 Manipulation of Control is the nearest, but it has no technique for an LLM facilities agent holding a BMS/access-control integration being steered by injected text into mass door-unlock, life-safety override, or camera disablement that the building system applies as authentic human-operator intent with no blast-radius limit. The agent-authority and injection seam maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051 (LLM Prompt Injection), which Enterprise/ICS ATT&CK does not incorporate.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Forescout OT:ICEFALL (2022) — Building-automation and OT controllers among the insecure-by-design devices that accept native engineering/control operations without authentication, so a facilities integration command actuates with no independent check. — <https://www.forescout.com/research-labs/ot-icefall/>
- *standard:* MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0831 Manipulation of Control — Manipulation of control over physical actuation (doors, HVAC, life-safety) via command messages treated as legitimate. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0831/>

**Detection (technical).** Alert on bulk/site-wide access-control state changes (mass unlock, simultaneous camera disable, life-safety override) inconsistent with operator pacing; require human dual-control for life-safety and mass-unlock actions; correlate agent tool-calls with an authenticated incident of record before executing safety-relevant overrides.

**References.** [Forescout — OT:ICEFALL research labs page](https://www.forescout.com/research-labs/ot-icefall/) · [MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0831 Manipulation of Control](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0831/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Smart-building operators are wiring AI agents into building-management and access-control platforms in 2025-2026 to automate facilities response, but these BMS/PACS authority models assume a human security operator and never anticipated an agent that can fan a single injected 'incident' into site-wide physical-security overrides at machine speed. This is the agent-authority-at-machine-speed primitive landing on cyber-physical building OT/hardware, a substrate only just becoming agent-driven, where an attacker who injects the agent's task stream can open every door or blind every camera. No PACS/BMS today distinguishes agent calls from operator calls or rate-limits agent-driven physical overrides, and no detection covers this seam.

**How to validate it.** In a BMS/access-control testbed, grant an LLM facilities agent the integration scope to unlock doors / override HVAC / disable cameras. Feed it an injected 'incident' description (calendar entry, sensor caption, ticket) and observe whether it issues mass override commands that the building system executes as operator intent, with no rate/scope/dual-control gate.

**Refuted if.** The BMS enforces per-action authorization, mass-action rate limits, or human dual-control on life-safety overrides, so the agent cannot drive a site-wide unlock from injected text.

**Prerequisites.** An LLM agent holding write scope to a BMS/access-control system; ability to inject text into its decision input.

**Existing controls.** Physical security systems typically scope automation narrowly and require operator confirmation for overrides; life-safety functions are often hard-wired. Broad agent-held BMS write scope is uncommon, making prerequisites strong. Closely related to GH3-agent-hmi.

**Proof-of-concept — Agent unlocks stub access-control door with no dual-auth / approval gate (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real vehicle/plant/medical/satellite/open-air RF.

*Setup:* No building system. Save as building_sim.py; it stands up a STUB physical-access controller and shows an orchestration agent issuing an 'unlock' that should require dual authorization but is accepted on the agent's say-so. 'Unlock' is a status flag.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH3 agent-building-access-orchestration SIM: STUB access controller, no PACS.
# An agent orchestrates building access. A privileged unlock (e.g. server room)
# should require dual-auth / approved ticket. The controller accepts the agent
# command directly. 'Unlock' flips an in-memory flag -- no door, no relay, no net.
# AUTHORIZED BENCH/SIM ONLY -- benign canary.
import sys

class StubPACS:
    def __init__(self):
        self.doors = {"server-room": {"locked": True}}
        self.dual_auth_required = True
    def unlock(self, door, approved_ticket, source):
        # THE SEAM: dual_auth_required never enforced for source=='agent'.
        self.doors[door]["locked"] = False
        return {"door": door, "locked": self.doors[door]["locked"],
                "approved_ticket": approved_ticket, "source": source}

def main():
    pacs = StubPACS()
    before = pacs.doors["server-room"]["locked"]
    res = pacs.unlock("server-room", approved_ticket=None, source="agent")
    after = pacs.doors["server-room"]["locked"]
    print("door locked before=%s after=%s dual_auth_required=%s approved_ticket=%s source=%s" %
          (before, after, pacs.dual_auth_required, res["approved_ticket"], res["source"]))
    if before and not after and pacs.dual_auth_required and res["approved_ticket"] is None:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] agent unlocked a dual-auth door with no approved "
              "ticket -- approval policy not enforced on the agent path")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[gated]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* Output shows the door going locked=True -> False with dual_auth_required=True and approved_ticket=None, then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' exit 0 -- the stub PACS honored an agent unlock without the required dual authorization.  
*Cleanup:* rm building_sim.py (in-memory only).  
*Safety:* Simulation only; 'unlock' flips an in-memory boolean -- no real access-control panel, no door relay, no network.

**Research hook.** Build a facilities-agent broker that requires human step-up for site-wide or life-safety physical actions and logs agent-vs-human provenance per command; demonstrate a poisoned automated-incident feed driving a facilities agent to mass-unlock and disable cameras in a test building.


## 49. Agent-driven rogue device commissioning into Matter/Zigbee fabric

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH3-agent-zigbee-matter-fleet-pairing` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | AI Agent → Wireless / RF + Hardware / IoT / Firmware |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; Wireless / RF Attacks; Hardware / IoT / Firmware |
| **Validity** | speculative (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A Zigbee/Matter/Thread smart-device fabric trusts that any pairing, commissioning, or group-binding command from the home/site controller reflects a deliberate human commissioning decision, so it admits new devices and rebinds control groups on the controller's say-so without re-validating who really initiated the join.

**The attack.** An LLM home/site-automation agent with controller authority is steered by injected text or a malicious device-advertised name into commissioning an attacker's device into the fabric or rebinding control groups to it, and the fabric grants the rogue node trusted membership because the commissioning trust was delegated to an agent that cannot verify the physical legitimacy of a join request.

**Mechanism (technical).** Zigbee/Matter/Thread fabrics admit new nodes and rebind control groups on the controller's commissioning command. In Zigbee, a device announcing itself as coordinator or spoofing the join/association handshake can insert itself or disrupt the mesh; in Matter, commissioning relies on device attestation but the fabric still trusts the commissioner's decision to admit a node. When an LLM home/site agent holds controller authority, injected text or a malicious device-advertised name can steer it to commission an attacker's device or rebind groups to it, granting the rogue node trusted fabric membership.

**Exploitation primitive.** Injected text or a crafted device-advertised name drives the controller-authority agent to commission an attacker device into the fabric or rebind a control group to it.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise has no technique for an LLM home/site agent with controller authority being steered (by injected text or a malicious device-advertised name) into commissioning a rogue device into a Zigbee/Matter/Thread fabric or rebinding control groups, granting an attacker node trusted membership; Enterprise has no IoT-fabric-commissioning model. The injection driver maps to MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051.001 (Indirect Prompt Injection), and the device commissioning itself has no ATT&CK or ATLAS technique.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Genge et al., Breaking Matter: Vulnerabilities in the Matter Protocol (Black Hat EU 2024) — Analyzes weaknesses in the Matter commissioning/attestation flow and fabric security model. — <https://i.blackhat.com/EU-24/Presentations/EU-24-Genge-BreakingMatterVulnerabiltiesInTheMatterProtocol-wp.pdf>
- *research:* Zigbee join/association handshake spoofing and coordinator impersonation — A rogue device announcing itself as coordinator or spoofing the join handshake can insert into or disrupt the Zigbee mesh. — <https://medium.com/csg-govtech/a-quick-look-into-zigbee-security-is-anyone-in-bee-tween-567f36c401f6>

**Detection (technical).** Alert on commissioning/group-rebind events not tied to a deliberate human commissioning session; enforce device-attestation certificate (DAC) validation in Matter and reject uncertified/unknown-vendor joins; baseline fabric membership and flag new nodes, channel changes, or coordinator-role claims.

**References.** [Breaking Matter: Vulnerabilities in the Matter Protocol (Black Hat EU 2024)](https://i.blackhat.com/EU-24/Presentations/EU-24-Genge-BreakingMatterVulnerabiltiesInTheMatterProtocol-wp.pdf) · [A quick look into ZigBee security — CSG @ GovTech](https://medium.com/csg-govtech/a-quick-look-into-zigbee-security-is-anyone-in-bee-tween-567f36c401f6)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Smart-home/IoT platforms are exposing commissioning and binding controls to AI assistants in 2025-2026 so an agent can 'add the new sensor' or 'group these lights', but the Matter/Zigbee commissioning trust model assumed a human physically initiating a pairing. An agent inheriting controller authority can be tricked by attacker-supplied device names or injected instructions into admitting a rogue node or rebinding control groups, granting an attacker persistent trusted membership in the local fabric. No platform distinguishes agent-initiated commissioning from human, no anti-injection guard exists on device-advertised strings the agent reads, and no detection flags agent-driven binding changes, leaving this agent-authority-to-wireless-fabric diagonal untooled.

**How to validate it.** Set up a Matter/Zigbee controller driven by an LLM home-automation agent with commissioning authority.
