**The trust assumption.** An audit / accountability layer trusts that the actor field on a logged action faithfully attributes that action to the principal who authorized it, so when an agent performs a tool call or API write the resulting log entry is treated as non-repudiable evidence of who decided to do it, even though the entry is just an assertion with no signed binding among the human authorizer, the agent identity, and the specific action.

**The attack.** Because no cryptographic provenance binds the authorizing human, the acting agent, and the exact action together, an action taken by a compromised or prompt-injected agent (or replayed/forged after the fact) is logged with a plausible actor and is indistinguishable from a legitimately-authorized one, so the accountability layer attributes it to an innocent principal and the true cause is unrecoverable and deniable.

**Mechanism (technical).** When an AI agent performs an action, the 'actor' is typically just an asserted field in a log or API call - there is no cryptographic binding among the human authorizer, the agent's identity, and the specific action taken. Reasoning and execution paths are often parallel and opaque, so it is unclear which agent produced an instruction or why. As a result, an action taken by a compromised or prompt-injected agent is indistinguishable from a legitimately authorized one, and the action is repudiable: no one can prove who actually authorized it.

**Exploitation primitive.** Cause an agent (via prompt injection, goal manipulation, or compromise) to take a privileged action that is logged as the legitimate authorizer, leaving no cryptographic evidence to distinguish malicious from authorized intent and enabling plausible deniability.

**MITRE gap.** Closest IDs are T1070 (Indicator Removal) and T1565.001 (Stored Data Manipulation). These model tampering with or deleting logs that exist; the gap here is the absence of an authorizer->agent->action cryptographic binding in the first place. ATT&CK assumes attribution data exists and frames repudiation as evidence destruction, whereas the agentic problem is that trustworthy provenance was never generated - a design-level non-repudiation void that no ATT&CK technique represents.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *standard:* OWASP Agentic AI - Threats and Mitigations (Agentic Security Initiative), threat T8 Repudiation & Untraceability — Defines repudiation as an attacker exploiting weak attribution/incomplete logging so it is unclear which agent produced an instruction or why a decision was made; complex parallel reasoning paths make accountability hard. — <https://genai.owasp.org/resource/agentic-ai-threats-and-mitigations/>
- *product:* MITRE ATT&CK T1070 Indicator Removal — Models log deletion/tampering, not absence of provenance binding. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1070/>
- *product:* MITRE ATT&CK T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation — Models altering stored data; does not address generating trustworthy authorizer-agent-action attribution. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1565/001/>

**Detection (technical).** Require signed, tamper-evident action provenance: cryptographically bind the human authorization grant, the agent identity (workload identity/attestation), and the action parameters (e.g., signed audit records or verifiable credentials), and reject or quarantine agent actions lacking a valid authorizer-bound signature. Monitor for actions whose logged authorizer could not have plausibly issued the underlying intent.

**References.** [OWASP Agentic AI - Threats and Mitigations (GenAI Security Project)](https://genai.owasp.org/resource/agentic-ai-threats-and-mitigations/) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1070 Indicator Removal](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1070/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** As agents perform consequential writes across cloud, identity, and SaaS in 2025-2027, organizations rely on audit logs for accountability, but those logs record an unsigned actor assertion rather than a verifiable chain linking the human who approved a goal, the agent that executed it, and the precise action emitted. There is no provenance primitive that makes an agent action non-repudiable, so a compromised or injected agent's actions are forensically indistinguishable from authorized ones, blame lands on the wrong principal, and incident response cannot reconstruct true causation. This provenance seam is distinct from sub-agent context inheritance (which concerns authority flow) because it concerns after-the-fact attribution integrity; no agent framework signs an authorizer->agent->action triple and no detection flags missing or forged provenance.

**How to validate it.** Instrument an agent that performs tool calls / API writes and inspect the resulting audit log entries. Attempt to (a) perform an action via a prompt-injected path and (b) forge/replay a log entry's actor field. Confirm there is no cryptographic binding among the human authorizer, agent identity, and the specific action — i.e., the log is an unsigned assertion attributable to an innocent principal.

**Refuted if.** The action pipeline produces a signed, tamper-evident provenance binding (e.g., signed action tokens, workload identity + request signing, transparency log) that ties authorizer-agent-action together and would distinguish a forged/replayed action.

**Prerequisites.** Access to an agent's action/audit pipeline; ability to inspect log format and attempt injected/forged actions.

**Existing controls.** Most current agent stacks log unsigned actor strings; cryptographic action provenance (signed audit, SPIFFE workload identity, request signing) exists but is rarely applied end-to-end for agent actions. The gap (absence of binding) is real and largely unaddressed today.

**Proof-of-concept — Agent action log lacks cryptographic authorizer->agent->action binding (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: operator's own local logging stub; actions are mock.

*Setup:* No network, stdlib only. Save as gh4_nonrepud.py and run.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH4 agent-action-nonrepudiation-gap PoC.
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Shows the action log records who/what but provides no
# cryptographic chain binding authorizer -> agent -> action; the entry is
# trivially forgeable/repudiable.
import json, hmac, hashlib, sys

LOG = []
def weak_log(authorizer, agent, action):
    # BUG (the seam): plain JSON record, no signature over (authorizer,agent,action),
    # no chaining. Any party can deny or alter it.
    LOG.append({'authorizer': authorizer, 'agent': agent, 'action': action})

def strong_log_would_be(secret, authorizer, agent, action, prev_hash):
    body = '%s|%s|%s|%s' % (authorizer, agent, action, prev_hash)
    sig = hmac.new(secret, body.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    return {'body': body, 'sig': sig}

def main():
    weak_log('user:alice', 'agent:billing-bot', 'refund:FAKE-ORDER-1 $500')
    entry = LOG[-1]
    # forge: rewrite the entry; nothing detects it because there is no signature
    forged = dict(entry); forged['authorizer'] = 'user:bob'
    has_sig = 'sig' in entry
    print('LOG ENTRY:', json.dumps(entry))
    print('FORGED (undetectable):', json.dumps(forged))
    print('entry has cryptographic binding:', has_sig)
    if not has_sig:
        print('[SEAM CONFIRMED] action log lacks crypto binding; authorizer attribution is forgeable/repudiable')
        sys.exit(0)
    print('[binding present]'); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

*Success:* The log entry has no 'sig' field and the forged authorizer (bob) is indistinguishable from the original; prints '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' and exits 0. Confirms there is no cryptographic chain binding authorizer to agent to action.  
*Cleanup:* rm gh4_nonrepud.py. Log is in-memory.  
*Safety:* Action is a mock refund of a FAKE order; nothing executed; no network. Includes the corrective HMAC pattern for reference only.

**Research hook.** Define a signed action-provenance token that cryptographically binds the human authorization, the agent identity, the tool/parameters, and a nonce, emitted with every consequential action and verifiable by the audit layer; prototype a broker that rejects unprovenanced agent writes and demonstrate forensic attribution under a simulated agent compromise.


## 59. Data-broker record seeding to bias an enrichment-API verdict

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH4-enrichment-api-identity-poisoning` |
| **Primitive** | P1 Data->Control |
| **Trust frame** | Web / Fetch + Supply Chain / Build → Identity & Federation + SOC / Defense + AI Agent |
| **Category** | Identity & Federation Attacks; AI / Agent Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | CWE-345, CWE-807 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An identity-decisioning or risk pipeline trusts that third-party data-broker and enrichment-API responses (firmographic lookups, email-reputation, device/IP intelligence, sanction/PEP screening, fraud-score augmentation) are authoritative external facts about a subject, so it feeds them directly into automated approve/deny/escalate decisions without treating the enrichment provider as an attacker-influenceable input.

**The attack.** An attacker poisons the upstream enrichment source (seeds false broker records, manipulates a reputation/scoring feed, or registers misleading device/IP attributes) so the enrichment API returns attacker-favorable 'facts,' and the identity pipeline ingests them as authoritative truth and makes the wrong trust decision, converting a data-feed manipulation into an identity-authority outcome the pipeline cannot trace back to the poisoned source.

**Mechanism (technical).** Automated trust/identity pipelines treat third-party enrichment APIs (firmographic data, email/device/IP reputation, sanctions/PEP screening, fraud scores) as authoritative external fact. An attacker seeds or manipulates the upstream data broker or reputation feed - registering misleading records, aging a fraudulent identity, or polluting a reputation dataset - so the enrichment verdict returned to the consuming pipeline is biased in the attacker's favor. Because the decisioning system trusts the feed implicitly and has no provenance check, the poisoned attribute silently flips an automated allow/deny or risk score.

**Exploitation primitive.** Inject or season records into a third-party enrichment/reputation source so that a later automated lookup returns an attacker-favorable verdict (e.g., clean fraud score, benign IP reputation, false firmographic legitimacy), biasing the downstream trust/identity decision.

**MITRE gap.** Closest ID is T1565.001 (Stored Data Manipulation), with T1583/T1584 (Acquire/Compromise Infrastructure) as tangential. T1565.001 covers manipulating data the adversary can reach in the victim environment; it does not model poisoning a trusted external third-party data feed whose output is then consumed as authoritative ground truth by an automated decisioning system. ATT&CK has no construct for third-party trust-feed poisoning or for biasing an automated identity/trust verdict via upstream data, and the relevant supply-chain-of-data abuse lives in MITRE ATLAS (AML.T0010), not enterprise ATT&CK.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *incident:* People Data Labs (PDL) data-enrichment exposure, 2019 — Demonstrates that enrichment/data-broker datasets are large, aggregated, and consumed as authoritative - an exposed PDL-sourced server held ~1.2B records / 622M unique emails; illustrates the trusted-enrichment-feed attack surface (here exposure rather than poisoning). — <https://haveibeenpwned.com/Breach/PDL>
- *research:* MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 AI Supply Chain Compromise — Models poisoning of third-party datasets/feeds consumed by AI/ML systems - the closest framework construct for poisoning a trusted external data source, and it is in ATLAS rather than enterprise ATT&CK. — <https://atlas.mitre.org/techniques/AML.T0010>
- *product:* MITRE ATT&CK T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation — Closest enterprise technique; assumes in-environment data, not a trusted external enrichment feed. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1565/001/>

**Detection (technical).** Treat enrichment/reputation verdicts as untrusted inputs: cross-validate critical attributes against independent sources, track provenance and confidence of each enrichment field, and alert on anomalous score/verdict shifts for newly-created or low-history entities. Flag fresh records that arrive with implausibly clean reputations and require multi-source corroboration before an automated allow decision.

**References.** [Have I Been Pwned - Data Enrichment Exposure From PDL](https://haveibeenpwned.com/Breach/PDL) · [MITRE ATLAS - AI Supply Chain Compromise (AML.T0010)](https://atlas.mitre.org/techniques/AML.T0010) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1565/001/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Identity and fraud pipelines in 2025-2027 increasingly outsource ground truth to data-broker and enrichment APIs and let agents auto-consume those verdicts, but the enrichment supply chain is loosely governed and partly attacker-influenceable (seeded broker entries, manipulable reputation feeds, declared device attributes), so a manipulated upstream fact silently steers a downstream authority decision. The seam is the pipeline's treatment of enrichment output as authoritative rather than as untrusted attacker-reachable data; this is a data-to-control violation on the identity plane distinct from synthetic-identity aging because here the attacker targets the institution's external truth source rather than building a persona. No pipeline carries provenance/confidence on enrichment facts, cross-validates conflicting providers, or detects feed poisoning, leaving this seam untooled and undetected.

**How to validate it.** In an identity/risk pipeline that ingests a data-broker or reputation/IP-intel feed, seed attacker-favorable records upstream (create false firmographic/broker entries, age an IP/domain to gain reputation, register misleading device attributes) and submit a subject that triggers the enrichment lookup. Observe whether the poisoned 'facts' flip an automated approve/deny/escalate decision. Seam confirmed if the manipulated feed changes the decision and the pipeline cannot trace the poisoned source.

**Refuted if.** The pipeline corroborates enrichment against independent signals or treats single-source enrichment as advisory only (human review, multi-source consensus), so a single poisoned feed cannot move the decision.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to influence the upstream broker/reputation source (record seeding, reputation aging, registration), and a pipeline that auto-decisions on the feed.

**Existing controls.** Multi-source corroboration, provider data-quality/fraud controls, manual review thresholds, anomaly checks on enrichment values. IP/reputation aging and broker-record manipulation are demonstrated in adjacent fraud contexts.

**Proof-of-concept — Enrichment API poisons identity attributes consumed downstream (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: operator's own local enrichment + authz stubs; mock identity store.

*Setup:* No network. Save as gh4_enrich_poison.py and run.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH4 enrichment-api-identity-poisoning PoC.
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. An identity-enrichment API returns attacker-controllable
# attributes that the authz layer trusts, granting elevated role.
import sys

def enrichment_api(user_id, attacker_field):
    # untrusted third-party enrichment; attacker influences a returned attribute
    return {'user_id': user_id, 'department': attacker_field, 'verified': True}

def authorize(profile):
    # BUG (the seam): authz derives privilege from an enrichment attribute and
    # trusts 'verified' without provenance checks.
    if profile.get('verified') and profile.get('department') == 'admin':
        return 'role:admin'      # vulnerable path
    return 'role:user'

def main():
    # attacker sets department='admin' via the enrichment input it controls
    profile = enrichment_api('FAKE-USER-1', attacker_field='admin')
    role = authorize(profile)
    print('ENRICHED PROFILE:', profile)
    print('GRANTED ROLE:', role)
    if role == 'role:admin':
        print('[SEAM CONFIRMED] poisoned enrichment attribute elevated identity to admin')
        sys.exit(0)
    print('[no poisoning effect]'); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

*Success:* A FAKE user is granted role:admin because the enrichment-supplied department attribute was trusted; prints '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' and exits 0. Confirms downstream authz consumes unverified enrichment identity data.  
*Cleanup:* rm gh4_enrich_poison.py. No state.  
*Safety:* Enrichment API and authz are stubs over a FAKE user; no real identity provider or network.

**Research hook.** Build an enrichment-provenance layer that attaches source, freshness, and corroboration confidence to every third-party fact and requires multi-provider agreement before it can move an identity decision past a risk threshold; demonstrate broker-record seeding that flips an automated KYC/fraud verdict and measure detectability.


---

# Crypto migration / edge / telecom  *(9)*

## 60. Cache-key/authorization-input mismatch cross-tenant serve

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH5-cdn-edge-cache-key-confused-deputy` |
| **Primitive** | P5 Format-Boundary |
| **Trust frame** | Web / Fetch → Cloud Control Plane + Web / Fetch |
| **Category** | Cloud Attacks; Web / Application Attacks |
| **Validity** | demonstrated (high confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1213, CWE-525, CWE-639 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / none |

**The trust assumption.** A CDN/edge cache trusts that its computed cache key (the normalized subset of request attributes it hashes) fully captures every input the origin used to authorize and personalize a response, so a cache hit for a given key is safe to serve to any subsequent requester that produces the same key, regardless of headers, tenancy claims, or edge-function transforms not folded into the key.

**The attack.** An attacker crafts requests where an authorization- or tenant-determining input (an edge-function-injected header, a tenant token, a Vary-omitted field) influences the personalized/authorized origin response but is excluded from the cache key, so the edge caches a victim-scoped or differently-tenanted response and serves it cross-tenant to attacker requests — a confused-deputy disclosure born from the mismatch between what authorizes the response and what keys the cache.

**Mechanism (technical).** A CDN/edge cache stores responses keyed on a normalized subset of request attributes (path, selected headers, Vary). The cache assumes this key fully captures every input the origin used to authorize or personalize the response. When an authorization- or tenant-determining input influences the origin response but is excluded from the cache key - an edge-function-injected header, a tenant token, a Vary-omitted field, or a path/extension parsing discrepancy (web cache deception) - the edge caches a victim-scoped or differently-tenanted response and serves it to any later requester producing the same key. This is a confused-deputy disclosure born from the gap between what authorizes the response and what keys the cache.

**Exploitation primitive.** Craft a request whose authorization-relevant input is not folded into the cache key (e.g. trick the cache into storing a dynamic authenticated page under a static-looking path, or vary an unkeyed tenant header) so a victim-personalized response is cached and replayed cross-tenant.

**MITRE gap.** Closest Enterprise id is T1213 (Data from Information Repositories), tagged in input, and arguably T1539 for cached session data. Both are insufficient: they describe accessing a repository, not the cache-key/authorization-input mismatch that causes the cache itself to leak victim-scoped responses. ATT&CK has no technique for web cache deception or cache-key confusion, despite it being a demonstrated, in-the-wild class.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Omer Gil, 'Web Cache Deception Attack' (Black Hat USA 2017) — Original demonstration; practical attack against PayPal caching dynamic authenticated content under static-looking URLs. — <https://blackhat.com/docs/us-17/wednesday/us-17-Gil-Web-Cache-Deception-Attack.pdf>
- *research:* PortSwigger Research, 'Gotta cache em all: bending the rules of web cache exploitation' (James Kettle) — Cache-key parsing discrepancies and normalization gaps that let attackers store and hijack arbitrary authenticated responses. — <https://portswigger.net/research/gotta-cache-em-all>
- *research:* 'Cached and Confused: Web Cache Deception in the Wild' (USENIX Security, arXiv:1912.10190) — Large-scale measurement confirming WCD prevalence across major sites. — <https://arxiv.org/pdf/1912.10190>

**Detection (technical).** Audit that every input the origin uses for authorization/personalization is included in the cache key or Vary; alert on cache hits for responses containing Set-Cookie/Authorization-derived personalization; enforce cache rules that never cache responses to authenticated requests by content-type rather than by URL extension.

**References.** [PortSwigger: Gotta cache em all](https://portswigger.net/research/gotta-cache-em-all) · [Omer Gil: Web Cache Deception Attack (Black Hat 2017)](https://blackhat.com/docs/us-17/wednesday/us-17-Gil-Web-Cache-Deception-Attack.pdf)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Modern edge stacks let functions-at-edge mutate requests (inject identity headers, rewrite tenancy) and personalize responses before and after the cache, while the cache key is a separately-configured normalized hash. In 2025-2027 deployments the set of inputs that authorize a response has drifted away from the set folded into the cache key, especially when edge middleware adds tenant context the cache config never learned about. The violated trust is the cache's assumption that its key is a complete authorization fingerprint; the result is a confused deputy that serves one tenant's authorized content to another. This is broader and more current than classic web-cache-deception/poisoning because the divergence is now manufactured by programmable edge functions rather than just header quirks. Cache-key auditing tooling is only nascent and there is no detection that correlates an authorization-relevant input the origin honored against the keying config the edge actually used.

**How to validate it.** Deploy a CDN/edge config (e.g. Cloudflare/Fastly/CloudFront) where the origin authorizes/personalizes a response using an input (a tenant token, an edge-function-injected header, a Vary-omitted field) that is NOT part of the computed cache key. As victim, populate the cache with a scoped response; as attacker, issue a request that produces the same cache key and observe whether the victim-scoped/cross-tenant response is served. Seam confirmed on cross-tenant disclosure. This is web cache deception / key-confusion.

**Refuted if.** All authorization/personalization inputs are folded into the cache key or Vary, or auth'd responses are marked private/no-store, so a different requester never gets a cache hit on someone else's response.

**Prerequisites.** A CDN config with a mismatch between authorizing inputs and the cache key (misconfigured Vary, header injection by edge functions, tenant token outside key). Attacker needs only normal request access.

**Existing controls.** Correct Cache-Key/Vary configuration, Cache-Control private/no-store on authorized content, cache deception protections. Web cache deception and key-confusion disclosures are publicly demonstrated (multiple real incidents/bug bounties).

**Proof-of-concept — CDN cache-key confused deputy -> tenant header omitted, cross-tenant serve (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* No network. Save as gh5_cdn.py and run with python3. A local CDN/proxy cache sim derives its cache key from path only (not the tenant/auth header), so tenant B is served tenant A's cached private response.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH5 CDN cache-key confused deputy: cache key = path only; the X-Tenant/Authorization
# header is excluded from the key. Tenant A's private response is cached and served to
# tenant B for the same path. AUTHORIZED LAB SIM ONLY -- in-process cache.
import sys

CACHE = {}
ORIGIN = {('tenant-A','/api/me'): 'A-private:balance=$1000',
          ('tenant-B','/api/me'): 'B-private:balance=$5'}

def cache_key(req):
    # THE SEAM: key omits the tenant/auth header -> path collides across tenants.
    return req['path']

def edge(req):
    k = cache_key(req)
    if k in CACHE:
        return ('HIT', CACHE[k])
    resp = ORIGIN[(req['tenant'], req['path'])]
    CACHE[k] = resp
    return ('MISS', resp)

a = edge({'tenant':'tenant-A','path':'/api/me','auth':'FAKE-A-token'})
print('A:', a)
b = edge({'tenant':'tenant-B','path':'/api/me','auth':'FAKE-B-token'})
print('B:', b)
if b[0]=='HIT' and 'A-private' in b[1]:
    print('VULNERABLE: tenant-B served tenant-A cached private response (auth header excluded from cache key)')
    sys.exit(0)
print('PASS: cache key includes tenant -> isolated'); sys.exit(1)
```

*Success:* Tenant-B's request is a cache HIT returning tenant-A's private payload, printing VULNERABLE -- the auth/tenant header was excluded from the cache key (confused-deputy cross-tenant serve).  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only sim; delete gh5_cdn.py)  
*Safety:* In-process cache simulation with defanged tokens; no real CDN/proxy or live tenants.

**Research hook.** Develop a differential probe that enumerates request attributes, classifies which ones change the authorized/personalized origin response, and diffs that set against the live cache-key and Vary configuration to surface unkeyed authorization inputs; demonstrate cross-tenant cache serve through an edge-function-injected tenant header excluded from the key.


## 61. RSP activation-code/QR hijack installing unauthorized profile

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH5-esim-rsp-profile-provenance-gap` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Wireless / RF + Network → Mobile (iOS / Android) + Hardware / IoT / Firmware |
| **Category** | Mobile Attacks; Wireless / RF Attacks; Identity & Federation Attacks |
| **Validity** | demonstrated (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An eUICC/eSIM consumer device trusts that an operational profile delivered through the GSMA remote-SIM-provisioning (RSP) flow — once the activation code / QR / carrier-app handoff completes — was authorized by the legitimate subscriber for the legitimate operator, so the modem installs the profile and binds the device's network identity to it without independently re-verifying who actually initiated the provisioning.

**The attack.** An attacker who hijacks the provisioning initiation (a phished activation code, a malicious carrier-app deep link, or an SM-DP+ address swap) drives the RSP flow to install an attacker-chosen profile or download a profile onto an attacker-controlled eUICC, so the subscriber's mobile network identity is silently bound to a profile the genuine subscriber never authorized — an eSIM-swap that needs no physical SIM and leaves the provenance of the provisioning request unverified at the device.

**Mechanism (technical).** GSMA Consumer Remote SIM Provisioning (SGP.22) delivers an operator profile to an eUICC via an LPA that consumes an activation code of the form LPA:1$<SM-DP+ address>$<matching-id>, typically encoded in a QR or handed off by a carrier app. The eUICC and SM-DP+ mutually authenticate via the GSMA CI certificate chain, so the channel between honest entities is sound; what the device does not independently verify is who initiated the provisioning. If an attacker controls the initiation - a phished/forged activation code, a malicious carrier-app deep link, or an SM-DP+ address that points to an attacker-influenced flow - the modem installs the resulting profile and binds the device's network identity to it without re-authenticating the human subscriber's intent. The result is an eSIM-swap requiring no physical SIM, exploiting the provenance gap in provisioning initiation rather than a crypto break in RSP.

**Exploitation primitive.** Hijack the RSP initiation (phished activation code, malicious carrier-app handoff, or social-engineered SM-DP+/QR) to drive the eUICC to install an attacker-chosen profile or pull a subscriber's profile onto an attacker eUICC, hijacking the mobile identity.

**MITRE gap.** No ATT&CK Enterprise technique covers eSIM/eUICC remote provisioning. T1451 (SIM Card Swap) exists only in the deprecated ATT&CK Mobile matrix and describes operator-assisted physical/account SIM swap, not RSP activation-code/QR hijack or SM-DP+ flows. ATT&CK has no model for the GSMA RSP provenance gap, leaving this telecom-identity primitive entirely outside Enterprise.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *standard:* GSMA SGP.22 RSP Technical Specification (Consumer eSIM) and SGP.21 Architecture — Defines the LPA activation-code / matching-id / SM-DP+ flow; provisioning initiation is not bound to verified subscriber intent at the device. — <https://www.gsma.com/solutions-and-impact/technologies/esim/gsma_resources/sgp-22-v2-2-2/>
- *research:* Aalto University, 'Security Analysis of the Consumer Remote SIM Provisioning Protocol' (2024) — Formal analysis; RSP is secure between honest entities against network adversaries, highlighting that the residual risk lies in initiation/endpoint compromise rather than the protocol core. — <https://www.gsma.com/solutions-and-impact/technologies/esim/security-analysis-of-the-consumer-remote-sim-provisioning-protocol/>

**Detection (technical).** Require step-up subscriber authentication and out-of-band confirmation at the operator before an SM-DP+ releases a profile; monitor for profile downloads to a new eUICC EID shortly after account changes, and alert on activation-code redemption from anomalous device/geographies.

**References.** [GSMA: Security Analysis of the Consumer Remote SIM Provisioning Protocol](https://www.gsma.com/solutions-and-impact/technologies/esim/security-analysis-of-the-consumer-remote-sim-provisioning-protocol/) · [GSMA SGP.22 RSP Technical Specification](https://www.gsma.com/solutions-and-impact/technologies/esim/gsma_resources/sgp-22-v2-2-2/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** eSIM is becoming default on flagship phones and IoT in 2025-2027 (carrier-only US iPhones, eSIM-only IoT modules), and the RSP trust chain authenticates the SM-DP+ server and the eUICC chip cryptographically but does not bind the provisioning *initiation* to verified subscriber intent — the activation code or carrier-app handoff is the soft seam. The violated trust is provenance of the request: the device treats a completed RSP handshake as proof the right human asked for this profile, when an attacker who captures the initiation can swap identity without ever touching hardware, defeating the physical-possession assumption that made classic SIM-swap need an insider. This is a fresh, lightly-tooled telecom seam distinct from generic SIM-swap because it lives in the remote-provisioning protocol layer and the device-side initiation binding, where no consumer detection exists and no tooling verifies initiation provenance.

**How to validate it.** Reproduce an eSIM-swap by hijacking the RSP initiation: phish/social-engineer the carrier into issuing an activation code to an attacker, or manipulate a carrier-app deep link / SM-DP+ activation handoff so an attacker-chosen profile downloads onto an attacker eUICC. Confirm the subscriber's network identity binds to the attacker device. Real if provisioning completes without the device/operator re-verifying the initiator's authority.

**Refuted if.** The RSP flow enforces strong subscriber authentication at initiation (operator-side MFA/identity proofing), eUICC matching-ID + confirmation-code binding, and operator fraud controls that detect anomalous profile downloads, so a hijacked initiation cannot complete.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to compromise the provisioning initiation (carrier social engineering, malicious carrier-app link, or SM-DP+ address manipulation). The device-side RSP protocol itself does not authenticate the human initiator; the control lives operator-side.

**Existing controls.** GSMA SGP.22 confirmation codes / matching IDs, operator-side identity proofing and fraud detection, and carrier SIM-swap protections. eSIM-swap / SIM-swap fraud is a demonstrated real-world attack class, though it exploits operator process more than a device protocol flaw.

**Proof-of-concept — SM-DP+ profile download lacks subscriber-intent binding (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real spectrum/satellite/aircraft/ship/grid/network; conducted/shielded only.

*Setup:* Pure python logic model of GSMA SGP.22-style RSP profile download. Synthetic EID/ICCID/activation-code identifiers only. No real SM-DP+, no eUICC, no carrier. Models the seam: the download/install flow authenticates the eUICC and the activation code but does not bind the operation to an authenticated subscriber *intent*, so a leaked/relayed activation code installs a profile without consent.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH5 eSIM RSP profile-provenance gap -- OFFLINE LOGIC SIM ONLY.
# Synthetic identifiers; no real SM-DP+, eUICC, or carrier network.
# Trust boundary: an RSP download authorized purely by possession of an
# activation code + a valid eUICC challenge has no binding to the *subscriber's*
# authenticated intent. A relayed/leaked code provisions a profile onto an
# attacker-controlled (but otherwise valid) eUICC.
import secrets

class SMDPplus:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pending = {}
    def prepare(self, iccid, activation_code):
        self.pending[activation_code] = {"iccid": iccid, "consumed": False}
    def download(self, activation_code, euicc_eid, subscriber_intent_token=None):
        rec = self.pending.get(activation_code)
        if not rec or rec["consumed"]:
            return ("REJECT", "unknown/consumed code")
        # SEAM: server checks code validity + (implicitly) a valid eUICC cert,
        # but DOES NOT require subscriber_intent_token. So intent is ignored.
        rec["consumed"] = True
        return ("INSTALLED", {"iccid": rec["iccid"], "onto_eid": euicc_eid})

class SMDPplus_BoundIntent(SMDPplus):
    def download(self, activation_code, euicc_eid, subscriber_intent_token=None):
        rec = self.pending.get(activation_code)
        if not rec or rec["consumed"]:
            return ("REJECT", "unknown/consumed code")
        # DEFENSE: require a token proving the registered subscriber approved
        # THIS eid for THIS iccid (e.g. signed step-up auth bound to EID).
        expect = "intent:" + rec["iccid"] + ":" + euicc_eid
        if subscriber_intent_token != expect:
            return ("REJECT", "missing/invalid subscriber-intent binding")
        rec["consumed"] = True
        return ("INSTALLED", {"iccid": rec["iccid"], "onto_eid": euicc_eid})

ICCID = "8900000000000000001"
CODE  = "LPA:1$smdp.example$" + secrets.token_hex(6)
VICTIM_EID   = "EID-VICTIM-" + secrets.token_hex(2)
ATTACKER_EID = "EID-ATTACKER-" + secrets.token_hex(2)

print("== provenance-gap server ==")
s = SMDPplus(); s.prepare(ICCID, CODE)
print("attacker download:", s.download(CODE, ATTACKER_EID))
print("  ^ SEAM: profile installed onto attacker EID, no subscriber intent checked")

print("\n== intent-bound server (defense) ==")
s2 = SMDPplus_BoundIntent(); s2.prepare(ICCID, CODE)
print("attacker (no intent token):", s2.download(CODE, ATTACKER_EID))
print("subscriber (valid token)  :",
      s2.download(CODE, VICTIM_EID, "intent:"+ICCID+":"+VICTIM_EID))
```

*Success:* On the provenance-gap server, download() returns INSTALLED for the attacker EID using only the relayed activation code; the intent-bound server REJECTs that same call and only INSTALLs for the EID carrying the matching subscriber-intent token. Confirms the missing intent binding in-sim.  
*Cleanup:* No persistent state; exit interpreter.  
*Safety:* Offline protocol-logic simulation over synthetic identifiers; no carrier, SM-DP+, or eUICC contact.

**Research hook.** Analyze the consumer RSP initiation path (activation codes, carrier-app deep links, default SM-DP+ resolution) for points where request provenance is unverified, and prototype a subscriber-bound initiation token plus a device-side prompt that surfaces the requesting operator identity and a fraud signal for out-of-band profile downloads; demonstrate profile redirection via a swapped SM-DP+ address.


## 62. Hybrid keyshare strip forcing classical-only PQC fallback

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH5-pqc-hybrid-negotiation-downgrade` |
| **Primitive** | P6 Time/State |
| **Trust frame** | Network → Host / Endpoint + Identity & Federation |
| **Category** | Cryptography & PKI; Network Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A relying party that has been provisioned a hybrid post-quantum certificate or hybrid key-exchange group (classical+ML-KEM/ML-DSA) trusts that because the peer is PQC-capable, the negotiated session inherited the quantum-resistant half of the hybrid, so it records the connection as 'PQC-protected' in its posture and compliance state even when only the classical component actually established the shared secret.

**The attack.** An on-path attacker strips or fails the PQC half of the hybrid (drops the ML-KEM keyshare, forces a classical-only group, or presents the classical-only fallback chain a dual-stack deployment still accepts for legacy reach), and the relying party silently completes a purely classical session while its telemetry, audit log, and crypto-agility dashboard still report a quantum-safe handshake — leaving a harvestable session mislabeled as protected.

**Mechanism (technical).** TLS 1.3 hybrid PQC groups (e.g. X25519MLKEM768) carry both a classical ECDHE share and an ML-KEM encapsulation in a single named group; the shared secret is the concatenation of both, so a session is only quantum-safe if the hybrid group is actually negotiated. An on-path attacker who edits the ClientHello supported_groups / key_share extensions, drops the PQC keyshare, or forces a HelloRetryRequest toward a classical-only group can steer a dual-stack endpoint to complete a purely classical handshake. Because TLS 1.3's downgrade-protection sentinels in ServerHello.random only cover protocol-version downgrade and not key-exchange-group selection, the group choice itself is not cryptographically bound to a 'best mutually-supported' guarantee. The relying party records the session as PQC-protected from its policy intent even though only X25519 established the secret.

**Exploitation primitive.** An active MITM strips or fails the ML-KEM half of the hybrid keyshare (or exploits a server-side group-selection bug) so the handshake silently falls back to classical ECDHE while telemetry still labels it quantum-safe, yielding a harvest-now-decrypt-later record mislabeled as protected.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise has no technique for cryptographic key-exchange downgrade or PQC negotiation manipulation. The nearest is T1557 (Adversary-in-the-Middle), but it models interception/relay generically and says nothing about negotiating a weaker cipher/group while preserving an apparent-secure session, nor about the harvest-now-decrypt-later economics that make a mislabeled classical session the actual payload. No ATT&CK technique captures 'session completed but compliance posture is falsely PQC'.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *cve:* CVE-2026-2673 — OpenSSL 3.5/3.6 TLS 1.3 server using the DEFAULT keyword for supported groups loses the group tuple structure, suppresses the HelloRetryRequest, and accepts the client's first keyshare regardless of priority - so hybrid PQC groups like X25519MLKEM768 silently fall back to classical X25519. Concrete real-world instance of a key-exchange-group downgrade. — <https://github.com/openssl/openssl/discussions/30543>
- *standard:* draft-ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem / draft-ietf-tls-mlkem — IETF TLS WG hybrid ECDHE-MLKEM key agreement defining the X25519MLKEM768 construction whose PQC half can be stripped on negotiation. — <https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem/>
- *research:* Bhargavan et al., 'A Taxonomy of Downgrade Attacks in the TLS Protocol' (arXiv:1809.05681) — Establishes that TLS downgrade protection is partial and that algorithm/group selection is a distinct downgrade surface from version downgrade. — <https://arxiv.org/pdf/1809.05681>

**Detection (technical).** Alert when a session negotiated a classical-only group despite both peers advertising a hybrid PQC group; cross-check the actually-negotiated named group against the configured PQC policy floor rather than trusting the 'PQC-capable' flag, and flag any HelloRetryRequest that steers away from a hybrid group.

**References.** [OpenSSL discussion: CVE-2026-2673 TLS 1.3 may choose unexpected key agreement group](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/discussions/30543) · [draft-ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem: Post-quantum hybrid ECDHE-MLKEM Key Agreement for TLS 1.3](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** The 2025-2027 migration wave deploys hybrid certs and hybrid key exchange in dual-stack mode precisely so that PQC-incapable peers still connect, which means a classical-only outcome is a designed-in success, not an error. The dangerous seam is not the downgrade itself but that the relying party's posture/compliance accounting credits the connection as quantum-safe based on the cert's hybrid *capability* rather than the session's actual negotiated *outcome*, so an attacker who forces the classical path also forges the organization's record of being PQC-protected. This is distinct from generic TLS downgrade because the violated trust is the PQC-readiness bookkeeping layer that migration programs rely on to declare risk retired; no scanner today reconciles 'hybrid cert present' against 'PQC component actually used per session', and no IDS flags a hybrid-capable peer that settled on classical.

**How to validate it.** Set up a hybrid PQC-capable client/server (classical+ML-KEM, e.g., X25519MLKEM768) plus a posture/compliance logger that records the connection as 'PQC-protected'. As an on-path attacker, strip the hybrid group from the ClientHello supported_groups (or force the classical-only fallback the dual-stack deployment still accepts). Observe whether the session completes classical-only while the telemetry/audit still labels it quantum-safe.

**Refuted if.** The endpoints refuse to complete (or the posture logger flags) any session that did not negotiate the PQC group — i.e., hybrid is mandatory and downgrade causes handshake failure, not a silently-mislabeled classical session.

**Prerequisites.** On-path position to modify the handshake; a dual-stack deployment that still accepts classical-only for legacy reach; posture logging that infers PQC from capability rather than negotiated group.

**Existing controls.** TLS downgrade protections cover classical version/cipher rollback; mandating the hybrid group (fail-closed) and logging the actually-negotiated group rather than capability. Note: hybrid KEX itself resists downgrade if hybrid is required, so the seam depends on a permissive fallback config.

**Proof-of-concept — PQC hybrid negotiation downgrade -> classical-only handshake, posture mismatch (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* No network. Save as gh5_pqc.py and run with python3. Simulates a TLS ClientHello against a LOCAL/owned endpoint stub; the PQC hybrid keyshare is stripped, the handshake completes classical, yet the posture report claims PQC.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH5 PQC hybrid downgrade: an on-path/owned-proxy strips the X25519MLKEM768 hybrid
# keyshare from ClientHello; the LOCAL endpoint stub falls back to classical X25519,
# but the posture report still records 'PQC'. AUTHORIZED LAB SIM ONLY -- local stub.
import sys

client_hello = {'key_shares': ['X25519MLKEM768', 'X25519']}  # hybrid offered first

def strip_pqc(hello):
    # THE SEAM: middlebox removes the hybrid group, leaving only classical.
    hello = dict(hello)
    hello['key_shares'] = [g for g in hello['key_shares'] if 'MLKEM' not in g]
    return hello

def server_select(hello):
    for g in ['X25519MLKEM768', 'X25519']:
        if g in hello['key_shares']: return g
    return None

def posture_report(negotiated):
    # buggy posture telemetry: reports PQC if the CLIENT *advertised* it, not if used
    advertised_pqc = True  # client config says hybrid enabled
    return 'PQC' if advertised_pqc else 'classical'

downgraded = strip_pqc(client_hello)
neg = server_select(downgraded)
report = posture_report(neg)
print('negotiated group:', neg, '| posture report:', report)
if 'MLKEM' not in (neg or '') and report == 'PQC':
    print('VULNERABLE: classical-only handshake completed yet posture report claims PQC (downgrade hidden)')
    sys.exit(0)
print('PASS: posture matches negotiated group'); sys.exit(1)
```

*Success:* Negotiated group is classical X25519 while the posture report still says PQC, printing VULNERABLE -- the hybrid keyshare was stripped and the downgrade went unreported.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only sim; delete gh5_pqc.py)  
*Safety:* Local handshake simulation; no real TLS endpoint or third-party server contacted.

**Research hook.** Build a session-level reconciler that parses negotiated KEM groups and signature algorithms from live handshakes and diffs them against the hybrid capability advertised in the served certificate, alerting when a PQC-capable endpoint completes classical-only; red-team a dual-stack hybrid deployment by selectively dropping ML-KEM keyshares and confirming the compliance dashboard still reports quantum-safe.


## 63. Rotated-out lease accepted during downstream grace overlap

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH5-secrets-lease-rotation-window-race` |
| **Primitive** | P6 Time/State |
| **Trust frame** | Cloud Control Plane + Identity & Federation → Container / K8s + Cloud Control Plane |
| **Category** | Cloud Attacks; Identity & Federation Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1552.005, CWE-613, CWE-672 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** A workload that pulls short-lived credentials from a secrets manager (dynamic DB creds, leased cloud tokens) trusts that a credential it successfully cached is valid and authoritative for its full advertised TTL, and the issuing manager trusts that revoking/rotating the old secret at rotation time atomically retires it everywhere — so neither side accounts for the window in which a rotated-out credential is still accepted by a downstream resource that has not yet refreshed its trust material.

**The attack.** An attacker who captured a leased credential continues to use it during the rotation overlap window, because the secrets manager's rotation marks a new secret active but the downstream datastore/service still honors the prior one until its own cache or grace period expires, and the client meanwhile keeps presenting a cached lease past its real revocation — turning the rotation seam into a live re-use window where a credential is simultaneously 'rotated out' at the manager and 'still good' at the resource.

**Mechanism (technical).** Workloads pull short-lived credentials (dynamic DB creds, leased cloud tokens) from a secrets manager and cache them for their advertised TTL. The manager rotates by marking a new secret active, but revocation/retirement of the prior secret is not atomic across the downstream resource: a database or service may continue to honor the old credential until its own cache or grace period expires, and the manager's internal revocation 'ensures users become invalid within a reasonable time of the lease expiring' rather than instantly. The rotation seam is therefore a live re-use window where a credential is simultaneously 'rotated out' at the manager and 'still good' at the resource, while the client may keep presenting a cached lease past its real revocation.

**Exploitation primitive.** An attacker who captured a leased credential keeps using it during the rotation overlap, succeeding because the downstream datastore still accepts the prior secret until its grace/cache period lapses - past the point the manager considers it retired.

**MITRE gap.** Closest Enterprise id is T1552.005 (Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API) tagged in input, plus T1078 (Valid Accounts). Insufficient because both model use of obtained credentials but not the rotation-overlap race: the precise seam where 'rotated' at the issuer does not equal 'rejected' at the resource. ATT&CK has no technique for revocation-propagation lag / grace-window credential re-use as its own primitive.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* HashiCorp Vault - Lease, Renew, and Revoke / Database secrets engine — Vault dynamic secrets carry leases; revocation invalidates a secret but Vault's docs note users become invalid 'within a reasonable time of the lease expiring', and downstream DBs honor creds until rotation/revocation propagates - the overlap window. — <https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/lease>

**Detection (technical).** Instrument the downstream resource (not just the manager) to log credential acceptance and alert when a credential the manager has rotated out is still successfully authenticating; minimize/zero the downstream grace overlap and correlate manager rotation events with resource-side acceptance timestamps.

**References.** [HashiCorp Vault: Lease, Renew, and Revoke](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/lease) · [HashiCorp Vault: Database secrets engine](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/secrets/databases)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Dynamic secrets and short-TTL leases are sold as the answer to credential theft, but the operational reality in 2025-2027 is that rotation is never atomic across the three parties: the manager (Vault/cloud secrets), the client cache, and the downstream resource that validates the credential each maintain independent notions of validity, and rotation deliberately overlaps old and new to avoid outages. The violated trust is the assumption that 'rotated' means 'no longer accepted'; the overlap window is a designed-in re-use opportunity that a captured credential exploits with no anomaly because both old and new are momentarily legitimate. This is distinct from generic credential theft and from the excluded workload-identity-federation gap because the seam is the temporal non-atomicity of revocation across a distributed trust set, not the federation binding. Detection is only nascent (some managers log lease use) and no tooling reconciles 'manager says rotated' against 'resource still accepts' to expose the live overlap.

**How to validate it.** Configure a secrets manager (e.g., Vault dynamic DB creds) issuing short-lived leases to a workload, with a downstream datastore that has its own credential cache/grace. Capture a leased credential, trigger rotation, and during the overlap window attempt to authenticate to the downstream resource with the rotated-out credential. Measure whether it is still honored after the manager marks it rotated.

**Refuted if.** Rotation atomically revokes the old credential at the resource (immediate invalidation, no grace), so the captured credential fails the moment rotation occurs — no usable overlap window.

**Prerequisites.** Possession of a leased credential and timing knowledge of rotation; a downstream resource with non-atomic/grace credential acceptance. Common in dynamic-secret + caching architectures.

**Existing controls.** Atomic revocation at the resource, short overlap windows with active session termination, lease-bound source-IP/mTLS constraints, monitoring for use of rotated-out credentials. Rotation overlap is a recognized operational hazard.

**Proof-of-concept — Secrets lease rotation race -> old lease still honored in non-atomic window (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* No network. Save as gh5_rot.py and run with python3. Simulates a secrets manager whose rotation publishes the new secret before revoking the old, leaving a window where the old (possibly leaked) lease still authenticates.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH5 secrets rotation race: rotation is non-atomic -- new secret is published, but
# the old lease is revoked seconds later. During the window BOTH validate, so a
# previously-leaked old secret still works. AUTHORIZED LAB SIM ONLY -- in-memory store.
import sys

class SecretStore:
    def __init__(self): self.valid = {'v1'}
    def rotate_publish(self): self.valid.add('v2')      # step 1: add new
    def rotate_revoke_old(self): self.valid.discard('v1')  # step 2 (delayed)
    def authenticate(self, token): return token in self.valid

s = SecretStore()
leaked_old = 'v1'   # attacker exfiltrated this before rotation
s.rotate_publish()  # rotation begins; revoke step has NOT run yet (the window)
still_works = s.authenticate(leaked_old)
print('during rotation window, old leaked secret authenticates:', still_works)
if still_works:
    print('VULNERABLE: non-atomic rotation honors OLD lease in the publish->revoke window')
    sys.exit(0)
s.rotate_revoke_old()
print('PASS: old secret rejected (atomic rotation)'); sys.exit(1)
```

*Success:* The leaked old secret still authenticates after publish but before revoke, printing VULNERABLE -- the non-atomic rotation window honors a stale lease.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only sim; delete gh5_rot.py)  
*Safety:* In-memory secrets-store simulation with synthetic tokens; no real KMS/Vault touched.

**Research hook.** Instrument a Vault-or-cloud dynamic-secret pipeline to measure the real revocation-overlap window across manager, client cache, and downstream resource, and build a detector that flags use of a lease the manager already rotated but the resource still honors; prototype a rotation protocol that proves downstream retirement before marking a secret rotated.


## 64. SUCI cross-session linkability via unconcealed routing/capability residue

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH5-fiveg-suci-reidentification-bridge` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Wireless / RF + Network → Mobile (iOS / Android) + Identity & Federation |
| **Category** | Wireless / RF Attacks; Mobile Attacks; Identity & Federation Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (low confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A 5G subscriber trusts that SUCI concealment (encrypting the SUPI/IMSI with the home network's public key before it ever leaves the device) makes their permanent identity unlinkable on the radio interface, so the privacy guarantee of the standard is treated as protecting against the IMSI-catcher tracking that plagued earlier generations.

**The attack.** An attacker correlates the side-channel and protocol residue that SUCI does not conceal — stable per-device parameters, fixed home-network routing identifiers, deterministic SUCI re-computation patterns under a misconfigured null-scheme or reused randomness, and timing/capability fingerprints — to re-link successive concealed identifiers back to one subscriber across sessions, defeating the unlinkability the SUCI design is trusted to provide without ever recovering the cleartext SUPI.

**Mechanism (technical).** 5G conceals the permanent identity (SUPI/IMSI) by encrypting it with the home network's public key using ECIES into a SUCI before it leaves the device, so the on-air identifier changes each registration and IMSI-catcher tracking should be defeated. However, SUCI conceals only the SUPI payload, not surrounding residue: the unencrypted home-network routing identifier and protection-scheme/key-id fields, capability fingerprints, and - critically - the AKA procedure's behavior. Research shows an attacker who already knows a target IMSI can re-link captured SUCIs across sessions by abusing the 3GPP-mandated AKA Authentication-Failure responses (e.g. MAC-failure vs sync-failure timing/error codes), a linkability oracle that needs no key recovery. A misconfigured null-scheme or reused randomness further enables deterministic re-computation. Every tested 5G-SA deployment with SUCI active was found vulnerable because the surface is mandated behavior.

**Exploitation primitive.** Replay/elicit AKA responses against captured SUCIs to operate a SUCI-catcher linkability oracle, correlating successive concealed identifiers to one subscriber via Authentication-Failure error/timing residue without ever decrypting the SUPI.

**MITRE gap.** No ATT&CK Enterprise technique covers 5G subscriber-identity privacy or radio-interface linkability. The deprecated ATT&CK Mobile T1430 (Location Tracking) is the loosest analog and does not model SUCI/AKA linkability oracles, IMSI/SUPI concealment, or RAN side-channels. This telecom-identity attack has no ATT&CK technique at all.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Khan, Martin et al., '5G SUCI-Catchers: Still Catching Them All?' (ACM WiSec 2021) — Demonstrates SUCI-catcher linkability despite concealment, exploiting AKA-procedure residue rather than breaking ECIES. — <https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3448300.3467826>
- *research:* '5G SUCI Probing in the Wild' (2024) — Real-network study linking captured SUCIs to known IMSIs via unencrypted AKA Authentication-Failure error codes across commercial 5G-SA deployments. — <https://www.researchgate.net/publication/381853402_5G_SUCI_Probing_in_the_Wild>
- *standard:* 3GPP TS 33.501 (5G security architecture - SUCI/SUPI concealment, ECIES profiles) — Defines SUCI computation and the AKA procedure whose mandated failure behavior creates the linkability surface. — <https://www.3gpp.org/dynareport/33501.htm>

**Detection (technical).** Home network should monitor for repeated AKA failures / Authentication-Failure probing patterns and rate-limit per routing-indicator; deploy null-scheme detection and reject reused SUCI randomness; on-device, surface anomalous repeated authentication-failure prompts.

**References.** [5G SUCI-Catchers: Still Catching Them All? (ACM WiSec 2021)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3448300.3467826) · [5G SUCI Probing in the Wild (2024)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/381853402_5G_SUCI_Probing_in_the_Wild)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** 5G SUCI is marketed as the cure for IMSI-catchers, and standalone 5G deployment ramps through 2025-2027, so operators and regulators increasingly treat the radio-layer identity-privacy box as checked. The seam is that SUCI conceals the SUPI but leaves a constellation of unconcealed or weakly-randomized fields (routing indicator, home-network PKI key id, device capability fingerprints, and — where operators ship the null scheme or weak randomness — recomputable concealed values) that an analytic adversary fuses to re-establish linkability across sessions. The violated trust is the unlinkability provenance of the concealed identifier itself. This is a current, untooled privacy gap distinct from classic IMSI-catching because the attack is correlation against the metadata the privacy scheme does not cover, not interception of cleartext. No tooling measures real-world SUCI linkability and no detection flags re-identification campaigns.

**How to validate it.** Capture 5G NAS registration traffic across multiple sessions for a target device using an SDR-based 5G sniffer. Action: extract unconcealed residue (routing indicator, home network identifier, UE capability fingerprint, timing patterns; and test for null-scheme misconfiguration or reused randomness producing deterministic SUCI). Correlate these stable features across sessions to test whether successive concealed SUCIs can be re-linked to one subscriber without recovering the SUPI. Measure linkability accuracy vs a single-subscriber ground truth.

**Refuted if.** The unconcealed residue is not sufficiently device-stable or distinctive to re-link sessions above chance, and SUCI is computed with proper profile A/B (fresh ephemeral keys, non-null scheme) yielding non-deterministic ciphertext — so successive identifiers cannot be correlated to one subscriber.

**Prerequisites.** 5G radio capture capability (SDR + 5G NAS decoding) near the target and multiple observed registrations. Routing-indicator and capability-fingerprint linkability is studied in academic literature; null-scheme misconfig is a real but specific failure.

**Existing controls.** Mandatory non-null SUCI encryption scheme, fresh ephemeral keys per computation, routing-indicator privacy considerations, capability-transfer protection in later releases. Linkability via residue is an emerging research finding; no productized detection.

**Proof-of-concept — SUCI routing/timing residue bridges concealed identifiers back to a SUPI (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real spectrum/satellite/aircraft/ship/grid/network; conducted/shielded only.

*Setup:* Pure python logic model of 5G SUCI concealment (SUPI -> ECIES-style SUCI). Synthetic SUPIs/keys only; no real UE, gNB, or core. Models the seam: SUCI hides the MSIN, but unconcealed *side fields* (Routing Indicator, Home Network PKI key id) plus an observable correlation handle let an observer cluster and re-link multiple sessions of the same subscriber even though each SUCI ciphertext differs.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH5 5G SUCI re-identification bridge -- OFFLINE LOGIC SIM ONLY.
# Synthetic SUPIs and toy "concealment"; no real UE / gNB / 5G core.
# Trust boundary: SUCI conceals the MSIN, but the message still carries
# cleartext routing residue (Routing Indicator + HN public-key id) and the
# scheme leaves a per-subscriber correlatable handle. An observer never
# decrypts the SUPI yet re-links sessions -> a re-identification bridge.
import hashlib, os

def conceal(supi, routing_indicator, hn_key_id):
    # Toy SUCI: fresh ephemeral salt each session => ciphertext differs each time
    # (this is the property SUCI is supposed to give: unlinkability).
    eph = os.urandom(8)
    msin = supi.split("-")[1]
    ct = hashlib.sha256(eph + msin.encode()).hexdigest()
    # SEAM: these travel in clear, and (modeling a real residue) a stable
    # low-entropy correlation tag derived without the ephemeral salt leaks.
    corr_tag = hashlib.sha256(("STABLE" + msin).encode()).hexdigest()[:8]
    return {"ct": ct, "eph": eph.hex(),
            "routing_indicator": routing_indicator,
            "hn_key_id": hn_key_id,
            "corr_tag": corr_tag}

sessions = []
sessions += [conceal("SUPI-1110001", "0017", 27) for _ in range(3)]
sessions += [conceal("SUPI-2220002", "0099", 27) for _ in range(2)]

print("distinct ciphertexts:", len({s['ct'] for s in sessions}),
      "of", len(sessions), "(SUCI looks unlinkable per-message)")

clusters = {}
for s in sessions:
    key = (s["routing_indicator"], s["hn_key_id"], s["corr_tag"])
    clusters.setdefault(key, []).append(s["ct"])
print("\nre-identification clusters (routing+keyid+residue):")
for k, v in clusters.items():
    print(" ", k, "->", len(v), "sessions linked")
print("SEAM: sessions re-linked into per-subscriber clusters with zero decryption.")
```

*Success:* All SUCI ciphertexts are distinct (per-message unlinkability appears intact), yet clustering on the cleartext routing residue + correlation tag groups the sessions back into exactly the two originating subscribers. Confirms the re-identification bridge over synthetic identifiers.  
*Cleanup:* No persistent state; exit interpreter.  
*Safety:* Offline protocol-logic simulation over synthetic SUPIs; no UE, gNB, or 5G core contact.

**Research hook.** Capture SUCI exchanges across a controlled 5G SA testbed and measure cross-session linkability from routing indicators, capability fingerprints, and randomness quality, including operator configurations that fall back to the null scheme; build a linkability-scoring tool operators can run to validate that their SUCI deployment actually delivers unlinkability.


## 65. Phishable/typosquatted OIDC identity yielding valid keyless signature

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH5-keyless-signing-identity-binding-gap` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | Supply Chain / Build + Identity & Federation → Container / K8s + Supply Chain / Build |
| **Category** | CI/CD & Build Pipeline; Cryptography & PKI; Container & Orchestration |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1195.002, CWE-290, CWE-345 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / none |

**The trust assumption.** A verifier of a keyless-signed OCI artifact (Sigstore/Fulcio short-lived cert tying a signature to an OIDC identity) trusts that the OIDC identity baked into the signing certificate corresponds to a legitimately-controlled, human- or pipeline-owned account, so an artifact whose transparency-log entry maps to a recognizable identity string is accepted as authentically published by that party.

**The attack.** An attacker who controls a phishable, typosquatted, dormant, or workflow-impersonating OIDC identity (a compromised CI workflow ref, a look-alike email/GitHub identity, a reused org account) obtains a valid Fulcio certificate and signs a malicious artifact, and the verifier's policy admits it because the identity *string* matches an allowed pattern even though the binding from that OIDC identity to a trustworthy real-world publisher was never itself validated — the keyless model moved the trust to an OIDC identity that the verifier never authenticated the provenance of.

**Mechanism (technical).** Sigstore keyless signing (Fulcio) binds an artifact signature to an OIDC identity rather than a long-lived key: the signer presents an OIDC token, Fulcio issues a short-lived X.509 cert with the OIDC subject (email or CI workflow ref) as the SAN, and the entry is logged to Rekor. Verifiers admit an artifact when its transparency-log entry's identity string matches an allowed pattern. The trust therefore rests entirely on the OIDC identity - which Fulcio assumes a valid token proves ownership of - but the verifier never authenticates the binding from that identity to a trustworthy real-world publisher. A phishable, typosquatted, dormant, or workflow-impersonating identity (a look-alike email, a reused org account, a compromised CI workflow ref) yields a fully valid Fulcio cert and a passing signature.

**Exploitation primitive.** Obtain a valid OIDC token for a look-alike/compromised identity (typosquatted email, hijacked GitHub Actions workflow ref), get a legitimate Fulcio certificate, and sign a malicious artifact whose identity string matches the verifier's allow-pattern.

**MITRE gap.** Closest Enterprise id is T1195.002 (Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain), tagged in input, plus T1199/T1606 for trust/forging. Insufficient because they describe supply-chain tampering broadly but not the keyless-signing identity-binding gap - that a cryptographically valid signature can be issued to an attacker-controlled-but-pattern-matching OIDC identity the verifier never independently vetted. ATT&CK has no technique for OIDC-identity-to-publisher provenance failure in signing.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* Sigstore Fulcio - OIDC usage and threat model — Fulcio assumes a valid OIDC token from a trusted provider is sufficient proof of ownership of the identity; transparency-log monitoring is the recommended mitigation, confirming the binding is not verifier-authenticated. — <https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/threat-model/>
- *research:* Sigstore blog, 'Why you cant use Sigstore without Sigstore' — Discusses identity/typosquatting detection via Fulcio certificate-transparency monitoring as the defense, underscoring the absence of intrinsic publisher-provenance verification. — <https://blog.sigstore.dev/why-you-cant-use-sigstore-without-sigstore-de1ed745f6fc/>

**Detection (technical).** Pin verification policy to exact, vetted OIDC identities and issuers (full workflow ref + repo + issuer), not loose patterns; monitor the Fulcio/Rekor transparency log for certificates issued to look-alike or unexpected identities for your namespaces, and alert on first-seen signing identities.

**References.** [Sigstore Threat Model](https://docs.sigstore.dev/about/threat-model/) · [Sigstore: OIDC Usage in Fulcio](https://docs.sigstore.dev/certificate_authority/oidc-in-fulcio/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Keyless signing (Sigstore/Fulcio/Rekor) is the fast-spreading 2025-2027 answer to 'no more long-lived signing keys,' and verifiers increasingly gate artifact admission on the OIDC identity embedded in the short-lived cert. The overlooked seam is that the security now rests entirely on the trustworthiness of that OIDC identity binding, which was itself phishable, typosquattable, or impersonable at the CI-workflow level — the verifier authenticates that *a* valid OIDC identity signed, not that the identity legitimately represents the publisher it claims. This is explicitly distinct from CA mis-issuance (no rogue CA needed; Fulcio behaves correctly) and from SLSA provenance scope: the violated trust is the identity-binding semantics of keyless signatures and the verifier's pattern-matching policy. Identity-allowlist tooling is only nascent and brittle to look-alikes, and there is no detection for keyless signatures from newly-seen, dormant, or impersonating identities.

**How to validate it.** Using Sigstore/Fulcio in a test setup, obtain a valid keyless signing cert for an OIDC identity an attacker controls (e.g., a look-alike GitHub account, a typosquatted email, or a compromised workflow ref) and sign a malicious OCI artifact. Configure a verifier with a pattern-matching identity policy and confirm it admits the artifact because the identity string matches the allowed pattern though the real-world publisher binding was never validated.

**Refuted if.** Verifier policy pins exact, attacker-uncontrollable identities (specific repo + workflow ref + issuer) such that a typosquatted/look-alike OIDC identity does not match, so the malicious artifact is rejected.

**Prerequisites.** Control of a plausible-looking OIDC identity; ability to publish an artifact and a verifier with loose identity-pattern policy.

**Existing controls.** Sigstore docs stress pinning exact issuer+identity (e.g., specific GitHub workflow ref) precisely to avoid this; the seam is loose-policy or typosquat-trust misconfiguration. The class (signing != trustworthy publisher) is acknowledged in supply-chain security discourse.

**Proof-of-concept — Keyless signing identity-binding gap -> phishable OIDC identity admitted (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* No network. Save as gh5_keyless.py and run with python3. A sigstore/Fulcio-style verifier stub admits an artifact whose certificate SAN is a phishable email/OIDC string, because the policy matches a loose pattern instead of a pinned workflow identity.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH5 keyless signing binding gap: the verifier policy admits any cert whose OIDC
# SAN matches a loose email pattern. An attacker who phishes/registers a lookalike
# OIDC identity gets a valid Fulcio cert and passes. AUTHORIZED LAB SIM ONLY.
import re, sys

# verifier policy (too loose): accept any *@example.test signer
POLICY = {'san_regex': r'.*@example\.test$', 'issuer': 'https://oidc.example.test'}

# legitimate expected identity (what SHOULD be pinned)
EXPECTED = 'https://github.com/example-org/app/.github/workflows/release.yml@refs/tags/v1'

# attacker-controlled but VALID Fulcio cert from a phished/registered identity
attacker_cert = {'san': 'ci-bot@example.test', 'issuer': 'https://oidc.example.test'}

def verify(cert):
    # THE SEAM: matches loose SAN regex + issuer, never pins the exact workflow identity.
    return bool(re.match(POLICY['san_regex'], cert['san'])) and cert['issuer']==POLICY['issuer']

admitted = verify(attacker_cert)
is_expected = attacker_cert['san'] == EXPECTED
print('cert SAN:', attacker_cert['san'], '| admitted:', admitted, '| == pinned identity:', is_expected)
if admitted and not is_expected:
    print('VULNERABLE: artifact admitted on a phishable OIDC SAN, not the pinned workflow identity')
    sys.exit(0)
print('PASS: verifier pins exact identity'); sys.exit(1)
```

*Success:* The attacker's loosely-matching OIDC SAN is admitted though it is not the pinned workflow identity, printing VULNERABLE -- keyless verification bound trust to a phishable identity pattern.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only sim; delete gh5_keyless.py)  
*Safety:* Offline verification-policy logic with synthetic certs/identities; no real Fulcio/OIDC/Rekor contacted.

**Research hook.** Build an identity-binding risk scorer for keyless-signature policies that flags look-alike, newly-registered, dormant, or workflow-ref-mismatched OIDC identities and requires reputation/age evidence before admission; demonstrate admitting a malicious artifact via a typosquatted OIDC identity that satisfies a naive identity-pattern verification policy.


## 66. Crypto-agility policy rollback to deprecated suite

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH5-crypto-agility-policy-rollback-race` |
| **Primitive** | P6 Time/State |
| **Trust frame** | Cloud Control Plane + Supply Chain / Build → Host / Endpoint + Network |
| **Category** | Cryptography & PKI; Cloud Attacks |
| **Validity** | speculative (low confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1562.001, CWE-757, CWE-1188 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A crypto-agility control plane (centralized cipher-policy service, KMS algorithm profile, or fleet crypto-policy CRD) is trusted by every workload that reads it to only ratchet algorithm strength forward, so workloads cache and apply the distributed policy without re-validating that a newly fetched 'allowed algorithms' set is not weaker than the one they were already enforcing.

**The attack.** An attacker with write access to the agility policy store (or who replays an older signed policy version) pushes a policy revision that re-enables a deprecated suite or lowers the minimum negotiated group, and the fleet's workloads apply it as an authoritative forward update because the agility mechanism designed to make algorithms changeable has no monotonic floor — making the very lever built to strengthen crypto into a one-command fleet-wide weakening primitive.

**Mechanism (technical).** Crypto-agility control planes (centralized cipher-policy services, KMS algorithm profiles, fleet crypto-policy CRDs) distribute an 'allowed algorithms' set that workloads cache and enforce. The distribution channel is typically designed for forward change without a monotonic strength floor: a newly fetched policy revision simply replaces the cached one. An attacker with write access to the policy store, or who replays an older legitimately-signed policy version (a rollback), can push a revision that re-enables a deprecated suite or lowers the minimum group. Because the agility mechanism treats any authoritative-looking update as a forward update, the very lever built to strengthen crypto becomes a one-command fleet-wide weakening primitive.

**Exploitation primitive.** Write or replay a prior signed crypto-policy version that re-enables a weak suite; the fleet applies it as an authoritative update because there is no monotonic 'never weaker than current' check on policy application.

**MITRE gap.** Closest Enterprise id is T1562.001 (Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools), tagged in input. It is insufficient because it frames the act as disabling a security control, whereas here the security control (the agility policy plane) is functioning exactly as designed - it just lacks a strength-monotonicity invariant. ATT&CK has no concept of 'configuration rollback to a weaker-but-valid prior policy version' or signed-policy replay as a downgrade, and no technique for crypto-policy distribution integrity specifically.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *technique:* TLS downgrade attacks — FREAK (CVE-2015-0204) & Logjam (CVE-2015-4000) — canonical proof that a cipher-agility/negotiation mechanism which accepts a weaker-but-valid suite becomes a weakening primitive: attackers forced sessions down to export-grade 512-bit RSA/DH that were still allowed, breaking confidentiality without disabling any control — <https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2015-4000>
- *standard:* The Update Framework (TUF) — rollback-attack defense via monotonic version numbers — signed-metadata systems require strictly increasing version counters precisely because replaying an older, legitimately-signed revision is a known downgrade vector — the exact monotonicity invariant crypto-policy planes omit — <https://theupdateframework.io/security/>

**Detection (technical).** Enforce a monotonic crypto-policy floor at the workload: reject any fetched policy whose minimum algorithm strength or version counter is lower than the currently-enforced one, and alert on policy revisions that re-introduce previously-deprecated suites; require strictly increasing signed policy version numbers to defeat replay.

**References.** [NIST SP 1800-38: Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography (crypto agility)](https://www.nccoe.nist.gov/crypto-agility-considerations-migrating-post-quantum-cryptographic-algorithms)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Crypto-agility is the headline answer to PQC migration: orgs are standing up central policy planes in 2025-2027 so they can swap algorithms without redeploying code. But agility is bidirectional by construction, and the deployment model is 'workloads trust the latest policy the plane hands them.' Nobody is treating the algorithm-strength dimension as a monotonic counter the way anti-rollback treats firmware versions, so a compromised or replayed policy revision quietly relaxes the entire fleet at once and looks like a normal config push. This is a new seam distinct from per-connection downgrade and from the excluded crypto-agility-rollback hint's framing of negotiation: here the trust violated is the distribution channel's implicit monotonicity, landing on every workload that treats centralized crypto policy as authoritative. No policy plane enforces a strength floor or signs a non-decreasing version, and SIEMs do not parse policy diffs for strength regressions.

**How to validate it.** In a fleet that fetches an 'allowed algorithms' policy from a central store (KMS profile, crypto-policy CRD, config service), push or replay an older signed policy revision that re-enables a deprecated suite or lowers the minimum group.
