**Mechanism (technical).** A TWAP oracle (e.g. Uniswap V3's geometric-mean accumulator) integrates price over an observation window by summing tick*time between checkpoints; the 'time' weight of each price is the number of seconds (effectively blocks) that price persisted. When a single actor controls consecutive block production - a PoS proposer who gets two adjacent slots, an L2 sequencer with unilateral ordering, or anyone exploiting long/irregular block intervals - they can push a low-liquidity pool's spot price in block N and revert it in block N+1, so a manipulated tick dominates the time-weighting with no arbitrage exposure between the two blocks. The averaging that the consumer treats as manipulation-resistant ground truth is defeated by warping the time dimension (which blocks land in-window, and for how long) rather than by overpowering the prices. Post-Merge this is materially cheaper because a proposer can back-run their own manipulation in a guaranteed multi-block sequence.

**Exploitation primitive.** Acquire two consecutive blocks (proposer/builder via MEV markets or sole L2 sequencer), spike a thin pool's price in the first block, read the now-skewed TWAP from a lending/liquidation contract in the same or next block, then unwind - extracting collateral or triggering liquidations against the warped average.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise has no technique for on-chain price-oracle or TWAP manipulation; there is no smart-contract data-source tactic. The nearest analogue, T1565 Data Manipulation (Stored/Transmitted), describes tampering with data at rest or in transit on conventional hosts and assumes an integrity boundary an attacker breaches - it cannot express manipulation of a value that is honestly computed from a public AMM whose pricing the attacker legitimately moves with capital and block-ordering control, so it is insufficient.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Euler Finance - Uniswap V3 TWAP manipulation cost-of-attack — Formal cost-of-attack analysis and simulator quantifying single- vs multi-block TWAP manipulation; shows feasibility hinges on low in-range liquidity and proposer multi-block control. — <https://github.com/euler-xyz/uni-v3-twap-manipulation>
- *research:* Uniswap Labs - Uniswap v3 TWAP Oracles in Proof of Stake — Uniswap's own analysis of how PoS multi-block proposer control reduces TWAP manipulation cost and recommended window/liquidity mitigations. — <https://blog.uniswap.org/uniswap-v3-oracles>
- *incident:* Mango Markets oracle manipulation ($117M, Oct 2022) — Real-world oracle price manipulation: thin MNGO liquidity let the attacker pump the oracle-referenced price ~2300% and borrow against inflated collateral; demonstrates the economic class even though the warp vector here was liquidity not block cadence. — <https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2022/10/12/how-market-manipulation-led-to-a-100m-exploit-on-solana-defi-exchange-mango>

**Detection (technical).** Off-chain: monitor for the same EOA/proposer appearing as fee_recipient/coinbase across two consecutive blocks containing a large swap and its inverse on a low-liquidity pool, plus an oracle-consuming call (liquidation/borrow) sandwiched between them. On-chain: alert when a pool's spot price deviates sharply from its own longer TWAP (e.g. >X% spot-vs-30min) within the same block an oracle read occurs, or when in-range liquidity collapses toward zero right before a price spike.

**References.** [Euler - uni-v3-twap-manipulation (cost-of-attack)](https://github.com/euler-xyz/uni-v3-twap-manipulation) · [Uniswap v3 TWAP Oracles in Proof of Stake](https://blog.uniswap.org/uniswap-v3-oracles) · [Mango Markets exploit explainer (CoinDesk)](https://www.coindesk.com/markets/2022/10/12/how-market-manipulation-led-to-a-100m-exploit-on-solana-defi-exchange-mango)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** After the flash-loan spot-oracle disasters, the entire DeFi industry treated TWAP as the cure, and 2025-2027 protocols on app-chains, rollups, and alt-L1s deploy short-window TWAPs as their canonical manipulation-resistant feed. The overlooked seam is that TWAP's security rests on an assumption about *time* — that observations are sampled at honest, roughly-uniform intervals — yet on chains where the attacker influences block timing (centralized L2 sequencers, MEV proposer control, configurable block times) the weighting can be skewed without sustaining the manipulated price, so a brief excursion gains outsized weight. This is distinct from the classic flash-loan spot-oracle hint because the violated trust is the temporal honesty of the sampling, not the instantaneous price; it is a time/state primitive failure that becomes exploitable precisely on the new high-throughput, single-sequencer venues protocols are migrating to. No oracle library validates inter-observation timing integrity against the sequencer, and no monitor flags a liquidation whose TWAP window was dominated by anomalously-spaced blocks.

**How to validate it.** On a testnet/fork, deploy a protocol using a short-window on-chain TWAP (e.g. Uniswap v2/v3 cumulative-price oracle) backing liquidation/mint. As an attacker controlling block production cadence (local sequencer, or a forked chain where you control block timestamps/ordering) on a low-liquidity pool, manipulate price and stuff/space blocks so the manipulated observations dominate the time-weighting, then trigger the dependent action within the warped window. Measure extracted value vs. a fair TWAP.

**Refuted if.** The TWAP window is long enough and the pool liquid enough that controlling cadence cannot make a brief manipulated price dominate, or the protocol cross-checks against an independent oracle (e.g. Chainlink) so the warped TWAP doesn't drive the action.

**Prerequisites.** Control of block production cadence (own L2 sequencer, proposer/builder power, or a chain with long/irregular block times) and a low-liquidity pool; a protocol relying on a short on-chain TWAP.

**Existing controls.** Longer TWAP windows, deep-liquidity pools, multi-oracle / Chainlink cross-checks, per-block manipulation caps. TWAP manipulation generally is demonstrated; the specific cadence/sequencer-warping vector is plausible but liquidity-bounded.

**Proof-of-concept — Oracle TWAP window skew via cross-block timestamp warp (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/local-testnet only, signed RoE. Throwaway keys, no mainnet/real funds.

*Setup:* Pure-logic simulation (no chain needed; faithful model of a Uniswap-V2-style cumulative-price TWAP). Save as twap_warp.py and run: python3 twap_warp.py . Models an oracle that derives a time-weighted average price from price0CumulativeLast snapshots taken at two block timestamps; an attacker who controls reserve mutation between snapshots and the (proposer-influenced) timestamp delta skews the average a consumer trusts.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH6 TWAP window cross-block warp -- AUTHORIZED LAB / OFFLINE SIM ONLY.
# Faithful logic model of a V2-style cumulative-price TWAP oracle.
# Demonstrates the trust boundary: TWAP correctness assumes an honest, well-spaced
# observation window; a short manipulated window (proposer-warped timestamp + reserve
# nudge) lets the average diverge from the true long-run price.
# No chain, no funds, no keys. Pure arithmetic.
import sys

UQ = 2**112  # fixed-point scale used by V2 oracles

class Pair:
    def __init__(self, r0, r1, ts):
        self.r0, self.r1 = r0, r1
        self.price0_cum = 0
        self.last_ts = ts
    def _accumulate(self, now):
        dt = now - self.last_ts
        if dt > 0:
            # price0 = reserve1/reserve0 in UQ112.112, times elapsed seconds
            self.price0_cum += (self.r1 * UQ // self.r0) * dt
            self.last_ts = now
    def sync(self, r0, r1, now):
        self._accumulate(now)
        self.r0, self.r1 = r0, r1

def twap(cum_a, ts_a, cum_b, ts_b):
    return (cum_b - cum_a) / (ts_b - ts_a) / UQ

def main():
    # True long-run price ~ 1.0 (reserves balanced) over an honest 30-min window.
    p = Pair(1_000_000, 1_000_000, 0)
    p._accumulate(0)
    snap_a = (p.price0_cum, p.last_ts)
    # HONEST consumer expects ~1800s spacing.
    p._accumulate(1800)
    honest = twap(snap_a[0], snap_a[1], p.price0_cum, p.last_ts)
    print("HONEST window dt=1800s  twap=%.4f" % honest)

    # ATTACK: re-snapshot, then within a tiny warped window the attacker swings
    # reserves AND the proposer warps the block timestamp to a 2s delta so the
    # skewed spot price dominates the 'average'.
    p2 = Pair(1_000_000, 1_000_000, 0)
    p2._accumulate(0)
    a = (p2.price0_cum, p2.last_ts)
    p2.sync(200_000, 1_800_000, 0)        # attacker pushes price0 to ~9.0
    p2._accumulate(2)                      # WARP: only 2s elapse before consumer reads
    skewed = twap(a[0], a[1], p2.price0_cum, p2.last_ts)
    print("WARPED window dt=2s     twap=%.4f" % skewed)

    drift = skewed / honest
    print("drift factor = %.2fx" % drift)
    if drift > 3.0:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] short warped observation window skews TWAP >3x vs honest window")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[no skew]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* stdout prints HONEST twap ~1.0, WARPED twap ~9.x, a drift factor >3x, and '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' with exit 0 -- proving an attacker-controlled (short) observation window lets the TWAP an oracle consumer trusts diverge sharply from the true long-run price.  
*Cleanup:* rm -f twap_warp.py (no node started). If you ran the optional anvil variant: kill the anvil PID.  
*Safety:* Local-only pure arithmetic simulation; no chain, no funds, no keys, models only the math of the trust boundary.

**Research hook.** Build a TWAP-window auditor that reconstructs the block-timestamp distribution feeding each oracle read and scores how concentrated the time-weight was on suspicious intervals, alerting on liquidations/mints whose window was cadence-skewed; on a single-sequencer L2 testbed, demonstrate value extraction by spacing blocks to over-weight a transient manipulated price without holding it.


## 75. Source-chain deep reorg after destination mint at fixed confirmation depth

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH6-bridge-lightclient-finality-reorg-mint` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Blockchain / Smart Contract → Blockchain / Smart Contract |
| **Category** | Cryptography & PKI; Impact; API & Business-Logic Abuse |
| **Validity** | plausible (high confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / none |

**The trust assumption.** A cross-chain bridge that verifies source-chain events with an on-target light client trusts that a header/proof which passed the source chain's stated finality rule is irreversible, so once the light client accepts a deposit proof at the bridge's configured confirmation depth it irrevocably mints or releases the wrapped asset on the destination chain.

**The attack.** An attacker exploits the gap between the bridge's hard-coded confirmation depth and the source chain's *actual* probabilistic or reconfigurable finality — triggering a deep reorg, a finality-gadget stall, or a validator-set rotation that orphans the proven block after the destination already minted — so a deposit that gets rolled back on the source chain has already been released on the destination, double-spending across the seam that assumes finality is a single global truth.

**Mechanism (technical).** A lock-and-mint bridge runs an on-destination light client (or relayer set) that accepts a source-chain deposit once the included header/proof reaches a hard-coded confirmation depth, then irreversibly mints the wrapped asset. The depth is a static assumption about finality, but real finality is probabilistic (PoW depth) or reconfigurable (PoS finality gadgets can stall, validator sets rotate, and some L2s have soft 'finality' that lags L1 settlement). If the source chain reorgs deeper than the configured depth - or a finality-gadget stall is resolved on a fork that orphans the proven block - the source-side Lock is rolled back while the destination-side Mint persists, producing unbacked wrapped tokens. The seam is the bridge equating its local confirmation count with a single global notion of irreversibility the source chain never actually guaranteed at that depth.

**Exploitation primitive.** Deposit on a source chain near a known reorg-prone condition (short-block PoW chain, contentious fork, or a chain where the attacker can induce/await a deep reorg), let the destination mint at the bridge's fixed depth, then cause or wait out a reorg that reverts the source deposit while keeping the minted wrapped asset - a cross-chain double-spend.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK has no concept of blockchain finality, chain reorganization, or cross-chain double-spend; no tactic models trust in a consensus-finality assumption. The loosest analogue is T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation, but a reorg is not the attacker corrupting stored data - it is the source ledger legitimately rewriting its own history per its consensus rules while a second system already acted on the rewritten state, which ATT&CK's host-centric integrity model cannot represent.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Across Protocol - A Guide to Finality Risk — Practitioner analysis of finality risk for bridges: confirmation-depth choices vs probabilistic/reconfigurable finality and the reorg double-mint scenario. — <https://across.to/blog/Dodging-the-Finality-Bullet-A-Guide-to-Finality-Risk>
- *research:* Double-Spending Attacks in Cross-Blockchain Ecosystems (ScienceDirect) — Peer-reviewed treatment of reorg-driven double-spend across linked chains via bridges, formalizing the confirmation-depth vs actual-finality gap. — <https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2096720925001058>
- *research:* LI.FI - Ethereum, Finality and Bridges — Explains why honoring source events before finality (or with optimistic depth) lets a later reorg remove the Lock while the Mint remains, yielding unbacked assets. — <https://li.fi/knowledge-hub/ethereum-finality-and-bridges>

**Detection (technical).** On-chain: cross-monitor source and destination - flag any destination mint whose corresponding source deposit transaction/log no longer exists at the canonical source height (proof references an orphaned block hash). Off-chain: track source-chain reorg depth in real time and raise alerts when observed reorg depth approaches or exceeds the bridge's configured confirmation depth; reconcile total wrapped supply against escrowed source collateral and alert on any positive divergence.

**References.** [Across - Dodging the Finality Bullet: A Guide to Finality Risk](https://across.to/blog/Dodging-the-Finality-Bullet-A-Guide-to-Finality-Risk) · [Double-Spending Attacks in Cross-Blockchain Ecosystems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2096720925001058) · [LI.FI - Ethereum, Finality and Bridges](https://li.fi/knowledge-hub/ethereum-finality-and-bridges)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Light-client bridges are sold in 2025-2027 as the trust-minimized successor to multisig bridges, verifying real consensus proofs instead of trusting a federation. The unexamined seam is that 'finality' is not a uniform property: many chains offer only probabilistic finality, some have finality gadgets that can stall or revert under attack, and validator-set rotations can invalidate a header the light client already accepted. Bridges encode a static confirmation depth as a proxy for irreversibility, but that proxy can be wrong exactly when an attacker forces a reorg deeper than the configured threshold, and the destination mint is irreversible while the source deposit is not. This is distinct from the validator-quorum-forgery bridge hint because the cryptographic proof is honest and the quorum is not forged — the violated trust is the equivalence between 'passed my confirmation depth' and 'final on the source chain.' No bridge tooling adapts confirmation depth to live reorg risk, and no detector reconciles a destination mint against a subsequent source-chain reorg that orphaned its proof.

**How to validate it.** On a testnet, deploy a bridge with an on-target light client minting at a fixed confirmation depth N. Deposit on source, let destination mint at depth N, then induce a source-chain reorg deeper than N (or a finality-gadget stall / validator-set rotation orphaning the proven block). Observe whether the destination retains the minted/released asset while the source deposit is rolled back, yielding a double-spend across the seam.

**Refuted if.** The light client requires economic/BFT finality (not just a depth heuristic) and rejects or reverses any proof whose source block does not reach irreversible finality, so a reorg cannot leave a minted asset standing.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to cause/observe a source-chain reorg deeper than the bridge's confirmation depth (PoW hashpower, validator influence, or a chain with reconfigurable/probabilistic finality); a bridge using fixed-depth confirmation. Bridge finality/reorg exploits are a documented incident class.

**Existing controls.** Waiting for true finality (e.g., Casper FFG finalized checkpoints) rather than fixed depth, conservative confirmation depths, fraud-proof/optimistic windows, pausing mint on reorg detection.

**Proof-of-concept — Bridge light-client mints on sub-finality header before reorg (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/local-testnet only, signed RoE. Throwaway keys, no mainnet/real funds.

*Setup:* Pure-logic simulation of a cross-chain bridge's light-client header verifier + mint queue. Save as bridge_reorg.py and run: python3 bridge_reorg.py . Models a source-chain header chain with a finality depth requirement; the bridge mints destination tokens after seeing a header at depth < required_finality, then the source chain reorgs that header away.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH6 bridge light-client finality / reorg mint -- AUTHORIZED LAB / OFFLINE SIM ONLY.
# Models the trust boundary: a light-client bridge must only mint on FINALIZED
# source headers (>= K confirmations). A relayer/verifier that accepts a header
# at insufficient depth mints tokens that a reorg later orphans -> unbacked mint.
import sys

REQUIRED_FINALITY = 12  # bridge policy: header must be buried this deep

class SourceChain:
    def __init__(self):
        self.canonical = ["genesis"]
    def extend(self, n, tag):
        for i in range(n):
            self.canonical.append("%s#%d" % (tag, i))
    def depth_of(self, header):
        # confirmations on top of `header` in the current canonical chain
        if header not in self.canonical:
            return -1  # orphaned
        return len(self.canonical) - 1 - self.canonical.index(header)
    def reorg_from(self, header, n, tag):
        idx = self.canonical.index(header)
        self.canonical = self.canonical[:idx] + ["%s#%d" % (tag, i) for i in range(n)]

def bridge_verify_and_mint(chain, header, enforce_finality):
    depth = chain.depth_of(header)
    if enforce_finality and depth < REQUIRED_FINALITY:
        print("REJECT mint: header depth=%d < required=%d" % (depth, REQUIRED_FINALITY))
        return False
    print("MINT issued for header %s at depth=%d" % (header, depth))
    return True

def main():
    chain = SourceChain()
    chain.extend(3, "a")               # deposit header buried only 2 deep
    deposit_header = chain.canonical[1]

    # THE SEAM: verifier does NOT enforce finality depth (or relayer races ahead).
    minted = bridge_verify_and_mint(chain, deposit_header, enforce_finality=False)

    # Source chain reorgs out the branch containing the deposit header.
    chain.reorg_from(chain.canonical[1], 30, "b")
    post_depth = chain.depth_of(deposit_header)
    print("post-reorg depth of deposit header = %d (orphaned)" % post_depth)

    if minted and post_depth == -1:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] tokens minted on sub-finality header now orphaned -> unbacked mint")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[safe: finality enforced]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* stdout shows 'MINT issued ... at depth=2', then 'post-reorg depth ... = -1 (orphaned)', then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' with exit 0 -- proving the bridge minted destination tokens backed by a source header that the reorg removed, i.e. an unbacked mint when finality depth is not enforced.  
*Cleanup:* rm -f bridge_reorg.py (no node started).  
*Safety:* Local-only simulation of header-chain logic; no real bridge, RPC, funds, or keys involved.

**Research hook.** Build a cross-chain finality reconciler that tracks every bridge mint and watches the source chain for reorgs/finality reversions that orphan the proving block, raising a clawback alarm; model the economic conditions under which renting hashpower or stalling a finality gadget makes a reorg-after-mint profitable against named light-client bridges, and prototype dynamic confirmation depth keyed to live reorg depth.


## 76. Post-revocation credential acceptance during status-list propagation lag

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH6-did-credential-status-staleness-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Blockchain / Smart Contract + Identity & Federation → Identity & Federation + Web / Fetch |
| **Category** | Identity & Federation Attacks; Cryptography & PKI |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A verifier of a W3C verifiable credential trusts that checking the issuer's signature plus the referenced status mechanism (StatusList2021 bitstring, on-chain revocation registry, did:method resolution) tells it the credential is currently valid, so a credential that resolves and whose status bit reads 'active' is accepted as a present-tense attestation of the holder's standing.

**The attack.** An attacker exploits the propagation and resolution latency in the decentralized status layer — presenting a credential after revocation but before the status list/registry the verifier caches has updated, or steering the verifier to a stale did:method resolution or an outdated status-list mirror — so a revoked or rotated credential is honored as active, because in a decentralized identity system there is no synchronous authority that can answer 'is this valid right now' and the verifier silently trusts a snapshot that lags the true state.

**Mechanism (technical).** A verifier of a W3C Verifiable Credential checks the issuer signature plus a referenced status mechanism - a Bitstring/StatusList credential (a compressed bitstring where each credential maps to one bit: 1=revoked), an on-chain revocation registry, or did:method resolution. Validity is read from a snapshot the verifier fetches and typically caches, but a decentralized identity system has no synchronous authority that answers 'is this valid right now': status lists are published/mirrored with latency, registries propagate across nodes, and DID document resolution can return cached or stale results. An attacker presents a credential after revocation but before the cached status list/registry/DID document the verifier sees reflects it - or steers the verifier to a stale mirror - so a revoked or rotated credential is honored as currently active. The verifier trusts a lagging snapshot as present-tense truth.

**Exploitation primitive.** Present a recently-revoked or key-rotated credential during the propagation window, or against a verifier pinned to a cached/outdated status-list mirror or stale DID resolution, so the status bit still reads 'active' and access is granted.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK has no technique for decentralized-credential revocation lag or DID status staleness; no tactic models trust in an eventually-consistent status layer with no synchronous authority. The nearest analogues, T1606 Forge Web Credentials and T1550 Use Alternate Authentication Material, assume a forged or stolen token, whereas here a genuinely-issued-then-revoked credential is honored because the distributed status source the verifier consulted had not yet converged - an availability/consistency gap in decentralized PKI ATT&CK does not represent.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *standard:* W3C Bitstring Status List v1.0 (Recommendation) — Defines the bitstring revocation/suspension mechanism whose publish-and-cache model creates the propagation-latency window this technique exploits. — <https://www.w3.org/TR/vc-bitstring-status-list/>
- *standard:* W3C Verifiable Credentials Status List 2021 (predecessor draft) — Earlier StatusList2021 draft; notes it was experimental/under heavy development, underscoring immature, non-synchronous status resolution in deployed systems. — <https://www.w3.org/TR/2023/WD-vc-status-list-20230427/>

**Detection (technical).** Verifier-side: enforce a maximum status-list cache TTL and a freshness/validUntil check on fetched status credentials; reject or step-up when the status list's issuance/refresh timestamp is older than a policy threshold. Fetch status from the issuer's canonical endpoint (not an arbitrary mirror) and pin the DID document version; log and alert when a credential validated against a status snapshot that is later found to have been stale (post-hoc reconciliation against the issuer's authoritative current list).

**References.** [W3C Bitstring Status List v1.0](https://www.w3.org/TR/vc-bitstring-status-list/) · [W3C Verifiable Credentials Status List v2021 (WD)](https://www.w3.org/TR/2023/WD-vc-status-list-20230427/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Decentralized identity (DIDs and W3C verifiable credentials) is moving from pilots to real deployment in 2025-2027 (EU digital identity wallets, reusable KYC, employment/age credentials), and the verification model deliberately removes the central authority that PKI/OCSP-style systems queried for live status. The seam is that revocation in this world is eventually-consistent: status lists are published artifacts that verifiers fetch and cache, did:method resolution can be cached or steered, and there is no synchronous 'check now' — so the window between revocation and propagation is a designed-in feature, not a bug, that an attacker times against. The violated trust is the provenance/freshness of the status answer the verifier relies on. This is distinct from classic cert-revocation-latency because the status authority is itself decentralized and the verifier often cannot even reach a canonical current source. No DID wallet/verifier tooling measures or bounds status freshness, and no detection flags credentials presented during their revocation-propagation window.

**How to validate it.** Set up a VC verifier using StatusList2021 (or an on-chain revocation registry) that caches the status list. Issue a credential, revoke it at the issuer, then present the credential to the verifier within the cache TTL / before the status list mirror updates. Observe whether the verifier accepts the revoked credential as active. Vary cache TTL and force resolution to a stale mirror to measure the acceptance window.

**Refuted if.** The verifier fetches a fresh, signed, timestamped status list on every verification (no stale cache) or uses a freshness/'valid as of' bound, so a credential revoked before presentation is reliably rejected; revocation propagation is effectively synchronous in the deployment.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to present a credential during the propagation/cache window, or to steer the verifier to a stale status-list mirror / did resolution. Window may be short.

**Existing controls.** Short status-list cache TTLs, signed and freshness-bounded status lists, OCSP-stapling-style freshness, online status checks. Revocation-propagation lag is a known general PKI/CRL problem; specific VC StatusList exploitation is plausible, not widely demonstrated.

**Proof-of-concept — DID/VC verifier honors cached status after revocation (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/local-testnet only, signed RoE. Throwaway keys, no mainnet/real funds.

*Setup:* Offline simulation of a Verifiable Credential verifier with a status-list cache (W3C StatusList2021-style). Save as did_staleness.py and run: python3 did_staleness.py . Models an issuer revoking a credential (flipping its status-list bit) while the verifier serves a TTL-cached status list, so a revoked credential still verifies as valid.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH6 DID/VC credential-status staleness -- AUTHORIZED LAB / OFFLINE SIM ONLY.
# Trust boundary: a verifier must check LIVE revocation status. A verifier that trusts
# a TTL-cached status list keeps accepting a credential after the issuer revokes it.
# No keys/chain -- models the status-list bit + cache.
import sys, time

class StatusList:
    def __init__(self, size=64):
        self.bits = [0]*size           # 0 = valid, 1 = revoked
        self.version = 1
    def revoke(self, idx):
        self.bits[idx] = 1
        self.version += 1

class Verifier:
    def __init__(self, issuer_list, ttl=3600):
        self.issuer = issuer_list
        self.ttl = ttl
        self.cache = None
        self.cache_at = -1
    def _status_list(self, now):
        # THE SEAM: refresh only when TTL expires; otherwise serve stale snapshot.
        if self.cache is None or now - self.cache_at >= self.ttl:
            self.cache = list(self.issuer.bits)
            self.cache_at = now
        return self.cache
    def verify(self, cred_idx, now):
        revoked = self._status_list(now)[cred_idx] == 1
        return (not revoked)

def main():
    issuer = StatusList()
    idx = 7
    v = Verifier(issuer, ttl=3600)

    now = 1000
    print("t=%d verify (pre-revoke):" % now, v.verify(idx, now))   # caches valid

    # Issuer revokes the credential.
    issuer.revoke(idx)
    print("issuer revoked credential idx=%d (list version=%d)" % (idx, issuer.version))

    now = 1500                       # still inside the 3600s TTL window
    accepted_stale = v.verify(idx, now)
    print("t=%d verify (post-revoke, within TTL):" % now, accepted_stale)

    now = 1000 + 3600 + 1            # after TTL the cache refreshes -> correct reject
    correct_after_ttl = v.verify(idx, now)
    print("t=%d verify (after TTL refresh):" % now, correct_after_ttl)

    if accepted_stale and not correct_after_ttl:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] revoked credential still verified valid while status list was TTL-cached")
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[fresh status enforced]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* stdout shows pre-revoke verify True, 'issuer revoked ...', post-revoke-within-TTL verify True, after-TTL verify False, then '[SEAM CONFIRMED]' with exit 0 -- proving the verifier accepted a revoked credential because it trusted a stale TTL-cached status list rather than live revocation status.  
*Cleanup:* rm -f did_staleness.py (no node started).  
*Safety:* Local-only simulation of status-list + cache logic; no DID network, issuer endpoint, chain, or keys involved.

**Research hook.** Measure real-world status-propagation latency across StatusList2021 and on-chain revocation registries in shipping VC stacks, and build a verifier-side freshness bound that rejects or down-trusts credentials whose status snapshot is older than a policy threshold; demonstrate acceptance of a just-revoked credential by presenting it inside the propagation window against a caching verifier.


## 77. Validation/execution state divergence draining paymaster deposit

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH6-erc4337-paymaster-validation-execution-gap` |
| **Primitive** | P4 Context-Inheritance |
| **Trust frame** | Blockchain / Smart Contract → Blockchain / Smart Contract |
| **Category** | API & Business-Logic Abuse; Impact |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An ERC-4337 paymaster that agrees to sponsor a user operation's gas trusts that the conditions it checked in the validation phase — the userOp's calldata, the account's state, an off-chain signed sponsorship policy, or a token allowance it will pull as repayment — still hold when the operation executes, so it commits to paying gas based on the picture it validated.

**The attack.** An attacker exploits the structural separation between ERC-4337's validation and execution phases (and the bundler's freedom to order/aggregate userOps) so that state the paymaster relied on during validation is changed before or during execution — the repayment token is drained, the sponsored action is swapped via reentrant or ordering effects, or aggregated userOps interfere — leaving the paymaster to pay gas for an operation that no longer matches what it agreed to sponsor, draining its deposit at scale.

**Mechanism (technical).** ERC-4337 separates a UserOperation into a validation phase (account and paymaster check signatures/policy/allowances via validatePaymasterUserOp) and a later execution phase (the account's call and the paymaster's postOp). A paymaster commits to sponsoring gas based on state observed during validation - the userOp calldata, account state, an off-chain signed sponsorship policy, or an ERC-20 allowance it will pull as repayment. Because execution happens after validation and bundlers may order/aggregate many userOps, the relied-upon state can change before postOp runs: the repayment token's balance/allowance is drained, the sponsored action is altered via ordering/reentrancy effects, or aggregated ops interfere. The paymaster then pays gas for an operation that no longer matches what it validated, and at scale its EntryPoint deposit is drained. ERC-7562 storage/opcode rules and staking constrain mempool-level griefing but do not eliminate validation-time-vs-execution-time state divergence the paymaster economically depends on.

**Exploitation primitive.** Submit userOps that validate against a paymaster's token-repayment or policy check, then within the same bundle/execution change that state (drain the repayment-token balance/allowance, or reorder a reentrant action) so postOp cannot collect, forcing the paymaster to eat the gas - repeated to drain its deposit.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK has no technique for account-abstraction paymaster economics or validation/execution TOCTOU; no tactic models a gas-sponsorship contract paying for an operation whose preconditions lapsed between phases. The closest analogue is T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service (resource exhaustion), but that targets service availability via traffic, whereas this is a contract-protocol economic drain exploiting ERC-4337's phase separation and bundler ordering - an on-chain mechanism ATT&CK does not model.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *standard:* ERC-4337: Account Abstraction Using Alt Mempool — Defines the validation/execution phase separation, paymaster sponsorship, EntryPoint deposit, and postOp repayment - the structure this technique abuses. — <https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-4337>
- *standard:* ERC-7562: Validation rules for AA (mempool/storage-access rules) — Specifies storage/opcode restrictions and staking/reputation that bundlers enforce to limit paymaster griefing and mass invalidation; documents the boundary and its limits. — <https://docs.erc4337.io/core-standards/erc-7562.html>
- *research:* OpenZeppelin - ERC-4337 Account Abstraction Incremental Audit — Audit of reference AA contracts including paymaster validation/postOp gas accounting and griefing protections, evidencing the divergence risk class. — <https://blog.openzeppelin.com/erc-4337-account-abstraction-incremental-audit>

**Detection (technical).** On-chain: monitor a paymaster's EntryPoint deposit balance and alert on abnormal drain rate or on postOp reverts/failed repayments clustered from few accounts/factories. Correlate userOps where the repayment token's balanceOf/allowance for the sender dropped between the validation block-state and execution; flag bundles whose userOps share a sponsoring paymaster and mutate each other's relied-upon state. Off-chain: track per-sender sponsorship cost vs successful repayment ratio and rate-limit or de-sponsor senders whose execution-phase state repeatedly diverges from validation.

**References.** [ERC-4337: Account Abstraction Using Alt Mempool](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-4337) · [ERC-7562 - ERC-4337 Documentation](https://docs.erc4337.io/core-standards/erc-7562.html) · [OpenZeppelin - ERC-4337 Account Abstraction Incremental Audit](https://blog.openzeppelin.com/erc-4337-account-abstraction-incremental-audit)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Account abstraction via ERC-4337 is hitting production wallets and chains in 2025-2027, and paymasters that sponsor gas are central to the gasless UX everyone is building. The protocol deliberately splits a userOp into a validation phase (where the paymaster decides to pay) and an execution phase (where the action runs), with strict storage-access rules during validation that prevent the paymaster from fully foreseeing execution-time state — and bundlers can order and aggregate userOps between those phases. The seam is context-inheritance: the paymaster's commitment to pay inherits the validation-time context, but execution runs in a context the attacker can mutate (drain the repayment token, change what the sponsored call does, exploit ordering), so the paymaster pays for something other than what it vetted. This is a fresh AA-specific seam with essentially no tooling; the paymaster-trust hint names the actor but not this validation/execution-context divergence. No paymaster framework re-checks preconditions at execution, and no detection flags sponsored ops whose execution-time state contradicts validation-time assumptions.

**How to validate it.** Deploy an ERC-4337 paymaster that validates a token allowance / state condition in validatePaymasterUserOp and pulls repayment post-execution. Craft a userOp (or a bundle) where the repayment token is drained or the relied-on state is mutated between validation and execution (reentrancy, ordering, or aggregated-userOp interference), forcing the paymaster to pay gas for an op that no longer matches what it validated. Real if the paymaster deposit is drained because validation-time state did not hold at execution.

**Refuted if.** The paymaster follows ERC-4337 validation rules (no reliance on mutable external state it cannot re-check, postOp re-verification, allowance pulled within validation context, and bundler simulation/storage-access restrictions per ERC-7562) so divergence is prevented or the op reverts without charging.

**Prerequisites.** A paymaster that relies in validation on state mutable before execution and pulls repayment without postOp safeguards; attacker can submit crafted userOps or influence bundling.

**Existing controls.** ERC-4337/ERC-7562 validation rules restricting storage access, postOp hook for repayment verification, and bundler simulation. Validation/execution divergence is a known, documented 4337 design hazard with audit guidance; specific paymaster-drain PoCs exist in research/CTF contexts.

**Proof-of-concept — ERC-4337 paymaster: validation-phase check invalid at execution (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/local-testnet only, signed RoE. Throwaway keys, no mainnet/real funds.

*Setup:* Pure-logic simulation of an ERC-4337 EntryPoint flow (validatePaymasterUserOp -> execution -> postOp). Save as aa_paymaster.py and run: python3 aa_paymaster.py . Models a paymaster that approves sponsorship during the validation phase based on a condition (e.g., an allowance/whitelist flag or balance) that the UserOp's own execution then invalidates before postOp, so the paymaster pays for an op it would have rejected.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH6 ERC-4337 paymaster validation/execution gap -- AUTHORIZED LAB / OFFLINE SIM.
# Trust boundary: a paymaster decides to sponsor in validatePaymasterUserOp (validation
# phase), but the decision rests on state the UserOp's execution can mutate before postOp.
# Result: paymaster commits gas/funds for an op whose sponsorship precondition is false
# by execution time. No chain/keys -- models the EntryPoint phase ordering.
import sys

class World:
    def __init__(self):
        # paymaster sponsors only accounts flagged eligible (e.g., holds a sponsor NFT/allowance)
        self.eligible = {"acct": True}
        self.paymaster_deposit = 100
        self.sponsored = []

class Paymaster:
    def __init__(self, w): self.w = w
    def validate(self, op):
        # VALIDATION PHASE: check sponsorship precondition against current state.
        if self.w.eligible.get(op["sender"], False):
            return {"sponsor": True, "max_cost": op["max_cost"]}
        return {"sponsor": False}
    def post_op(self, op, ctx, actual_cost):
        # POST-OP: paymaster pays. THE SEAM: it does NOT re-check eligibility here,
        # and execution already revoked it.
        self.w.paymaster_deposit -= actual_cost
        self.w.sponsored.append((op["sender"], actual_cost))

def entrypoint_handle(op, pm, w):
    ctx = pm.validate(op)                      # 1. validation phase
    if not ctx.get("sponsor"):
        print("validation: NOT sponsored"); return False
    print("validation: sponsored (eligible=%s)" % w.eligible[op["sender"]])
    # 2. EXECUTION PHASE: the op's own calldata revokes its eligibility.
    if op["calldata"] == "revoke_self_eligibility":
        w.eligible[op["sender"]] = False
    # 3. postOp -- paymaster pays based on the stale validation decision.
    pm.post_op(op, ctx, op["max_cost"])
    print("execution done; eligible now=%s ; paymaster paid=%d" %
          (w.eligible[op["sender"]], op["max_cost"]))
    return True

def main():
    w = World(); pm = Paymaster(w)
    op = {"sender":"acct", "calldata":"revoke_self_eligibility", "max_cost":40}
    ok = entrypoint_handle(op, pm, w)
    paid = w.sponsored and w.sponsored[0][1] > 0
    if ok and paid and not w.eligible["acct"]:
        print("[SEAM CONFIRMED] paymaster paid %d for an op whose sponsorship precondition was revoked during execution" % w.sponsored[0][1])
        sys.exit(0)
    print("[validation re-checked at postOp]"); sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

*Success:* stdout shows 'validation: sponsored (eligible=True)', 'execution done; eligible now=False ; paymaster paid=40', then '[SEAM CONFIRMED] paymaster paid 40 ...' with exit 0 -- proving the paymaster committed funds based on a validation-phase eligibility check that the UserOp's execution invalidated before postOp.  
*Cleanup:* rm -f aa_paymaster.py (no node started).  
*Safety:* Local-only simulation of ERC-4337 EntryPoint phase ordering; no bundler, chain, paymaster deposit, or keys touched.

**Research hook.** Enumerate paymaster designs and identify which sponsorship preconditions can be invalidated between validation and execution under hostile bundler ordering and reentrancy, then build a paymaster-side guard that re-asserts critical invariants at execution start and a detector for sponsored ops whose repayment or sponsored action diverged; demonstrate deposit drain by mutating the repayment-token balance after validation.


---

# PNT / space / cyber-physical  *(10)*

## 78. Coherent capture-and-drag GNSS trajectory injection

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH7-gnss-position-spoof-autonomy-ground-truth` |
| **Primitive** | P1 Data->Control |
| **Trust frame** | PNT / GNSS / Space-Ground → Automotive / CAN + OT / ICS / SCADA |
| **Category** | Wireless / RF Attacks; OT / ICS Attacks; Impact |
| **Validity** | demonstrated (high confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / nascent / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** An autonomous platform's navigation/guidance stack (drone flight controller, ag/mining autonomy, maritime ECDIS autopilot, last-mile robot) trusts the GNSS fix as the absolute ground-truth anchor that corrects its inertial dead-reckoning, so a plausibly-continuous position stream is fused into the state estimator and converted directly into actuation (waypoint, heading, geofence, return-to-home) without the fix ever competing against an independent absolute reference.

**The attack.** An attacker injects a coherent spoofed GNSS constellation that smoothly captures the receiver and then drags the reported position along an attacker-chosen trajectory consistent with vehicle dynamics, so the autonomy treats spoofed data as control input — steering the platform, triggering a false geofence breach, or commanding return-to-home toward attacker coordinates — because the state estimator weights the externally-supplied fix as truth over its own physics.

**Mechanism (technical).** An autonomous platform fuses the GNSS position fix as the absolute ground-truth anchor that bounds inertial dead-reckoning, converting the fused state directly into actuation (waypoint, heading, geofence, return-to-home). A coherent spoofer first matches and slightly raises power on the genuine signals to capture the receiver's tracking loops without a position jump, then smoothly drags the reported position along an attacker-chosen, dynamics-consistent trajectory. Because the spoofed constellation is internally consistent and the drag respects vehicle dynamics, the state estimator weights it as truth over its own IMU physics, and the autonomy steers, breaches a false geofence, or returns-to-home toward attacker coordinates. This capture-and-drag technique was publicly demonstrated against a superyacht (UT Austin) and is the mechanism widely attributed to drone-redirection events.

**Exploitation primitive.** Coherently capture the receiver's tracking loops (power-matched takeover) then incrementally offset the spoofed position along a smooth trajectory, feeding the autonomy a false but plausible ground truth it actuates on.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise has no GNSS/navigation technique. ICS has no position-spoofing technique either; the closest conceptual ICS id is T0856 (Spoof Reporting Message), but that models falsified process-reporting on a control network, not an RF physical-layer spoof of the navigation sensor feeding an autonomous state estimator. No ATT&CK technique captures GNSS position spoofing of autonomy.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *incident:* UT Austin (Todd Humphreys) superyacht 'White Rose of Drachs' GPS spoofing, 2013 — First openly acknowledged civilian capture-and-drag spoof steering an $80M vessel onto a parallel track hundreds of meters off course while displays showed nominal. — <https://news.utexas.edu/2013/07/29/ut-austin-researchers-successfully-spoof-an-80-million-yacht-at-sea/>
- *incident:* Iran-U.S. RQ-170 Sentinel incident, 2011 (GPS-spoofing claim) — Iran claimed GPS spoofing was used to redirect the drone; U.S. assessment notes INS-primary navigation - cite as widely-reported claim, not confirmed mechanism. — <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93U.S._RQ-170_incident>

**Detection (technical).** Fuse GNSS against independent absolute references (vision/terrain-relative nav, multi-antenna direction-of-arrival, IMU consistency residuals) and alert when GNSS-vs-INS innovation exceeds dynamics bounds; use antenna-array anti-spoofing, signal-authentication (Galileo OSNMA), and AGC/C-N0 anomaly monitoring for power-matched takeover.

**References.** [UT Austin: Researchers Successfully Spoof an $80 million Yacht at Sea](https://news.utexas.edu/2013/07/29/ut-austin-researchers-successfully-spoof-an-80-million-yacht-at-sea/) · [Inside GNSS: GPS Spoofing Experiment Knocks Ship off Course](https://insidegnss.com/gps-spoofing-experiment-knocks-ship-off-course/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** GNSS position spoofing is demonstrated in the literature, but the frontier seam for 2025-2027 is the data->control coupling inside increasingly autonomous platforms where the fix is not merely displayed to a human but fed directly into a closed-loop estimator that actuates. The trust violated is that an externally-radiated position is ground truth that should dominate the platform's own inertial physics; a dynamics-consistent capture-and-drag defeats the cheap consistency checks (jump detection, signal-strength) most COTS receivers ship. This differs from manual-navigation spoofing because the victim is an automated control loop with machine-speed reaction and no human sanity check, and AI-driven trajectory synthesis makes the drag arbitrarily plausible. No COTS autonomy stack cross-validates GNSS against independent absolute references (vision/terrain/RF-ranging) as an authority arbiter rather than a smoother, and there is no detection that distinguishes a physics-consistent slow drag from real motion.

**How to validate it.** In an RF-shielded lab (or with a licensed/contained SDR like HackRF/USRP running GPS-SDR-SIM/gps-sdr-sim-style coherent spoofer), capture-and-drag a target receiver's position smoothly along an attacker trajectory consistent with vehicle dynamics. Feed the receiver into an autonomy/state estimator and observe whether it actuates on the spoofed fix (heading change, false geofence breach, RTH toward attacker coordinates) without rejecting the fix.

**Refuted if.** The platform fuses an independent absolute reference or anti-spoof signal (multi-antenna/CRPA angle-of-arrival, RAIM, signal-quality/AGC anomaly detection, INS innovation gating) and rejects or down-weights the spoofed fix so actuation does not follow it.

**Prerequisites.** RF transmit capability and (lawfully) a shielded environment; a target autonomy stack that fuses GNSS as ground truth without independent cross-checks. Coherent capture-and-drag requires moderate sophistication.

**Existing controls.** RAIM, multi-constellation/multi-frequency, antenna-array anti-spoofing (CRPA), INS/visual cross-checks, signal authentication (Galileo OSNMA). GNSS spoofing including capture-and-drag is publicly demonstrated (academic and real-world maritime/UAV incidents).

**Proof-of-concept — Gradual GNSS position-drag is accepted as autonomy ground truth (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real spectrum/satellite/aircraft/ship/grid/network; conducted/shielded only.

*Setup:* Pure python model of an autonomy PVT consumer that fuses GNSS fixes as ground truth with a simple innovation gate. No RF, no receiver, no vehicle. A spoof injects a slow per-fix position drag. (Bench variant in comments: GNSS SIMULATOR -> receiver in a SHIELDED enclosure.) Models the seam: if GNSS is trusted as ground truth and only large per-step jumps are gated, a slow drag steers the estimated position arbitrarily far.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH7 GNSS position-spoof as autonomy ground truth -- OFFLINE SIM ONLY.
# No RF, no receiver, no vehicle, no transmit. Pure estimator model.
# BENCH VARIANT (documented, do NOT radiate): GNSS SIGNAL SIMULATOR -> receiver
# over CONDUCTED COAX in a SHIELDED Faraday enclosure under signed RoE.
#
# Trust boundary: an autonomy stack that treats the GNSS fix as ground truth
# and only rejects large single-step jumps will accept a slow positional drag,
# integrating it into the state estimate and steering the platform off course.

def estimator(reported_track, jump_gate_m=5.0, alpha=0.5):
    est = [0.0, 0.0]
    last = [0.0, 0.0]
    trace = []
    for fix in reported_track:
        step = ((fix[0]-last[0])**2 + (fix[1]-last[1])**2) ** 0.5
        if step > jump_gate_m:          # coarse jump -> reject this fix
            fix = last
        est[0] += alpha*(fix[0]-est[0])
        est[1] += alpha*(fix[1]-est[1])
        last = fix
        trace.append((round(est[0],2), round(est[1],2)))
    return trace

N = 400
true_track  = [(0.0, 0.0)]*N
# Spoof: drag +1.0 m east per fix (< 5 m gate) -> ~400 m total displacement.
spoof_track = [(1.0*i, 0.0) for i in range(N)]

print("honest final estimate:", estimator(true_track)[-1])
sp = estimator(spoof_track)[-1]
print("spoof  final estimate:", sp)
assert sp[0] > 100, "expected large accumulated drift"
print("SEAM: estimated position dragged ~%.0f m east by sub-gate drift; gate never tripped." % sp[0])
```

*Success:* The estimator's spoofed run drifts hundreds of metres east (>100 m) while the per-step jump gate is never tripped, versus ~0 for the honest stationary run. Confirms a slow GNSS position-drag is accepted as ground truth in-sim. (Bench: receiver position readout walks while simulator drags, inside the shielded enclosure.)  
*Cleanup:* No persistent state; exit interpreter. Bench: stop simulator, power down receiver, RF terminated.  
*Safety:* Offline estimator simulation; bench variant is conducted/shielded GNSS simulator only, no radiated or live signal.

**Research hook.** Prototype an authority-arbitration layer that demotes GNSS from ground truth to one vote among vision/terrain-matching/multi-antenna-bearing and refuses actuation when the fix diverges from the inertial-only prediction beyond a dynamics bound; demonstrate capture-and-drag against an open-source flight controller and measure the divergence before any onboard alarm.


## 79. Unauthenticated AIS phantom-vessel and MMSI-clone injection

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH7-ais-broadcast-position-as-truth` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Wireless / RF + PNT / GNSS / Space-Ground → OT / ICS / SCADA + Network |
| **Category** | Wireless / RF Attacks; Impact; Defense Evasion |
| **Validity** | demonstrated (high confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** A Vessel Traffic Service, port-management system, collision-avoidance display, and maritime-domain-awareness analytics platform trusts that an AIS message carrying an MMSI, position, course, and identity was broadcast by the real vessel it names, so it ingests AIS as authoritative real-time ground truth for traffic deconfliction, automated routing, and sanctions/anomaly analytics without any cryptographic origin binding.

**The attack.** An attacker transmits or replays unauthenticated AIS frames over VHF (or injects via a compromised AIS aggregator/feed) to fabricate phantom vessels, clone a real MMSI to a decoy track, or erase a ship's presence, and the VTS/MDA stack treats the spoofed broadcasts as genuine positions — corrupting collision-avoidance, triggering false port/safety responses, or laundering a vessel's true movements past sanctions analytics.

**Mechanism (technical).** AIS broadcasts vessel MMSI, position, course and identity over VHF (161.975/162.025 MHz) with no cryptographic origin binding or integrity - the protocol has never had authentication. Vessel Traffic Services, port management, collision-avoidance displays, and maritime-domain-awareness analytics ingest AIS as authoritative real-time ground truth. An attacker with a VHF/SDR transmitter (or access to a compromised AIS aggregator feed) can inject or replay frames to fabricate phantom vessels, clone a real MMSI onto a decoy track, or erase a ship's presence, and the consuming stack accepts the spoofed broadcasts as genuine positions. This corrupts deconfliction, triggers false safety responses, or launders true movements past sanctions analytics; AIS spoofing incidents surged sharply in 2022-2023.

**Exploitation primitive.** Transmit or replay unauthenticated AIS frames over VHF (or via a compromised aggregator) to create phantom vessels, MMSI-clone decoys, or vanish a real track in the VTS/MDA picture.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise has no maritime/AIS technique. ICS has no AIS technique; the loosest analog ICS T0856 (Spoof Reporting Message) covers falsified process reporting on a control network, not RF broadcast injection of unauthenticated navigation data consumed by VTS/MDA. No ATT&CK technique covers AIS spoofing.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Balduzzi, Pasta, Wilhoit (Trend Micro), 'A Security Evaluation of AIS' (ACSAC 2014) — Foundational demonstration that AIS is unauthenticated and that vessels, phantom ships, and tracks can be spoofed/injected over RF and via providers. — <https://www.trendmicro.com/en_us/research/14/d/the-dangers-of-hacking-radio-frequency-protocols.html>
- *incident:* AIS spoofing surge 2022-2023 (Pole Star Global / Windward analysis) — Documented ~400% rise in AIS spoofing incidents Oct 2022-Apr 2023, including MMSI-cloning and location-obscuring used to evade sanctions analytics. — <https://www.polestarglobal.com/resources/ais-spoofing/>
- *research:* 'AIS Data Vulnerability Indicated by a Spoofing Case-Study' (Applied Sciences, MDPI 2021) — Peer-reviewed case study of AIS spoofing impact on traffic picture. — <https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/11/11/5015>

**Detection (technical).** Cross-validate AIS against independent sensors (coastal/space-based radar, satellite RF geolocation, position-vs-kinematic plausibility), flag MMSI/position/kinematic inconsistencies and impossible jumps, and correlate frequency-of-arrival of satellite AIS messages to detect spoofed transmitters.

**References.** [Pole Star Global: AIS Spoofing Research - 4 Main Typologies](https://www.polestarglobal.com/resources/ais-spoofing/) · [MDPI: AIS Data Vulnerability Indicated by a Spoofing Case-Study](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/11/11/5015)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** AIS has no authentication by design and 2025-2027 maritime operations increasingly automate on AIS feeds (autonomous-assist collision avoidance, sanctions and dark-fleet analytics, port digital twins), so the broadcast-as-truth assumption now feeds machine decisions, not just a watchstander's screen. The trust violated is provenance: the consumer believes an MMSI identifies the vessel that sent it, when any SDR can forge frames and aggregator APIs can be poisoned upstream. This is an emerging seam rather than mature classic spoofing because the novel exposure is the analytics/automation layer that fuses AIS as ground truth, and AI-driven track synthesis makes phantom fleets statistically plausible. Detection is only nascent (kinematic/RF-fingerprint anomaly research), there is no cryptographic AIS origin binding deployed at scale, and ATT&CK does not model maritime broadcast trust.

**How to validate it.** Using an SDR transmitter on the AIS VHF channels (in a controlled/shielded RF environment to stay legal), craft and emit unauthenticated AIS frames: a phantom-vessel MMSI/position, a clone of a real MMSI to a decoy track, or frames erasing a ship's presence. Action: feed a VTS/MDA ingest stack (or an AIS decoder/aggregator) the frames and observe whether it renders the phantom/clone as a genuine track with no origin validation. (Alternatively inject via a compromised AIS aggregator feed.)

**Refuted if.** The VTS/MDA stack cross-validates AIS against independent sources (radar, satellite, RF geolocation) and flags/rejects positions lacking corroboration or with impossible kinematics — so spoofed broadcasts do not become trusted ground truth.

**Prerequisites.** AIS-band SDR transmit capability (or access to inject into an AIS aggregator feed), and a downstream system that ingests AIS as authoritative without corroboration. AIS lacks any authentication by design; spoofing is publicly demonstrated.

**Existing controls.** Radar/AIS fusion, satellite-AIS cross-checks, RF direction-finding, kinematic plausibility checks, anomaly analytics in MDA platforms. AIS spoofing is well documented (research and observed real-world spoofing of vessel tracks); the lack of cryptographic origin binding is inherent to the standard. Detection via fusion/anomaly analytics is nascent-to-partial.

**Proof-of-concept — Synthetic AIS message decoded into a phantom track in a VTS fusion stub (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real spectrum/satellite/aircraft/ship/grid/network; conducted/shielded only.

*Setup:* Pure python. Generate a synthetic AIS Type-1 position-report payload IN MEMORY, decode it with a software AIS decoder (RECEIVE-ONLY path), and feed it to a stub VTS/fusion that trusts broadcast position. No radio, never transmitted. Models the seam: AIS is unauthenticated, so a decoder + fusion that treat any received report as truth will plot a phantom vessel.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH7 AIS broadcast-position-as-truth -- OFFLINE SIM / RECEIVE-ONLY DECODE.
# A synthetic AIS payload is built in memory and DECODED (never broadcast,
# no radio, no transmit). Then fed to a stub VTS fusion.
# Trust boundary: AIS Type-1 reports are unauthenticated; a system that plots
# any decoded report as a real vessel will show a phantom track.

def encode_ais_type1(mmsi, lat, lon):
    # Build the 168-bit AIS Type-1 payload as a bitstring (subset of fields).
    def bits(val, n): return format(val & ((1<<n)-1), '0%db' % n)
    lat_i = int(round(lat * 600000)) & ((1<<27)-1)
    lon_i = int(round(lon * 600000)) & ((1<<28)-1)
    b  = bits(1,6) + bits(0,2) + bits(mmsi,30) + bits(0,4) + bits(0,8)
    b += bits(0,2) + bits(0,3) + bits(0,1) + bits(lon_i,28) + bits(lat_i,27)
    b += bits(0,12)+ bits(0,9) + bits(0,6) + bits(0,2) + bits(0,3)
    b += bits(0,1) + bits(0,19)
    return b[:168]

def decode_ais_type1(b):
    def u(s,n,off): return int(s[off:off+n],2), off+n
    msgtype,o = u(b,6,0); _,o = u(b,2,o); mmsi,o = u(b,30,o)
    _,o = u(b,4,o); _,o = u(b,8,o); _,o = u(b,2,o); _,o = u(b,3,o); _,o = u(b,1,o)
    lon_i,o = u(b,28,o); lat_i,o = u(b,27,o)
    if lon_i & (1<<27): lon_i -= (1<<28)   # sign-extend
    if lat_i & (1<<26): lat_i -= (1<<27)
    return {"type":msgtype,"mmsi":mmsi,"lon":lon_i/600000.0,"lat":lat_i/600000.0}

class VTSFusion:
    def __init__(self): self.tracks = {}
    def ingest(self, rpt):
        # SEAM: trust decoded position as a real vessel.
        self.tracks[rpt["mmsi"]] = (rpt["lat"], rpt["lon"])
        return self.tracks[rpt["mmsi"]]
    def ingest_corroborated(self, rpt, radar_hit):
        # DEFENSE: require an independent sensor (radar/coastal) corroboration.
        if not radar_hit: return ("UNCORROBORATED", rpt["mmsi"])
        self.tracks[rpt["mmsi"]] = (rpt["lat"], rpt["lon"]); return ("PLOTTED",)

PHANTOM_MMSI = 999000123
payload = encode_ais_type1(PHANTOM_MMSI, 37.8000, -122.4000)
rpt = decode_ais_type1(payload)
print("decoded (receive-only):", rpt)

vts = VTSFusion()
print("phantom track plotted at:", vts.ingest(rpt), "  <-- SEAM: unauthenticated AIS = truth")
print("corroboration-required (no radar):", vts.ingest_corroborated(rpt, radar_hit=False))
```

*Success:* The software decoder recovers the synthetic MMSI and lat/lon, and the trusting VTSFusion plots a phantom track at the spoofed coordinates; the corroboration-required path returns UNCORROBORATED without an independent radar hit. Confirms unauthenticated AIS is accepted as truth in-sim, with no transmission.  
*Cleanup:* No persistent state; exit interpreter.  
*Safety:* Receive-only decode of an in-memory synthetic AIS message; no radio, antenna, or transmission.

**Research hook.** Build a cross-source AIS validator that fuses terrestrial-VHF receive-fingerprint, satellite-AIS, and radar/EO tracks to score each MMSI track's physical plausibility and flag clones/phantoms, and red-team an MDA analytics pipeline by injecting dynamics-consistent dark-fleet tracks to measure false-clearance of sanctions screens.


## 80. Ghost-aircraft ADS-B squitter injection into fusion

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH7-adsb-datalink-track-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Wireless / RF + PNT / GNSS / Space-Ground → OT / ICS / SCADA + Network |
| **Category** | Wireless / RF Attacks; Impact |
| **Validity** | demonstrated (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | none — unmodeled by ATT&CK |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** An air-traffic surveillance fusion system, UTM/drone-traffic service, and increasingly an aircraft's own ADS-B-In/TCAS display trusts that an ADS-B broadcast carrying an ICAO address and GNSS-derived position came from the aircraft it identifies, so it fuses the squitter into the surveillance picture and conflict-detection logic as a real cooperative target without origin authentication.

**The attack.** An attacker transmits unauthenticated 1090ES/UAT ADS-B squitters to inject ghost aircraft, spoof an ICAO identity onto a false track, or shift a reported position, and the surveillance fusion or UTM deconfliction logic treats the fabricated targets as genuine cooperative traffic — generating spurious conflict alerts, distorting the drone-traffic picture, or masking a real intruder by saturating the airspace model.

**Mechanism (technical).** ADS-B broadcasts an aircraft's ICAO 24-bit address and GNSS-derived position over 1090 MHz Extended Squitter (1090ES) or 978 MHz UAT with no message authentication or integrity. Air-traffic surveillance fusion, UTM/drone-traffic services, and aircraft ADS-B-In/TCAS displays fuse received squitters into the surveillance picture and conflict-detection logic as genuine cooperative targets. An attacker with a low-cost SDR (USRP-class) can encode and transmit valid 1090ES/UAT frames to inject ghost aircraft, spoof an ICAO identity onto a false track, or shift a reported position, generating spurious conflict alerts, distorting the drone-traffic picture, or masking a real intruder by saturating the model. Costin and Francillon publicly demonstrated practical ADS-B injection at Black Hat USA 2012.

**Exploitation primitive.** Encode and transmit unauthenticated 1090ES/UAT squitters with an SDR to inject ghost aircraft or spoof an ICAO/track into ATC, TCAS, or UTM fusion as real cooperative traffic.

**MITRE gap.** ATT&CK Enterprise has no aviation/ADS-B technique. ICS has none either; the loosest analog ICS T0856 (Spoof Reporting Message) models control-network reporting spoofing, not RF broadcast injection of unauthenticated air-surveillance data into ATC/UTM fusion. No ATT&CK technique covers ADS-B spoofing.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Costin & Francillon, 'Ghost in the Air(Traffic): On insecurity of ADS-B protocol and practical attacks' (Black Hat USA 2012) — First public implementation of ADS-B message injection/spoofing with USRP SDR, exploiting the lack of integrity and authentication in 1090ES. — <https://www.s3.eurecom.fr/docs/bh12us_costin.pdf>
- *research:* 'Detecting ADS-B Spoofing Attacks using Deep Neural Networks' (arXiv:1904.09969) — Confirms ADS-B's lack of authentication and treats spoofed-squitter detection as an open ML problem. — <https://arxiv.org/pdf/1904.09969>

**Detection (technical).** Cross-validate ADS-B against independent surveillance (primary/secondary radar, multilateration time-difference-of-arrival, received-signal-strength/AoA plausibility) and kinematic feasibility; flag tracks with no MLAT corroboration or implausible position/velocity, and alert on duplicate/teleporting ICAO addresses.

**References.** [Ghost in the Air(Traffic) - Costin & Francillon (Black Hat 2012)](https://www.s3.eurecom.fr/docs/bh12us_costin.pdf) · [Detecting ADS-B Spoofing Attacks using Deep Neural Networks (arXiv 1904.09969)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1904.09969)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** ADS-B carries no authentication and the 2025-2027 frontier is its expansion into UAS Traffic Management and automated detect-and-avoid, where ghost targets feed deconfliction algorithms that command drone maneuvers rather than merely informing a controller. The trust violated is provenance: a GNSS-sourced position relayed over an open datalink is accepted as the named aircraft's true state. This is emerging rather than purely classic because the consumer shifting from human controllers (who apply multilateration cross-checks) to automated UTM logic that may trust the broadcast directly is the new exposure, compounded by GNSS-spoofing feeding falsified positions into otherwise-genuine transponders. Multilateration/RF-fingerprint detection is only partially deployed, there is no authenticated ADS-B at scale, and UTM-layer trust of cooperative broadcasts is unmodeled.

**How to validate it.** In an RF lab (or with a controlled receiver), transmit crafted unauthenticated 1090ES/UAT ADS-B squitters with a fabricated ICAO address and GNSS position into a surveillance/UTM fusion or ADS-B-In receiver, and observe whether ghost aircraft appear in the picture and trigger conflict/deconfliction logic as genuine cooperative targets.

**Refuted if.** The fusion system rejects the injected tracks via multilateration/radar correlation, plausibility/physics checks, or origin validation, so unauthenticated squitters do not enter the surveillance picture as real targets.

**Prerequisites.** SDR transmit capability on 1090/978 MHz (legal/regulatory constraint) and a target ADS-B receiver/fusion system or simulator.

**Existing controls.** ADS-B's lack of authentication and ghost-aircraft injection are well-documented in academic literature and DEF CON talks; defenses (MLAT cross-check, radar fusion, plausibility filtering, future ADS-B authentication proposals) exist. Mechanism is established.

**Proof-of-concept — Synthetic ADS-B (DF17) message decoded into a ghost track in a UTM stub (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real spectrum/satellite/aircraft/ship/grid/network; conducted/shielded only.

*Setup:* Pure python. Build a synthetic Mode-S/ADS-B DF17 airborne-position payload IN MEMORY, decode it with a software decoder (RECEIVE-ONLY), and feed to a stub UTM/ATC fusion. No radio, never transmitted. Models the seam: ADS-B is unauthenticated, so a fusion that trusts the downlinked track plots a ghost aircraft.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH7 ADS-B datalink track-trust -- OFFLINE SIM / RECEIVE-ONLY DECODE.
# Synthetic DF17 frame built and DECODED in memory; no radio, no transmit.
# Trust boundary: ADS-B position is unauthenticated; a UTM/ATC fusion that
# trusts the downlinked track will plot a non-existent ("ghost") aircraft.

def make_df17(icao, payload_tag):
    # Minimal synthetic DF17 frame: DF=17, CA=5, ICAO(24), then a 56-bit ME
    # field. We carry an opaque ident/altitude tag rather than full CPR for the
    # stub fusion; this is a logic model, not a transmitter.
    df_ca = (17 << 3) | 5
    frame = bytes([df_ca]) + icao.to_bytes(3, "big") + \
            (0x58).to_bytes(1,"big") + payload_tag.to_bytes(6, "big")
    return frame

def decode_df17(frame):
    df = frame[0] >> 3
    icao = int.from_bytes(frame[1:4], "big")
    me_type = frame[4] >> 3
    tag = int.from_bytes(frame[5:11], "big")
    return {"df": df, "icao": hex(icao), "me_type": me_type, "tag": tag}

class UTMFusion:
    def __init__(self): self.tracks = {}
    def ingest(self, msg):
        if msg["df"] != 17: return ("REJECT","not DF17")
        # SEAM: accept downlinked identity/track as real.
        self.tracks[msg["icao"]] = msg["tag"]
        return ("TRACK", msg["icao"])
    def ingest_mlat_checked(self, msg, mlat_consistent):
        # DEFENSE: cross-check against independent MLAT/radar geometry.
        if not mlat_consistent: return ("DROP_GHOST", msg["icao"])
        self.tracks[msg["icao"]] = msg["tag"]; return ("TRACK", msg["icao"])

GHOST_ICAO = 0xABCDEF
frame = make_df17(GHOST_ICAO, 0x010203040506)
msg = decode_df17(frame)
print("decoded (receive-only):", msg)

utm = UTMFusion()
print("ghost plotted:", utm.ingest(msg), "  <-- SEAM: unauthenticated ADS-B = truth")
print("MLAT-checked (inconsistent):", utm.ingest_mlat_checked(msg, mlat_consistent=False))
```

*Success:* The software decoder recovers DF=17 and the synthetic ICAO/track tag, and the trusting UTMFusion creates a ghost track; the MLAT-checked path returns DROP_GHOST when independent geometry doesn't corroborate. Confirms unauthenticated ADS-B is accepted as a track in-sim, with no transmission.  
*Cleanup:* No persistent state; exit interpreter.  
*Safety:* Receive-only decode of an in-memory synthetic ADS-B message; no radio, antenna, or transmission.

**Research hook.** Develop a UTM-side cross-validator that requires multilateration or multi-receiver time-difference-of-arrival agreement before a cooperative ADS-B target influences automated deconfliction, and quantify how injected ghost tracks alter detect-and-avoid maneuver commands in an open UTM simulator.


## 81. Directional delay injection skewing PTP offset

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH7-ptp-asymmetric-delay-offset-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P6 Time/State |
| **Trust frame** | Network → OT / ICS / SCADA + Cloud Control Plane + PNT / GNSS / Space-Ground |
| **Category** | Network Attacks; OT / ICS Attacks; Impact |
| **Validity** | demonstrated (high confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T0830, CWE-367, CWE-345 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / none |

**The trust assumption.** A PTP/NTP time client trusts that the network path to its time source is symmetric — that forward and reverse propagation delays are equal — so it computes its clock offset as half the round-trip and applies the result as authoritative time correction, having no way to independently measure one-way delay and therefore no way to detect that the symmetry assumption was violated.

**The attack.** An on-path attacker (transparent-clock relay, compromised switch, or queue-manipulating middlebox) introduces a controlled asymmetric delay — adding latency to only one direction — which the client folds into its offset calculation as a real time error and slews its clock by the injected asymmetry, so the consumer's notion of 'now' is shifted by an amount it cannot detect because authenticated PTP/NTP packets protect content but not path symmetry.

**Mechanism (technical).** PTP (IEEE 1588) and NTP estimate clock offset assuming a symmetric path: with forward and reverse delays equal, offset = ((t2-t1)-(t4-t3))/2, half the round-trip. The protocols can authenticate packet content (integrity/origin) but cannot measure one-way delay, so they cannot detect that path symmetry was violated. An on-path attacker - a transparent-clock relay, compromised switch, or queue-manipulating middlebox - adds latency to only one direction; the client folds the injected asymmetry directly into its offset and slews its clock by that amount, undetectably. In IEC 61850 substations this corrupts PMU/synchrophasor phase math (sub-microsecond budgets, C37.118 TVE limits); it is a demonstrated attack class on commercial PTP devices.

**Exploitation primitive.** From an on-path position, inject a controlled one-directional (asymmetric) delay into PTP/NTP exchanges so the client computes and applies a false offset, slewing its clock by the injected asymmetry while authentication still passes.

**MITRE gap.** Closest id is ICS T0830 (Adversary-in-the-Middle), tagged in input. It is insufficient because it generically describes intercept/modify/inject, while the asymmetric-delay attack injects no malformed content and modifies no packet - it only adds directional latency, which authenticated PTP (even with MACs) cannot detect because the trust violated is path symmetry, not content integrity. ATT&CK has no technique for time-protocol delay/asymmetry attacks specifically.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Moradi et al., 'PTPsec: Securing the Precision Time Protocol Against Time Delay Attacks Using Cyclic Path Asymmetry Analysis' (arXiv:2401.10664) — Demonstrates and defends against MITM asymmetric-delay attacks that authenticated PTP cannot detect. — <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.10664>
- *research:* 'Vulnerability of IEEE 1588 under Time Synchronization Attacks' — Hardware-testbed study of delay, packet-modification and spoofing attacks on commercial PTP devices. — <https://www.researchgate.net/publication/335129065_Vulnerability_of_IEEE_1588_under_Time_Synchronization_Attacks>
- *standard:* IEEE C37.118 / IEC 61850 PMU timing (Total Vector Error budget) — Sub-microsecond synchrophasor timing budget that asymmetric-delay slew can violate, corrupting phase-angle measurement. — <https://standards.ieee.org/ieee/C37.118.1/4902/>

**Detection (technical).** Deploy cyclic/round-trip path-asymmetry monitoring (e.g. PTPsec), redundant disjoint time paths with a trusted supervisor node that cross-checks per-slave offset/delay (IEEE 1588-2019), and one-way-delay anomaly alerts; cross-validate PTP-derived time against an independent local holdover oscillator.

**References.** [PTPsec (arXiv 2401.10664)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.10664) · [Vulnerability of IEEE 1588 under Time Synchronization Attacks](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/335129065_Vulnerability_of_IEEE_1588_under_Time_Synchronization_Attacks)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** As enterprises move off GNSS toward network-delivered PTP/NTP for resilient timing (telco fronthaul, exchanges, data-center fabrics), the under-modeled seam is that even fully authenticated PTP (with security TLVs) and NTS-secured NTP defend message integrity but cannot defend the half-round-trip symmetry assumption baked into offset math. The trust violated is temporal: the client believes its computed offset reflects real clock error when an attacker has injected directional delay it cannot observe. This is distinct from the GNSS-time gap (no satellite involved) and from spoofing/replay (packets are genuine and authenticated): the attacker manipulates only path timing. There is no deployed detection because one-way delay is not independently measurable on a single path, and SIEMs do not model per-direction latency asymmetry as a time-integrity signal.

**How to validate it.** On a PTP/NTP-synced lab segment, insert an on-path element (transparent-clock relay, programmable switch, or tc/netem middlebox) that adds latency to only one direction. Measure the client's computed offset and observe it slew its clock by ~half the injected one-way asymmetry. Confirm authenticated PTP (e.g. with integrity protection) still mis-syncs because the path-symmetry assumption, not packet content, is violated.

**Refuted if.** The client measures or bounds one-way delay independently (multi-path/redundant time sources, two-way symmetric verified paths, GNSS cross-check) and rejects or corrects the asymmetric error, so the injected delay does not shift its clock undetectably.

**Prerequisites.** On-path position to introduce directional delay (compromised switch, transparent clock, or inline middlebox).

**Existing controls.** Redundant/diverse time sources, GNSS or holdover cross-check, asymmetry monitoring, hardware timestamping with calibrated paths, multiple PTP grandmasters. PTP asymmetric-delay attacks are well established in academic literature and demonstrated in testbeds.

**Proof-of-concept — Asymmetric path delay yields an attacker-chosen PTP offset (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized bench/lab only, signed RoE. No real spectrum/satellite/aircraft/ship/grid/network; conducted/shielded only.

*Setup:* Pure python model of an IEEE-1588 PTP delay-request/response exchange feeding a clock servo. Synthetic timestamps only; no network, no PTP hardware. Models the seam: PTP offset math assumes symmetric forward/reverse path delay; an on-path adversary that adds delay to only one direction injects a deterministic, attacker-chosen offset that the servo applies.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH7 PTP asymmetric-delay offset-trust -- OFFLINE SIM ONLY.
# Synthetic timestamps; no network, no PTP daemon, no hardware clock.
```
