**The attack.** An attacker who compromises one low-sensitivity integration (or its long-lived token) chains it against a second sanctioned integration in the same tenant - reading sensitive records through App A and pushing them out through App B's outbound scope - so the combined capability exceeds any single consent screen the tenant ever approved, and because every hop uses a valid, sanctioned token the lateral movement is indistinguishable from normal SaaS-to-SaaS automation traffic.

**Mechanism (technical).** A SaaS tenant evaluates each third-party OAuth grant in isolation ('App A may read my CRM') and never reasons about the transitive capability graph where App A's read scope composes with App B's external-write/outbound scope into a read-then-exfiltrate primitive neither grant alone implies. Each app holds long-lived OAuth access/refresh tokens; once one low-sensitivity integration (or its token) is compromised, the attacker reads sensitive records through App A and pushes them out through App B's sanctioned outbound scope. Because every hop uses a valid, tenant-sanctioned token, the lateral movement is indistinguishable from normal SaaS-to-SaaS automation traffic, and no single consent screen ever showed the combined authority.

**Exploitation primitive.** Compromise one low-privilege integration token, enumerate the tenant's other sanctioned OAuth grants, and chain a read scope on App A with an outbound/write scope on App B to exfiltrate data using only valid tokens, no new consent and no credential theft.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1550.001 (Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token) and T1199 (Trusted Relationship), but each models a single token/relationship. ATT&CK has no technique for OAuth transitive-scope toxic combinations, the composition of independently-granted scopes across multiple sanctioned apps into a capability exceeding any one consent; T1550.001 describes using one token, not chaining the scope graph.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *incident:* Salesloft Drift OAuth token compromise (UNC6395), Aug 2025 — Compromised Drift OAuth tokens were used to authenticate to 700+ orgs' Salesforce instances and mass-exfiltrate data (AWS keys, Snowflake tokens); shows long-lived sanctioned OAuth tokens enabling SaaS-to-SaaS lateral data theft. — <https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/data-theft-salesforce-instances-via-salesloft-drift>
- *research:* AppOmni - Drift breach (UNC6395): why Salesforce OAuth integrations are a growing risk — Analysis of how sanctioned OAuth integration scopes become an exfiltration path indistinguishable from normal automation. — <https://appomni.com/blog/drift-breach-salesforce-unc6395-saas-prevention/>

**Detection (technical).** Build a tenant-wide OAuth grant inventory and compute the transitive scope graph to flag toxic read+external-write combinations; alert on OAuth tokens performing bulk reads followed by outbound transfers, on first-seen app-to-app data flows, and on refresh-token use from new IPs/ASNs; cap token lifetime and require periodic re-consent for high-scope apps.

**References.** [Google Cloud (Mandiant) - Data theft via Salesloft Drift (UNC6395)](https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/data-theft-salesforce-instances-via-salesloft-drift) · [AppOmni - Drift breach (UNC6395) analysis](https://appomni.com/blog/drift-breach-salesforce-unc6395-saas-prevention/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** The 2025 Salesloft-Drift OAuth breach showed that valid third-party integration tokens, replayed, can pivot into hundreds of downstream SaaS tenants while blending into sanctioned traffic, but the deeper unmodeled seam is that consent is granted per-app while authority composes across the integration mesh. No tenant consent UI, OAuth scope model, or SSPM control evaluates the transitive scope graph - the toxic combination of App A's read and App B's write - so a defender approving each integration individually cannot see the read-then-exfiltrate primitive their integration sprawl already implements. This is distinct from generic stolen-token replay because the violated trust is the implicit assumption that per-grant scope bounds per-grant authority; the attacker needs no scope escalation, only to compose grants the tenant itself installed. Detection that reasons over the cross-integration scope graph rather than per-app grants is nascent, and there is no transitive-scope governance standard.

**How to validate it.** In a test SaaS tenant, install two third-party OAuth apps: App A with a read scope on a sensitive object (e.g. Salesforce/Google read on contacts or files) and App B with an outbound scope (e.g. send-mail, external-share, or webhook-post). Using App A's token read a sensitive record set, then feed it through App B's token to an external sink. Observe that no consent screen, no per-app authorization, and no single audit event represents the composite read-then-exfiltrate capability. Confirm the composition required no new grant beyond the two pre-approved ones.

**Refuted if.** The platform blocks or flags the composition - e.g. a cross-app DLP/CASB policy, App B refusing data not originating from a sanctioned source, or an admin-visible 'app-to-app data flow' audit edge fires - meaning the transitive graph IS evaluated and the combined capability is not silently permitted.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to install or compromise the token of at least one low-sensitivity third-party integration in a tenant that also has a second integration with outbound reach; or admin over a test tenant to stage both apps.

**Existing controls.** SaaS Security Posture Management (SSPM) tools (Adaptive Shield, AppOmni, Obsidian, Valence) enumerate per-app OAuth grants but rarely compute toxic-combination chains; CASB/DLP can catch the egress hop in some cases. No mainstream control evaluates the transitive scope graph as a unit.

**Proof-of-concept — OAuth transitive scope toxic-chain: read-token reused to reach write app (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Self-contained in-process stub of OAuth App A (read scope) and App B (write scope) sharing one user identity. No network.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-chain
# Local STUB. App A holds 'docs.read'; App B accepts any token bound to the same
# user subject and grants 'docs.write'. Authority binds to identity, not to scope.
# Shows token issued for READ being reused to perform a WRITE (toxic combination).
USER = "user-7@example.test"

ISSUED = {  # token -> claims
  "FAKE-TOKEN-A": {"sub": USER, "scope": "docs.read", "aud": "app-A"},
}

def app_b_write(token, doc):
    # VULN PATTERN: App B trusts the user subject, never re-checks that the
    # presented token was actually scoped/audienced for App B's write op.
    claims = ISSUED.get(token)
    if not claims:
        return ("DENY", "unknown token")
    # weak check: subject is known to us, so accept
    if claims["sub"] == USER:
        return ("WRITE-OK", f"{claims['sub']} wrote {doc} via reused {claims['scope']} token")
    return ("DENY", "subject unknown")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    tok = "FAKE-TOKEN-A"   # token minted ONLY for App A read scope
    status, detail = app_b_write(tok, "/finance/payroll.csv")
    print(f"presented_token_scope={ISSUED[tok]['scope']} aud={ISSUED[tok]['aud']}")
    print(f"app_B_result={status} :: {detail}")
    if status == "WRITE-OK":
        print("VULNERABLE: read-scoped token reused for cross-app WRITE (toxic scope chain)")
    else:
        print("PASS: write rejected; authority bound to scope/audience")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: read-scoped token reused for cross-app WRITE' -- App B authorized a write using a token minted only for App A's read scope.  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* Fully in-process stub with FAKE-TOKEN and example.test; no network, no real OAuth provider.

**Research hook.** Build a SaaS tenant scope-graph analyzer that enumerates installed integrations, models each app's effective read/write/identity scopes, and flags toxic compositions (read-sensitive in one app reachable to external-write in another); then red-team by chaining two benign-looking apps to demonstrate exfiltration that no single consent screen authorized, and propose a transitive-scope consent model.


## 100. Shared-connection reuse by unauthorized iPaaS flow

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fanout` |
| **Primitive** | P4 Context-Inheritance |
| **Trust frame** | Human Operator / User + Supply Chain / Build → Cloud Control Plane + Identity & Federation |
| **Category** | Identity & Federation Attacks; API & Business-Logic Abuse |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1078.004, T1199, CWE-269, CWE-668 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** An iPaaS platform (Zapier/Make/Workato-style) trusts that any recipe or scenario built in a workspace may execute under the workspace's shared, pre-authorized connections, so a flow author inherits the full authority of every connection an admin once added - dozens of OAuth grants to business-critical systems - without the platform re-checking that this particular automation, author, or trigger was authorized for that specific connection's scope.

**The attack.** An attacker who can author or edit a flow in the workspace (a compromised low-privilege builder account, an over-shared workspace, or a malicious imported template) attaches an existing privileged shared connection to a new automation and pivots its authority into systems they were never individually granted, so the connection's stored, long-lived OAuth tokens become an ambient capability any flow can borrow, and one workspace compromise inherits the union of every connected SaaS at once.

**Mechanism (technical).** iPaaS platforms (Zapier/Make/Workato) let any recipe/scenario in a workspace execute under the workspace's shared, pre-authorized connections, so a flow author inherits the full authority of every connection an admin once added, dozens of long-lived OAuth grants to business-critical systems, without the platform re-checking that this particular automation/author/trigger was authorized for that specific connection's scope. The stored OAuth tokens become an ambient capability any flow can borrow. One workspace compromise (a low-privilege builder account, an over-shared workspace, or a malicious imported template) inherits the union of every connected SaaS at once, because attaching an existing connection to a new flow requires no fresh consent.

**Exploitation primitive.** With author/edit rights in the workspace, create or import a flow that attaches an existing privileged shared connection and use it to pivot into systems you were never individually granted, the connection's stored tokens execute the flow with the owner's authority.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud) and T1199 (Trusted Relationship), but neither models iPaaS shared-connection authority, the platform-mediated borrowing of one principal's stored OAuth grant by an unrelated automation. ATT&CK has no technique for shared-connection privilege fanout where the automation layer re-uses ambient credentials across flows; T1078.004 assumes possession of the account, not inheritance via a shared connection object.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Obsidian Security - Integration Security for SaaS Applications — Notes that compromise of an iPaaS account inherits OAuth tokens for every connected app and that individually-safe integrations form toxic combinations when chained. — <https://www.obsidiansecurity.com/blog/saas-integration-security-best-practices>
- *product:* Zapier - Manage app connections / managed apps — Documents the shared-connection model where flows execute under workspace connections. — <https://help.zapier.com/hc/en-us/articles/44795921426317-Manage-app-connections-with-managed-apps>

**Detection (technical).** Inventory shared connections and which flows reference them; alert when a new/edited flow attaches a privileged connection or when an imported template references existing connections; bind connections to specific automations/owners and require re-authorization to attach a connection to a new flow; monitor connected-SaaS audit logs for actions from iPaaS service principals that don't match the flow's expected scope.

**References.** [Obsidian Security - SaaS integration security best practices](https://www.obsidiansecurity.com/blog/saas-integration-security-best-practices) · [Zapier - Manage app connections with managed apps](https://help.zapier.com/hc/en-us/articles/44795921426317-Manage-app-connections-with-managed-apps)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** iPaaS platforms centralize connections so authors do not re-authenticate per recipe, which means a single workspace holds the OAuth tokens for potentially dozens of business systems and any flow can borrow them - a blast radius security teams call toxic combinations. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: the platform binds authority to the workspace-level connection rather than to the specific automation+author+trigger, so authoring rights silently equal the union of all connection scopes. This is distinct from the SaaS-to-SaaS OAuth chain (which composes scopes across separate first-party integrations a tenant installed) because here a single multi-tenant automation broker holds a shared credential pool that any in-workspace flow inherits, and the entry point is flow-authoring rather than token theft. Per-connection authorization scoping, recipe-level least privilege, and detection of a flow attaching a connection it has no business using are largely absent across the major platforms, especially as iPaaS becomes the execution layer for agentic/MCP automation.

**How to validate it.** In a Zapier/Make/Workato test workspace holding several shared pre-authorized connections (one privileged, e.g. an admin Salesforce or AWS connection), authenticate as a low-privilege flow builder. Author a new recipe/scenario that attaches the privileged shared connection and triggers an action outside the builder's own direct entitlements (read restricted records, write to production). Observe whether the platform executes under the connection's full authority without re-checking that this author/automation/trigger was authorized for that connection's scope.

**Refuted if.** The platform enforces per-connection authorization or scoping (the builder cannot attach a connection they do not own/were not granted, or the run is denied/scoped down), meaning shared-connection authority is NOT ambiently borrowable.

**Prerequisites.** A low-privilege builder/editor seat in a workspace that contains privileged shared connections; or an over-shared workspace / a malicious importable template.

**Existing controls.** Workato/Workato-style folder permissions and connection-level RBAC, Zapier shared-connection restrictions, and workspace role separation exist but default sharing is permissive; SSPM coverage of iPaaS is thin.

**Proof-of-concept — iPaaS shared connection privilege fanout: authority bound to connection not author (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of an iPaaS flow engine where multiple flows reuse one shared connection's credential.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fanout
# Local STUB. A low-priv author builds a flow that uses a SHARED connection owned
# by an admin. The engine runs the flow with the connection's privileges, not the
# author's -> privilege fanout (confused deputy).
CONNECTIONS = {
  "conn-admin-crm": {"owner": "admin@example.test", "scopes": ["crm.read","crm.write","crm.export"], "token": "FAKE-CONN-TOKEN"},
}
USERS = {"intern@example.test": {"scopes": ["crm.read"]}}

def run_flow(author, connection_id, op):
    conn = CONNECTIONS[connection_id]
    # VULN PATTERN: effective scope = connection scope; author scope ignored
    effective = conn["scopes"]
    allowed = op in effective
    return allowed, effective

if __name__ == "__main__":
    author = "intern@example.test"
    op = "crm.export"
    allowed, eff = run_flow(author, "conn-admin-crm", op)
    print(f"author={author} author_scopes={USERS[author]['scopes']}")
    print(f"op={op} effective_scopes={eff} allowed={allowed}")
    if allowed and op not in USERS[author]["scopes"]:
        print("VULNERABLE: low-priv author executed op via shared connection's privileges (fanout)")
    else:
        print("PASS: flow runs with author's own authority")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: low-priv author executed op via shared connection's privileges' -- intern with only crm.read performed crm.export.  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process map of fake connections/tokens; no real iPaaS or CRM contacted.

**Research hook.** Map how leading iPaaS platforms scope shared connections to flows and authors, build a privilege-fanout analyzer that computes the effective reachable systems from a single workspace foothold, and demonstrate connection-borrowing by a low-privilege author or imported template; propose per-recipe connection binding and approval so authoring rights no longer equal the union of all connections.


## 101. Rogue exporter added via collector remote-config (OpAMP) for egress

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH9-observability-collector-egress-remoteconfig-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Supply Chain / Build + Network → SOC / Defense + Cloud Control Plane |
| **Category** | CI/CD & Build Pipeline; API & Business-Logic Abuse |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1567, T1071.001, CWE-441, CWE-668 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** An enterprise treats its telemetry pipeline - the OpenTelemetry/Datadog/Fluent collector running with broad host and network reach - as a trusted, benign plumbing component, so it is allowed to read logs/metrics/traces from everywhere and ship them to whatever exporter destinations its (often remotely-managed) config names, and the SOC trusts that whatever the pipeline delivers is the complete, unmanipulated record of what happened.

**The attack.** An attacker who can push a collector config (via the OpAMP/remote-management channel, a poisoned config repo, or a modified collector image) adds an attacker-controlled exporter to turn the always-trusted, egress-permitted agent into a sanctioned exfiltration channel, and simultaneously inserts processors that drop or filter security-relevant signals, blinding the SOC during the operation - all while the pipeline's provenance as 'just observability' suppresses scrutiny of its destinations and omissions.

**Mechanism (technical).** An enterprise treats its telemetry pipeline (OpenTelemetry/Datadog/Fluent collector) as benign plumbing with broad host/network read reach and permission to egress to whatever exporter destinations its often remotely-managed config names. The seam is the remote-config channel (OpAMP) plus the collector's standing egress trust: an attacker who can push a config, via the OpAMP management server, a poisoned config repo, or a modified collector image, adds an attacker-controlled exporter, turning the always-trusted, egress-permitted agent into a sanctioned exfiltration channel, and simultaneously inserts processors that drop/filter security-relevant signals (e.g. removing the filelog receiver for audit logs), blinding the SOC during the operation. The pipeline's 'just observability' provenance suppresses scrutiny of its destinations and omissions.

**Exploitation primitive.** Push a collector config via OpAMP (or a poisoned config repo) that adds a second exporter pointing at attacker infrastructure and a filter/processor that drops alerting-relevant spans/logs, exfiltrating telemetry while blinding detection.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1567 (Exfiltration Over Web Service) and T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols), but these describe generic egress, not the trusted-telemetry-pipeline-as-exfil-channel via remote config, combined with the dual signal-suppression that blinds the SOC. ATT&CK has no technique for observability-collector remote-config abuse (rogue exporter + dropped security signals) where the egress is pre-sanctioned plumbing.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Systems Hardening - OTel Collector Remote Configuration Security: Hardening the OpAMP Trust Boundary — Describes pushing an OpAMP config to add a secondary exporter for immediate telemetry exfiltration and to silence specific alert pipelines (remove filelog receiver / drop alerting spans). — <https://www.systemshardening.com/articles/observability/otel-collector-remote-config-security/>
- *standard:* OpenTelemetry OpAMP specification — Open Agent Management Protocol governs remote config push to collectors, the trust boundary being abused. — <https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/opamp/>

**Detection (technical).** Allowlist exporter destinations and treat any new destination as an auditable PR-gated change; run a dedicated security-critical collector with no OpAMP supervisor so it cannot receive remote config; require mTLS with a private CA on the OpAMP channel and reject unknown management servers; alert within seconds on collector config-change events and on telemetry egress to non-allowlisted endpoints, and detect drops in expected security signal volume.

**References.** [Systems Hardening - OTel Collector Remote Config Security (OpAMP)](https://www.systemshardening.com/articles/observability/otel-collector-remote-config-security/) · [OpenTelemetry - Open Agent Management Protocol (OpAMP)](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/opamp/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Telemetry collectors are deployed fleet-wide with privileged read and broad egress, and increasingly take remote configuration over OpAMP, making the config channel and exporter destination set a high-value, under-watched control surface; a non-allowlisted exporter push is itself a high-confidence compromise indicator that few orgs alarm on. The unmodeled seam is provenance trust: the SOC trusts the pipeline to faithfully and completely relay reality, so an attacker who controls config can both exfiltrate through the egress-blessed agent and delete the evidence of doing so in the same move. This is distinct from generic log tampering and from EDR-disable because the trust object is the observability pipeline's role as the authoritative, benign, egress-permitted relay - it is simultaneously a sanctioned data-out channel and the system that would otherwise witness the attack. There is little enforcement that the deployed exporter set matches an allowlist, and detection that the pipeline itself has started dropping or rerouting security telemetry is nascent.

**How to validate it.** Stand up an OpenTelemetry Collector managed via an OpAMP server (or a config repo/Helm values). Push a config that (a) adds an attacker-controlled OTLP/HTTP exporter to a destination outside the org and (b) inserts a filter/transform processor dropping security-relevant log records. Observe that the collector, which already has broad read and egress allowance, ships telemetry to the rogue exporter and that the dropped signals never reach the SOC backend.

**Refuted if.** The collector validates/signs remote configs, restricts exporter destinations via egress allowlist/network policy, or the SOC detects exporter-destination changes and signal-volume drops - so a rogue exporter cannot be added or goes unnoticed only briefly.

**Prerequisites.** Write access to the collector's remote-config channel (OpAMP server, config repo, CI values) or ability to ship a modified collector image; the collector must already have egress permission, which is typical.

**Existing controls.** OpAMP supports config signing/verification but it is optional and rarely enabled; egress network policy and DLP can constrain destinations; config-as-code review. Detection of pipeline tampering is immature.

**Proof-of-concept — Observability collector audit: rogue exporter / remote-config push trust (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 + pyyaml if available (falls back to bundled string). READ-ONLY audit of an OTel collector config (yours).

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH9-observability-collector-egress-remoteconfig-trust
# READ-ONLY audit of an OpenTelemetry collector config. Flags (a) an exporter
# egressing to a non-allowlisted endpoint and (b) an OpAMP/remote-config source
# trusted without TLS pinning/allowlist -> attacker can redirect telemetry/config.
import os, sys, re
SAMPLE = '''exporters:
  otlp/internal:
    endpoint: collector.internal.example.test:4317
  otlp/rogue:
    endpoint: 203.0.113.9:4317
extensions:
  opamp:
    server:
      endpoint: ws://remote-config.example.test/v1/opamp
      tls: {insecure: true}
service:
  pipelines:
    traces: {exporters: [otlp/internal, otlp/rogue]}
'''
ALLOWLIST = ("collector.internal.example.test",)
def load():
    p = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else None
    if p and os.path.exists(p):
        return p, open(p).read()
    return "<bundled-sample>", SAMPLE
if __name__ == "__main__":
    src, txt = load()
    eps = re.findall(r'endpoint:\s*([^\s]+)', txt)
    rogue = [e for e in eps if not any(a in e for a in ALLOWLIST)]
    insecure_remote = ("opamp" in txt and re.search(r'insecure:\s*true', txt))
    print(f"source={src}")
    print(f"endpoints={eps}")
    print(f"non_allowlisted_egress={rogue}")
    print(f"insecure_remote_config={bool(insecure_remote)}")
    if rogue or insecure_remote:
        print("VULNERABLE: collector egresses off-allowlist and/or trusts insecure remote-config")
    else:
        print("PASS: exporters allowlisted and remote-config secured")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: collector egresses off-allowlist and/or trusts insecure remote-config' listing the rogue OTLP endpoint and insecure OpAMP.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only)  
*Safety:* Read-only regex scan of a YAML config; no collector run, no telemetry sent.

**Research hook.** Build a collector-config attestation and exporter-allowlist enforcement layer that alarms on any new destination or signal-dropping processor, run a dedicated SOC-pipeline collector with no remote-management surface, and red-team the OpAMP channel to demonstrate combined exfiltration-plus-blinding while measuring how long the SOC stays dark.


## 102. Calendar-injection summoning auto-join bot for covert capture

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-identity-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Email / Messaging + Web / Fetch → Human Operator / User + AI Agent |
| **Category** | Email & Collaboration Abuse; Initial Access |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1566, T1114.002, CWE-862, CWE-441 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A calendar-integrated AI meeting bot trusts that any event on a user's calendar is a legitimate invitation to attend, record, and transcribe, so it auto-joins on the user's behalf and inherits a standing presence in conversations; downstream, the org trusts the bot's transcript/summary as a faithful record and lets it flow into CRM notes, action items, and agent workflows as authoritative meeting truth.

**The attack.** An attacker injects a calendar event (via an open invite, a spoofed organizer, or a compromised scheduling integration) to summon a victim's note-taker bot into a meeting it was never meant to attend - silently exfiltrating the conversation - or seeds the meeting/transcript with content engineered so the AI summary and extracted action items carry attacker-chosen instructions or false 'agreed' decisions into the downstream systems that ingest the bot's output as trusted record.

**Mechanism (technical).** Calendar-integrated AI meeting bots (Otter, Fireflies, Read, Fathom) treat any event on a user's calendar as a legitimate invitation to attend, record, and transcribe, so they auto-join on the user's behalf via a highly permissive OAuth grant to the calendar, and downstream the org trusts the bot's transcript/summary as authoritative meeting truth feeding CRM notes, action items, and agent workflows. Two abuse paths: an injected calendar event (open invite, spoofed organizer, or compromised scheduling integration) summons a victim's note-taker into a meeting it was never meant to attend, silently exfiltrating the conversation; and content seeded into the meeting/transcript can steer the AI summary so attacker-chosen instructions or false 'agreed' decisions flow into the downstream systems that ingest the bot's output as trusted record (a prompt-injection-style data-to-control crossing).

**Exploitation primitive.** Inject or spoof a calendar invite that triggers the victim's auto-join bot to attend and transcribe a meeting, exfiltrating the conversation; or seed transcript text engineered so the AI-generated summary/action items carry attacker-chosen 'decisions' into CRM/automation.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1566 (Phishing) and T1114.002 (Email Collection: Remote Email Collection), but neither captures calendar-injection-as-summons of an auto-join AI agent, nor the downstream trust placed in AI-summarized transcripts as authoritative record. ATT&CK has no technique for AI-agent auto-join eavesdropping or transcript-summary poisoning; T1114 is email-scoped and misses the agentic meeting-capture and downstream-ingestion seam.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Nudge Security - Shadow AI is taking notes: the growing risk of AI meeting assistants — Describes auto-join via permissive OAuth calendar grants, viral re-invite dark patterns, and bots joining every future meeting; 800 accounts in 90 days at one customer. — <https://www.nudgesecurity.com/post/shadow-ai-is-taking-notes-the-growing-risk-of-ai-meeting-assistants>
- *product:* Fireflies.ai AI note taker — Representative calendar-integrated auto-join meeting bot whose transcripts feed CRM/action-item workflows. — <https://fireflies.ai/>

**Detection (technical).** Govern AI-notetaker OAuth grants (least-privilege calendar scope, block auto-add-to-all-meetings); alert on bot participants joining meetings, especially externally-organized or recently-injected events; require host admit/consent for recording bots; treat AI-generated summaries as untrusted input, do not auto-execute action items into CRM/automation without human confirmation, and watch for instruction-like text in transcript-derived fields.

**References.** [Nudge Security - Shadow AI is taking notes (AI meeting assistants)](https://www.nudgesecurity.com/post/shadow-ai-is-taking-notes-the-growing-risk-of-ai-meeting-assistants) · [University of Cambridge - Prevent AI note-taking bots from joining Teams meetings](https://help.uis.cam.ac.uk/service/collaboration/365/teams/preventing-ai-note-taking-bots-joining-teams-meeting)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Auto-join note-takers (Otter, Fireflies, Fathom and peers) are pervasive 2025-2026 shadow IT - they attend on calendar triggers and route summaries into CRMs and downstream automations - and the 2025 Otter class action highlighted the consent/capture exposure of silent attendance. The unmodeled seam is provenance: the bot's presence is authorized by the calendar (an attacker-writable surface via open/spoofed invites) and its transcript is later trusted as an authoritative neutral record by humans and by agent pipelines that act on the extracted decisions. This is distinct from generic phishing and from prompt-injection-into-RAG because the trust objects are specifically (a) a calendar event as authorization for autonomous bot presence and (b) an AI meeting summary as ground-truth organizational record feeding action automation - a two-stage seam unique to the meeting-bot economy. There is no provenance signal that a transcript item was attacker-seeded, and no governance that gates which calendar events may summon a recording identity.

**How to validate it.** With a calendar-integrated note-taker (Otter/Fireflies/Read.ai/Fathom) configured to auto-join, send the victim an event (open invite or spoofed organizer) for a meeting they should not attend, or add the bot's invite address. Confirm the bot auto-joins and records. Separately, seed a meeting/transcript with crafted speech and observe whether the AI-generated summary/action items carry injected instructions or fabricated 'agreed' decisions into downstream CRM/agent sinks that ingest the output as authoritative.

**Refuted if.** The bot requires explicit per-meeting host approval before joining and the summary pipeline sanitizes/grounds extracted action items against verified speakers, so neither covert capture nor prompt-injection-to-downstream succeeds.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to place an event on the victim's calendar or invite their bot (often just an email address); for the injection variant, presence in or content seeding of a meeting the bot transcribes.

**Existing controls.** Auto-join toggles, host-admit gating, and meeting-recording-consent prompts exist; transcript prompt-injection into downstream agents has no standard mitigation. Calendar-summoning of note-takers is a discussed but not formally tracked technique.

**Proof-of-concept — Meeting-bot auto-join: calendar event trusted as authoritative identity (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of a transcription bot that auto-joins based solely on a calendar invite event.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-identity-trust
# Local STUB. The bot auto-joins any meeting whose calendar event 'organizer'
# field claims an internal domain, and tags the transcript with that identity --
# without verifying the event was issued by the real calendar/IdP.
def autojoin(calendar_event):
    org = calendar_event.get("organizer","")
    # VULN PATTERN: trust the event-supplied organizer string; no IdP verification
    internal = org.endswith("@example.test")
    return internal, org
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Forged event (any attacker can craft an ICS / API invite)
    forged = {"organizer": "ceo@example.test", "subject": "Q3 Strategy", "signed": False}
    joined, org = autojoin(forged)
    print(f"event_signed={forged['signed']} claimed_organizer={org}")
    print(f"bot_joined={joined} transcript_attributed_to={org if joined else 'n/a'}")
    if joined and not forged["signed"]:
        print("VULNERABLE: bot auto-joined+attributed transcript to UNVERIFIED organizer identity")
    else:
        print("PASS: bot requires signed/verified invite before joining")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: bot auto-joined+attributed transcript to UNVERIFIED organizer identity' for an unsigned forged invite.  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process dict event; no calendar API, no meeting platform, example.test identities.

**Research hook.** Demonstrate calendar-injection that summons a target's note-taker into an unintended meeting and quantify covert capture; separately seed a meeting with content that steers the AI summary's action items, and trace whether downstream CRM/agent automations execute the injected decisions - then propose invite-provenance gating for auto-join and provenance tagging on summary-derived actions.


## 103. RPA orchestrator/vault compromise harvesting bot credentials

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-impersonation` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | Host / Endpoint + Supply Chain / Build → Identity & Federation + Active Directory |
| **Category** | Identity & Federation Attacks; Persistence |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1555, T1078.002, CWE-522, CWE-798 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** Enterprise systems trust that actions performed under a user identity reflect that human's intent and presence, while RPA platforms run attended and unattended bots that hold and replay human or human-equivalent credentials to drive those same systems through the UI, so target applications grant the bot the full authority of the impersonated identity and attribute its every action to a person who was not there.

**The attack.** An attacker who compromises the RPA orchestrator, a bot's credential vault, or an unattended runner harvests the standing human-equivalent credentials and drives privileged business actions through the bot's sanctioned UI automation, and because the target system attributes the activity to the human identity the bot impersonates, the malicious actions are non-repudiably blamed on an innocent user and blend into expected automated workflow traffic.

**Mechanism (technical).** Enterprise systems attribute actions performed under a user identity to that human's intent and presence, while RPA platforms run attended/unattended bots that hold and replay human or human-equivalent credentials to drive those same systems through the UI. Unattended robots must log into Windows and target apps with stored user credentials retrieved from the orchestrator's credential store (native vault or Azure Key Vault/HashiCorp integration), so the target grants the bot the impersonated identity's full authority and records every action as that person. An attacker who compromises the RPA orchestrator, a bot's credential vault, or an unattended runner harvests these standing human-equivalent credentials and drives privileged business actions through the sanctioned UI automation, and because the activity is attributed to the human, malicious actions are non-repudiably blamed on an innocent user and blend into expected automation traffic.

**Exploitation primitive.** Compromise the RPA orchestrator or its credential store (or a runner host) to retrieve the stored human-equivalent credentials/tokens, then drive privileged business transactions through the bot's UI automation under the victim's identity.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1555 (Credentials from Password Stores) and T1078.002 (Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts), but neither models the RPA-orchestrator/credential-vault as a concentration of standing human-equivalent identities, nor the non-repudiation collapse where bot actions are attributed to a human who was not present. ATT&CK has no technique for RPA bot-identity impersonation or unattended-runner credential harvesting as a business-process abuse.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* UiPath Orchestrator - unattended robot credentials & credential stores — Unattended robots require stored user credentials retrieved from the orchestrator vault (native, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp) to log into Windows and target apps, the concentrated standing-credential surface. — <https://docs.uipath.com/orchestrator/automation-cloud/latest/user-guide/integrating-credential-stores>
- *product:* UiPath Robot ICAM credentialing guidance — Vendor guidance on credentialing robots and managing their access, confirming bots hold human-equivalent credentials. — <https://assets.ctfassets.net/5965pury2lcm/42wrZZJMZWORVfGGJwSVyT/0e2966a58b8c92ce41adecd9604883be/UiPath_Robot_ICAM_Guidance.pdf>

**Detection (technical).** Give each robot a distinct, attributable non-human identity rather than a shared human credential; alert on orchestrator credential-asset retrievals and on robot logons outside scheduled windows/hosts; baseline per-bot action patterns and flag privileged transactions diverging from the automation's defined workflow; monitor for human-attributed sessions whose source is an RPA runner during the user's off-hours.

**References.** [UiPath Orchestrator - Integrating credential stores](https://docs.uipath.com/orchestrator/automation-cloud/latest/user-guide/integrating-credential-stores) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1555 - Credentials from Password Stores](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1555/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** RPA fleets impersonate humans by design - attended bots run under user accounts with no isolation from the controlling user, and unattended bots hold elevated credentials to perform privileged operations - making the credential store and orchestrator a concentrated, high-value identity cache, and vendor guidance itself flags credential misuse as the top RPA risk. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority with a non-repudiation twist: the target application cannot distinguish bot-driven from human-driven actions, so a compromised bot both wields the human's authority and launders attribution onto that human. This is distinct from generic stolen-credential use and from orphaned-NHI gaps because the bot is a deliberately human-impersonating actor with no second factor of presence, sitting between the credential vault and the UI it drives; the attacker inherits not just access but the victim's accountability. Behavioral separation of bot vs human actions, and detection of a bot acting outside its normal recipe, remain weak as RPA expands and fuses with AI agents.

**How to validate it.** In a UiPath/Automation Anywhere/Power Automate Desktop test deployment, configure an unattended bot whose vault (Orchestrator credential asset / CyberArk integration) holds a human-equivalent account. Simulate compromise of the orchestrator or runner host, retrieve the stored credential, and drive a privileged business action through the bot's UI automation. Observe that the target application attributes the action to the impersonated human and that it blends into expected RPA traffic.

**Refuted if.** Credentials are non-retrievable from the vault (HSM-backed, just-in-time, never exposed to the runner in usable form), bot identities are distinct non-human accounts with their own audit attribution, and orchestrator access is strongly isolated - so harvest and human-attribution both fail.

**Prerequisites.** Compromise of the RPA orchestrator, the credential vault integration, or an unattended runner host; or insider access to the RPA platform.

**Existing controls.** CyberArk/RPA vault integrations, dedicated bot service accounts, session isolation, and PAM for runners exist; misconfiguration and reused human credentials remain common. Credential theft from automation hosts is a well-established class.

**Proof-of-concept — RPA unattended bot: vault hands human creds with no presence factor (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of a credential vault issuing a human user's credential to an unattended RPA bot.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-impersonation
# Local STUB vault. An unattended bot requests a HUMAN user's credential and the
# vault releases it with no presence/2FA/just-in-time check -> bot impersonates human.
VAULT = {"alice@example.test": {"secret": "FAKE-HUMAN-PW", "requires_presence": True}}
def checkout(requester_type, target_user, presence_proof=None):
    rec = VAULT[target_user]
    # VULN PATTERN: presence requirement ignored for bot requesters
    if rec["requires_presence"] and presence_proof is None and requester_type == "bot":
        # misconfig: bots bypass presence
        return ("RELEASED", rec["secret"])
    if rec["requires_presence"] and presence_proof is None:
        return ("DENY", None)
    return ("RELEASED", rec["secret"])
if __name__ == "__main__":
    status, secret = checkout("bot", "alice@example.test", presence_proof=None)
    print(f"requester=bot target=alice@example.test presence_proof=None")
    print(f"vault_result={status} cred={'<redacted FAKE>' if secret else None}")
    if status == "RELEASED":
        print("VULNERABLE: unattended bot obtained a HUMAN credential with no presence factor")
    else:
        print("PASS: presence factor enforced; human cred withheld from bot")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: unattended bot obtained a HUMAN credential with no presence factor' (vault_result=RELEASED).  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process vault with a FAKE password; nothing written, no real IAM/vault touched.

**Research hook.** Prototype an identity-attribution layer that distinguishes RPA-driven from human-driven sessions (signed bot context, presence factors) so target systems can authorize and audit bot actions distinctly; red-team an orchestrator/vault compromise to show authority capture plus attribution laundering, and define a non-repudiation standard for automated identities.


## 104. Forged/replayed e-signature completion callback releasing value

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-release-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Web / Fetch + Document / File → Cloud Control Plane + Human Operator / User |
| **Category** | API & Business-Logic Abuse; Email & Collaboration Abuse |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1190, T1565.001, CWE-345, CWE-294 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A business process trusts the 'agreement completed/signed' signal from an e-signature workflow as a final, authoritative legal fact, so the completion event - delivered by callback or read from the platform - is wired to release value automatically: provisioning access, paying an invoice, triggering the next agreement, or archiving an authoritative copy, on the assumption that a completion event means the right parties validly signed the right unaltered document.

**The attack.** An attacker subverts the completion signal rather than the cryptographic seal - forging or replaying the completion webhook, manipulating the signer-routing/recipient logic so an unauthorized party satisfies the signing step, or racing a document/template swap before the downstream system fetches the 'completed' artifact - so the receiving system releases value on a completion that is procedurally invalid even though each tamper-sealed page checks out, exploiting the gap between 'a completion event arrived' and 'the intended parties validly agreed to this content'.

**Mechanism (technical).** A business process trusts the 'agreement completed/signed' signal from an e-signature workflow (DocuSign Connect callback or a status read) as a final legal fact and wires it to release value automatically: provisioning access, paying an invoice, triggering the next agreement, or archiving the authoritative copy. The seam is the gap between 'a completion event arrived' and 'the intended parties validly agreed to this content'; an attacker subverts the completion signal rather than the per-page cryptographic seal, by forging or replaying the completion webhook (e.g. missing/weak HMAC, no timestamp/replay protection), manipulating signer-routing/recipient logic so an unauthorized party satisfies the signing step, or racing a document/template swap before the downstream system fetches the completed artifact, so the receiving system releases value on a procedurally invalid completion even though each tamper-sealed page checks out.

**Exploitation primitive.** Forge/replay the DocuSign Connect completion webhook (or manipulate recipient routing / swap the template before fetch) so the downstream system treats the envelope as validly completed and releases value (access, payment, next-step trigger).

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1565.001 (Stored Data Manipulation), but neither captures completion-event-as-value-release trust, the business-logic seam where a procedurally-invalid 'completed' signal triggers downstream value despite valid page seals. ATT&CK has no technique for e-signature completion-callback forgery/recipient-routing abuse; T1190 implies a code exploit, this is trusted-event business-logic abuse.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* DocuSign Connect webhooks / HMAC verification — Connect completion events drive downstream provisioning/order/payment automation; vendor guidance requires HMAC verification plus replay protection (timestamp/unique ID), absence of which enables forgery/replay. — <https://developers.docusign.com/platform/webhooks/connect/>
- *research:* eSignGlobal - DocuSign Connect: securing webhooks with HMAC signature verification — Details HMAC verification and replay-protection practices for completion callbacks; the abuse condition is their omission. — <https://www.esignglobal.com/blog/docusign-connect-securing-webhooks-hmac-signature-verification-code>

**Detection (technical).** Verify the Connect HMAC over the raw body and enforce timestamp/idempotency replay protection; never act on the callback alone, re-fetch envelope status and the completed artifact (by envelope ID and document hash) from the e-signature API before releasing value; validate recipient-routing/signer identity against the intended parties; alert on completion events whose envelope/document hash differs from the originally-sent document.

**References.** [DocuSign Connect (webhooks)](https://developers.docusign.com/platform/webhooks/connect/) · [eSignGlobal - Securing DocuSign Connect webhooks with HMAC](https://www.esignglobal.com/blog/docusign-connect-securing-webhooks-hmac-signature-verification-code)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** E-signature platforms emphasize PKI tamper-seals on the document, but the integration economy increasingly wires the completion event itself - via Connect-style webhooks and workflow builders that chain a second agreement or fire an ERP invoice on completion - into automatic value release, and that event channel is where trust is misplaced. The unmodeled seam is provenance of the completion fact as distinct from integrity of the document bytes: the downstream system treats 'completed' as proof the right parties validly agreed, while routing logic, recipient substitution, or a forged/replayed completion callback can produce a 'completed' state that the tamper-seal never contradicts. This is distinct from the generic inbound-webhook gap because the trust object is specifically the legal completion attestation that gates automated value transfer, and distinct from document-tamper attacks because the seal stays valid - the procedural validity, not the bytes, is forged. There is no downstream control that re-verifies signer identity and routing provenance before acting on completion, and no detection of value released on a procedurally-invalid completion.

**How to validate it.** Build a downstream system wired to a DocuSign/Adobe Sign 'envelope completed' callback that auto-releases value (provision access, pay invoice). Test: (1) forge/replay the completion webhook without valid HMAC; (2) manipulate signer-routing so an unauthorized recipient satisfies the signing step; (3) race a template/document swap between completion and the downstream fetch of the 'completed' artifact. Observe whether value is released on a procedurally invalid completion even though tamper-seal hashes verify.

**Refuted if.** The downstream verifies the completion webhook signature with replay protection AND re-fetches and validates signer identity, recipient routing, and document hash from the platform API before releasing value - closing the 'event arrived' vs 'right parties validly signed' gap.

**Prerequisites.** Knowledge of the downstream's completion-webhook endpoint/schema; for routing abuse, ability to influence envelope recipient configuration; for the swap, write access to the template/document store and timing control.

**Existing controls.** E-sign providers offer Connect HMAC signing and authoritative API re-fetch; PowerForm/signer-authentication controls. The seam is the downstream's reliance on the event rather than re-verification - overlaps strongly with the webhook-control-plane gap.

**Proof-of-concept — E-signature completion event trusted to release value without verification (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of a payment-release workflow gated only on an e-sign 'completed' webhook field.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-release-trust
# Local STUB. A funds-release step trusts an e-signature provider 'status:completed'
# event WITHOUT verifying the envelope server-side or the event signature.
import json, hmac, hashlib
PROVIDER_SECRET = b"FAKE-ESIGN-SECRET"
def release_funds(event_json, sig=None, verify=False):
    if verify:
        expect = hmac.new(PROVIDER_SECRET, event_json, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
        if sig != expect:
            return ("DENY","bad signature")
    evt = json.loads(event_json)
    if evt.get("status") == "completed":
        return ("RELEASE", f"wired {evt.get('amount')} for envelope {evt.get('envelope_id')}")
    return ("HOLD","not completed")
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Attacker-forged completion event, no valid signature, verify disabled
    forged = json.dumps({"envelope_id":"env-FAKE","status":"completed","amount":"50000"}).encode()
    status, detail = release_funds(forged, sig=None, verify=False)
    print(f"event_signature=None server_verify=False")
    print(f"result={status} :: {detail}")
    if status == "RELEASE":
        print("VULNERABLE: value released on UNVERIFIED e-signature completion event")
    else:
        print("PASS: envelope/signature verified before release")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: value released on UNVERIFIED e-signature completion event' (result=RELEASE) for a forged completion event.  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process JSON, FAKE secret, no payment rail, no e-signature provider contacted.

**Research hook.** Map e-signature completion-to-action integrations (callback trust, signer-routing flexibility, race windows before artifact fetch) and demonstrate value release on a forged/replayed or mis-routed completion whose document seal remains valid; propose a downstream gate that re-verifies signer identity, routing, and a nonce-bound completion attestation against the platform API before releasing value.


## 105. Citizen-dev flow referencing a shared privileged connection

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connector-borrow` |
| **Primitive** | P4 Context-Inheritance |
| **Trust frame** | Human Operator / User + Supply Chain / Build → Cloud Control Plane + Identity & Federation |
| **Category** | Identity & Federation Attacks; API & Business-Logic Abuse |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1078.004, T1098, CWE-269, CWE-668 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** A low-code/no-code platform trusts that a flow built by any citizen developer may run under whatever connection reference is attached to it, so when an admin shares a privileged service connection (or a flow itself) into a maker environment, every automation that references it executes with that connection's elevated authority on the assumption that the maker was implicitly entitled to it.

**The attack.** A citizen developer (or an attacker who has taken over a maker account, or who plants a shared flow/template) authors an automation that references an admin's privileged shared connection and uses it to perform actions far beyond their own rights - reading restricted data, writing to production systems, or creating identities - so the platform silently elevates a low-privilege author to the connection owner's authority, and the action is logged as the service connection rather than the human who actually directed it.

**Mechanism (technical).** Low-code/no-code platforms (Microsoft Power Platform) let a flow built by any citizen developer run under whatever connection reference is attached to it, so when an admin shares a privileged service connection (or a flow) into a maker environment, every automation referencing it executes with that connection's elevated authority on the assumption the maker was implicitly entitled to it. This indirect (implicit) use of a connection circumvents the maker's own access rights, and the platform logs the action under the service connection identity rather than the human who directed it. A citizen developer, an attacker who took over a maker account, or a planted shared flow/template can author an automation that references the admin's privileged connection to read restricted data, write to production, or create identities, silently elevating a low-privilege author to the connection owner's authority. DLP policy historically enforced only on new connections, leaving large back-catalogs of existing shared connections exploitable.

**Exploitation primitive.** As a low-privilege maker (or via a compromised maker account / planted shared template), build a flow that references an admin's shared privileged connection and use its stored authority to perform actions beyond your own rights; the action is logged as the connection, not you.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud) and T1098 (Account Manipulation), but neither models low-code shared-connector authority borrowing, where the platform executes a flow with a connection owner's privilege regardless of the author's rights, and attribution is to the connection not the human. ATT&CK has no technique for citizen-dev privileged-connector borrow / implicit-connection privilege escalation.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Secureworks (CTU) - Power Platform Privilege Escalation — Demonstrates privilege escalation via the Power Platform API by elevating an existing service principal; shows the platform as a privilege-escalation target. — <https://www.secureworks.com/research/power-platform-privilege-escalation>
- *research:* Zenity Labs - The Long and Winding Road of DLP Patches in Power Platform — Documents that DLP restrictions were enforced only on new connections, not existing ones, leaving shared connections borrowable. — <https://labs.zenity.io/p/long-winding-road-dlp-patches-power-platform>

**Detection (technical).** Inventory shared connections/connection references and which flows and makers consume them; alert on a flow referencing a connection whose owner differs from the author, and on newly imported shared flows/templates; enforce DLP on existing (not just new) connections; correlate connected-system audit logs for service-connection actions that exceed the directing maker's own entitlements.

**References.** [Secureworks - Power Platform Privilege Escalation](https://www.secureworks.com/research/power-platform-privilege-escalation) · [Zenity Labs - DLP Patches in Power Platform](https://labs.zenity.io/p/long-winding-road-dlp-patches-power-platform)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Low-code/no-code platforms (Power Platform, App-builder and flow tools) let business users compose automations against shared connections, and the prevalent failure is that authority is bound to the connection reference, not to the author or the action - so a privileged connection shared into an environment becomes ambient elevation for anyone who can attach it. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: a citizen-dev flow inherits the owning service connection's privilege without the platform re-authorizing the specific author for that scope, and audit attributes the action to the connection rather than the person. This is distinct from the iPaaS shared-connection gap (a third-party automation broker holding a multi-system token pool) because here the elevation happens inside the enterprise's own first-party low-code tenant via citizen developers and shared/imported flows, and distinct from RPA bots because there is no impersonation runner - the privilege leaks through the connection-reference model itself. As these platforms become persistence-friendly hosting for agentic logic, attacker-planted shared flows on privileged connectors are a quiet foothold, and governance/detection for connection-borrowing by under-entitled makers is largely absent.

**How to validate it.** In a Power Platform / Power Automate test environment, have an admin share a privileged service connection (e.g. SQL/Dataverse with elevated rights) into a maker environment. As a low-privilege maker, author a cloud flow that references the shared connection and performs actions beyond the maker's own rights (read restricted data, create a user). Observe the platform executes under the connection owner's authority and logs the action as the connection/service principal, not the human maker.

**Refuted if.** The platform enforces that a maker can only use connections they own or were explicitly granted with matching scope, blocks the privileged action, or attributes/audits it to the actual human author - so silent privilege borrowing does not occur.

**Prerequisites.** A maker seat in an environment where a privileged connection or flow has been shared; or takeover of a maker account / a planted shared template.

**Existing controls.** Power Platform DLP policies, environment-level connector governance, and CoE Starter Kit auditing exist; sharing remains permissive by default and connection-reference authority is broadly inherited. Near-duplicate of the iPaaS shared-connection gap.

**Proof-of-concept — Citizen-dev flow borrows a privileged connector beyond builder's authority (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of a low-code platform where a citizen-dev app references a maker-shared privileged connector.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connector-borrow
# Local STUB. A citizen developer references a connector shared org-wide that wraps
# an admin service principal. The app runs with the connector's grant, not the maker's.
CONNECTORS = {
  "shared-sql-admin": {"shared":"org-wide","principal":"svc-sqladmin@example.test","grants":["db.read","db.write","db.drop"]},
}
MAKERS = {"citizen@example.test": {"grants":["db.read"]}}
def invoke(maker, connector, action):
    c = CONNECTORS[connector]
    # VULN PATTERN: action authorized against connector grant, maker grant ignored
    return action in c["grants"], c["principal"], c["grants"]
if __name__ == "__main__":
    maker="citizen@example.test"; action="db.drop"
    ok, principal, grants = invoke(maker, "shared-sql-admin", action)
    print(f"maker={maker} maker_grants={MAKERS[maker]['grants']}")
    print(f"action={action} ran_as={principal} connector_grants={grants} allowed={ok}")
    if ok and action not in MAKERS[maker]["grants"]:
        print("VULNERABLE: citizen-dev app borrowed privileged connector to exceed maker authority")
    else:
        print("PASS: app constrained to maker's own grants")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: citizen-dev app borrowed privileged connector to exceed maker authority' -- citizen with db.read ran db.drop as svc-sqladmin.  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process connector map; example.test principals; no Power Platform/SQL contacted.

**Research hook.** Analyze how low-code platforms bind execution authority to shared connections versus authors, build a maker-environment privilege-leak analyzer that flags flows referencing connections beyond the author's own rights, and demonstrate elevation and persistence via a shared/imported flow on a privileged connector; propose per-flow connection authorization and human-attributed auditing of connector-driven actions.


---

# Data / secrets / MLOps infrastructure  *(9)*

## 106. Credential recovery from intermediate container image layers

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-cicd-secret-sprawl-cache-layer-log-residue` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Supply Chain / Build + Host / Endpoint → Cloud Control Plane + Container / K8s |
| **Category** | CI/CD & Build Pipeline; Credential Access |
| **Validity** | demonstrated (high confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1552.001, T1610, CWE-538, CWE-540 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** A CI/CD platform trusts that a secret injected into a build is ephemeral and confined to the running job - present only as a masked environment variable for the step that needs it - so it treats the build cache, the produced container image layers, and the job log stream as non-sensitive build byproducts that can be persisted, shared across jobs, cached publicly, and retained indefinitely without secret-grade controls.

**The attack.** A secret leaks out of its intended ephemeral scope into a durable artifact - baked into an intermediate container layer by a RUN that referenced it, written to a restored/cross-job build cache, or echoed into a job log that masking missed because the value was transformed, base64'd, or split - and an attacker who can read those persisted byproducts (a public layer, a shared cache key, a retained log) recovers a live credential long after the job ended, crossing from a transient build context into standing access the platform never modeled as a secret store.

**Mechanism (technical).** Secrets injected at build time persist in artifacts and logs beyond the step that used them. A secret passed via ARG/ENV or written by a RUN is recorded in image layer metadata and the filesystem of that layer; a later 'rm' only adds a whiteout in a new layer, so the value remains recoverable via docker history --no-trunc or by extracting the underlying layer tarballs (docker save). Restored cross-job CI caches can carry credentials into unrelated jobs, and GitHub Actions log masking is a literal search-and-replace over the exact secret string - so any transform (base64, URL-encoding, JSON/YAML wrapping, string splitting) defeats redaction, and secrets can also surface unmasked in error output. The net effect is credential recovery from intermediate image layers, build cache, and job logs long after the build.

**Exploitation primitive.** Pull a published image and run docker history --no-trunc or unpack layer tarballs to recover secrets baked by ARG/RUN (even if deleted in a later layer), or read CI logs where a transformed/structured secret bypassed masking.

**MITRE gap.** T1552.001 (Unsecured Credentials: Credentials in Files) treats a credential as a file on a live host, and T1610 (Deploy Container) is about running an attacker container - neither models credential residue in immutable image layer history / build cache, where the secret was 'deleted' but persists in a lower layer, nor the redaction-bypass property of CI log masking. ATT&CK has no technique for build-time secret sprawl across the supply chain (layer history, restored caches, masked-but-transformed logs); it is closest to T1552.001/T1552.004 but misses the layered-artifact persistence and CI-log-masking-as-non-security-boundary semantics that make this a distinct, demonstrated class. (Maps better to CI/CD-specific frameworks like OWASP Top 10 CI/CD Security Risks than to ATT&CK.)

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* Dahlmanns, Sander, Decker, Wehrle - 'Secrets Revealed in Container Images: An Internet-wide Study on Occurrence and Impact' (ASIA CCS 2023, RWTH Aachen COMSYS) — Analyzed 337,171 Docker Hub images plus thousands from private registries; 8.5% contained secrets, including 52,107 private keys and 3,158 API secrets, with hundreds of thousands of dependent hosts - demonstrates secret persistence in image layers at internet scale. — <https://www.comsys.rwth-aachen.de/publication/2023/2023_dahlmanns_secrets-revealed-in-container/>
- *incident:* BleepingComputer - 'Over 10,000 Docker Hub images found leaking credentials, auth keys' (Dec 2025) — Reported 10,000+ Docker Hub images exposing live production credentials, CI/CD DB creds, and ~4,000 AI/ML API keys (OpenAI, Anthropic, Hugging Face) across 100+ organizations; ~42% of exposed images had five or more secrets. — <https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/over-10-000-docker-hub-images-found-leaking-credentials-auth-keys/>
- *product:* GitHub Actions - Secure use reference (secret masking guidance) — Documents that secret redaction relies on exact-string matching and that wrapping secrets in JSON/XML/YAML significantly reduces the chance of proper redaction; masking is a convenience, not a security boundary. — <https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/security/secure-use>
- *incident:* actions/runner issue #2265 - 'Masked secrets leaked in error logs' — Reported case where a masked secret (GitHub App token) appeared unmasked in Terraform error output in workflow logs; closed as not planned, illustrating masking gaps on error paths. — <https://github.com/actions/runner/issues/2265>
- *product:* Docker docs - Build secrets (RUN --mount=type=secret / BuildKit) — BuildKit secret mounts keep secrets in memory during a build step and out of image layers - the sanctioned mitigation for ARG/ENV layer leakage. — <https://docs.docker.com/build/building/secrets/>

**Detection (technical).** Run layer-aware secret scanners (e.g., on docker history --no-trunc output and per-layer filesystem diffs, or tools like trivy/grype/trufflehog over saved image tars) in CI and on registry pushes; scan CI job logs for high-entropy strings and known-secret patterns including base64/URL-encoded variants; block builds that use ARG/ENV for secrets and require RUN --mount=type=secret; rotate any credential ever present in a build context and treat masked logs as non-authoritative.

**References.** [Secrets Revealed in Container Images: An Internet-wide Study - RWTH Aachen COMSYS (ASIA CCS 2023)](https://www.comsys.rwth-aachen.de/publication/2023/2023_dahlmanns_secrets-revealed-in-container/) · [Over 10,000 Docker Hub images found leaking credentials, auth keys - BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/over-10-000-docker-hub-images-found-leaking-credentials-auth-keys/) · [Secure use reference - GitHub Docs (Actions)](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/security/secure-use) · [Masked secrets leaked in error logs - actions/runner #2265](https://github.com/actions/runner/issues/2265) · [Build secrets - Docker Docs](https://docs.docker.com/build/building/secrets/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** CI/CD secret injection assumes ephemerality, but the surrounding build infrastructure - layer caches, cross-job and remote build caches, and retained log streams - is engineered for durability and sharing, and 2025-2027 supply-chain incidents repeatedly show live credentials recovered from published image layers and cached build state. The unmodeled seam is provenance/lifetime: a value is trusted to remain a transient in-job secret, but the build byproducts that capture it are governed as non-sensitive plumbing, so masking (which only redacts exact verbatim matches in the log channel) and ephemeral scoping both fail against transformed values and persisted caches/layers. This is distinct from the context-window-secret-persistence gap (LLM transcript export) and from classic hardcoded-secret findings because the trust object is the assumed confinement of an injected secret to its job, broken specifically by the cache/layer/log durability of the build platform rather than by source-code exposure. Provenance-aware secret tracking across cache and layer boundaries, and detection of transformed-secret leakage that bypasses verbatim masking, are nascent.

**How to validate it.** Construct a Dockerfile/CI job that references a secret in a RUN layer (or restores a cross-job cache, or echoes a base64'd/transformed secret a masker misses). Build and publish the image to a registry and persist the cache/logs. As a separate party with read access to the published layers / shared cache key / retained log, run dive or extract layer tarballs (or 'docker history') and recover the live credential after the job ended.

**Refuted if.** The secret never lands in a durable byproduct - BuildKit --mount=type=secret keeps it out of layers, cache is scoped per-job and unreadable, and log masking catches transformed values - so no recoverable residue exists post-job.

**Prerequisites.** Read access to a published image's intermediate layers (public or pull-permitted registry), a shared/restorable build cache, or retained job logs.

**Existing controls.** BuildKit secret mounts, GitHub/GitLab secret masking, OIDC short-lived credentials replacing long-lived secrets, and scanners (TruffleHog, Trivy, gitleaks, dive) directly target this; it remains a frequently-realized leak class (documented incidents of secrets in public layers).

**Proof-of-concept — CI/CD secret sprawl scanner: build log / image layer / cache residue (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. READ-ONLY regex scan of a sample build log + cache dir (yours). Bundled DEFANGED sample if no path given.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-cicd-secret-sprawl-cache-layer-log-residue
# READ-ONLY scanner. Greps a build log / cache / extracted image layer for secret
# residue that escaped masking (env echoes, set -x, cached files).
import os, re, sys
SAMPLE = '''+ export DEPLOY_TOKEN=FAKE-TOKEN-ghp_0000000000000000000000000000000000
+ aws configure set aws_secret_access_key FAKEAWSSECRETKEY0000000000000000000000AA
Saving cache for key node-modules
echo "db_url=postgres://user:FAKEPASSWORD@db.example.test/app"
[masked] ***
'''
PATTERNS = {
  "github_pat": re.compile(r'ghp_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}'),
  "aws_secret": re.compile(r'(?i)aws_secret_access_key\s+[A-Za-z0-9/+]{20,}'),
  "url_cred":   re.compile(r'://[^:/\s]+:[^@/\s]+@'),
  "bearer":     re.compile(r'(?i)(token|secret|password)=[^\s"]{6,}'),
}
def load():
    p = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else None
    if p and os.path.exists(p):
        return p, open(p, errors="replace").read()
    return "<bundled-sample>", SAMPLE
if __name__ == "__main__":
    src, txt = load()
    hits = {}
    for name, rx in PATTERNS.items():
        m = rx.findall(txt)
        if m: hits[name] = len(m)
    print(f"source={src} findings={hits}")
    if hits:
        print(f"VULNERABLE: {sum(hits.values())} secret-like residue token(s) found in CI artifact (sprawl)")
    else:
        print("PASS: no secret residue detected")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: N secret-like residue token(s) found in CI artifact' for a log containing masked-but-leaked secrets.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only)  
*Safety:* Read-only regex over sample text with FAKE tokens; reports counts only, prints no secret values.

**Research hook.** Build a secret-flow tracer that follows an injected credential through build cache restoration, image layer construction, and log emission - detecting transformed/encoded leakage that verbatim masking misses - and quantify how often live secrets survive in public layers and shared caches; propose taint-tracked secret scoping and layer/cache redaction that treats all build byproducts as secret-bearing until proven clean.


## 107. Onward re-share of a received lakehouse dataset beyond provider authorization

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-lakehouse-cross-workspace-share-transitive-grant` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | Cloud Control Plane + Identity & Federation → Cloud Control Plane + Vector Store / RAG Index |
| **Category** | Cloud Attacks; Collection |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1530, T1199, CWE-668, CWE-863 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A data lakehouse provider (Snowflake share / Databricks Delta Sharing / BigQuery Analytics Hub) treats a share grant as a bilateral, terminal trust edge - 'provider account P grants recipient account R read on object O' - and assumes the recipient is the consuming endpoint, so it never re-evaluates whether R has itself re-shared O onward, mounted it into a second metastore, or exposed it through an open Delta Sharing recipient token, letting the original provider's access decision silently propagate across a share graph the provider can no longer see.

**The attack.** An attacker who controls or compromises a downstream recipient account (a partner workspace, an over-permissive analytics-hub listing, or a leaked Delta Sharing bearer token) re-exposes the shared dataset to accounts the provider never authorized, so a single grant fans out transitively across organizational boundaries; because each hop is a legitimate platform share operation logged under the recipient's identity, the provider's audit shows only the first authorized edge and never the onward propagation that turned a scoped partner grant into broad multi-tenant exposure.

**Mechanism (technical).** Lakehouse data-sharing protocols treat a grant as a bilateral, terminal relationship between provider and one recipient, but the recipient holds an artifact that can be propagated onward outside the provider's visibility. In Databricks Delta Sharing 'open sharing', the recipient receives a credential file containing a long-lived bearer token plus an endpoint URL; anyone who possesses that file can call the sharing REST API and fetch short-lived pre-signed object URLs, so the token, not an identity, is the access control boundary. Snowflake direct shares forbid consumer re-share, yet listings with resharing.enabled=true permit a resharer to legitimately propagate data to further accounts, and a Snowflake-created reader account can mount the data into a separate account the provider does not govern. BigQuery Analytics Hub subscriptions create a read-only 'linked dataset' the subscriber can join with their own data and re-expose through their own listings/exports, so onward distribution is invisible to the original provider.

**Exploitation primitive.** Exfiltrate or copy a Delta Sharing recipient credential file (bearer token + endpoint) and replay it from an unauthorized network/account to pull all shared tables, or stand up a resharer listing / reader account to forward provider data to a downstream account the provider cannot see or revoke.

**MITRE gap.** T1530 (Data from Cloud Storage) models reading objects from a bucket the adversary already has access to, and T1199 (Trusted Relationship) models initial access through a third party - neither models the transitive re-grant of an already-shared managed dataset, where the receiving tenant lawfully holds data and then propagates it onward (token leak, second-metastore mount, resharer listing) beyond the provider's authorization graph. ATT&CK has no technique for 'recipient becomes an unauthorized provider' or for bearer-token-as-credential data-plane access in a sharing protocol; T1550 (Use Alternate Authentication Material) is the closest for the token replay but does not capture the data-sharing onward-distribution semantics or the provider's loss of revocation/visibility.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* Databricks Delta Sharing - Create a recipient using bearer tokens (open sharing) — Documents the recipient credential file (bearer token + endpoint) for non-Databricks recipients and warns recipients to treat the credential as a secret and not distribute it; supports token expiration/rotation and IP access lists as mitigations. — <https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-sharing/create-recipient-token>
