- *product:* Databricks blog - Security Best Practices for Delta Sharing — Recommends short-lived tokens (never-expiring tokens are a 'huge risk'), provider-side token expiration control, and IP access lists restricting REST API, activation URL, and credential file download. — <https://www.databricks.com/blog/2022/08/01/security-best-practices-for-delta-sharing.html>
- *standard:* delta-io/delta-sharing PROTOCOL.md — Open protocol spec: profile file carries a bearerToken and endpoint; the token is the credential used against the sharing server REST API. — <https://github.com/delta-io/delta-sharing/blob/main/PROTOCOL.md>
- *product:* Snowflake docs - Reshare incoming data as a resharer — Re-sharing of received data is supported only via listings with resharing.enabled=true; direct shares cannot be re-shared. Confirms a controlled-but-real onward-share path. — <https://docs.snowflake.com/en/collaboration/resharing-as-resharer>
- *product:* Snowflake docs - Manage reader accounts — Provider creates reader accounts to share with consumers who lack Snowflake; the data lands in an account the consumer operates, illustrating mount-into-second-account semantics. — <https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-reader-create>
- *product:* Google Cloud - Introduction to BigQuery sharing (Analytics Hub) — Subscribing creates a read-only linked dataset (a pointer) in the subscriber's project that can be combined with their own data; onward exposure governed only by the subscriber. — <https://docs.cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/analytics-hub-introduction>

**Detection (technical).** Provider side: monitor Delta Sharing server / recipient REST access logs for credential use from new source IPs or ASNs not on the recipient's declared IP access list, anomalous bulk table enumeration, and pre-signed URL fetch spikes; enforce short token TTL with rotation and alert on token-age. In Snowflake, audit ACCOUNT_USAGE / LISTING telemetry for resharing.enabled listings and reader-account creation tied to a share; in BigQuery, use Audit Logs + INFORMATION_SCHEMA on Analytics Hub to track linked-dataset creation and subscriber exports, and apply VPC Service Controls to bound egress.

**References.** [Create a recipient object using bearer tokens (Databricks-to-Open sharing) - Databricks on AWS](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/delta-sharing/create-recipient-token) · [Security Best Practices for Delta Sharing - Databricks Blog](https://www.databricks.com/blog/2022/08/01/security-best-practices-for-delta-sharing.html) · [delta-sharing PROTOCOL.md - delta-io/delta-sharing](https://github.com/delta-io/delta-sharing/blob/main/PROTOCOL.md) · [Reshare incoming data as a resharer - Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/collaboration/resharing-as-resharer) · [Introduction to BigQuery sharing - Google Cloud Documentation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/analytics-hub-introduction)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Lakehouse data sharing (Snowflake Secure Data Sharing, Databricks Delta Sharing, BigQuery Analytics Hub) is the dominant 2025-2027 mechanism for cross-org data exchange, and its trust model is per-edge: the provider authorizes a recipient and reasons about that one bilateral grant, while the protocol (especially open Delta Sharing with long-lived recipient bearer tokens) and the recipient's own re-share rights let access propagate transitively to accounts the provider never evaluated. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority across a share graph the provider cannot observe: the provider's access decision is trusted to bound who can read the data, but onward re-share, secondary metastore mounts, and token leakage break that bound while every hop remains a sanctioned, separately-logged platform operation. This is distinct from the SaaS-OAuth transitive-scope gap (which composes scopes across first-party app integrations in one tenant) and from generic cloud bucket exposure because the trust object is specifically the lakehouse share-grant edge and its assumed terminality across organizational data-marketplace boundaries. There is no cross-provider standard that propagates or revokes share authority transitively, no provider-side visibility into recipient re-sharing, and detection of onward propagation is absent.

**How to validate it.** Using Databricks Delta Sharing (or Snowflake share / BigQuery Analytics Hub): provider P grants recipient R read on object O. As R, re-expose O - mount into a second metastore, create a new open Delta Sharing recipient with a bearer token, or re-list in an analytics hub - to a third account never authorized by P. Confirm the third account reads O and that P's audit log shows only the original P-to-R edge with no record of the onward propagation.

**Refuted if.** The platform forbids onward re-sharing of received shares (no re-share / no recursive grant), or surfaces the onward edge to the original provider's audit, so the provider can see and control transitive exposure.

**Prerequisites.** Control or compromise of a downstream recipient account/workspace, or a leaked Delta Sharing bearer token, or write access to an analytics-hub listing.

**Existing controls.** Unity Catalog and Snowflake restrict some re-share scenarios and log share operations under the recipient's account; provider-side visibility into transitive propagation is genuinely limited. Open Delta Sharing bearer tokens are a known leakage vector.

**Proof-of-concept — Lakehouse share-graph audit: transitive cross-workspace re-share (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. READ-ONLY audit of a sample share-grant graph (Delta Sharing / Unity-style) for transitive re-share to an external recipient.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-lakehouse-cross-workspace-share-transitive-grant
# READ-ONLY graph audit. Detects a path where a share granted to workspace B is
# re-shared by B to an EXTERNAL recipient -> data reaches a principal A never approved.
GRAPH = {  # share -> recipients (a recipient may itself re-share)
  "share:pii_gold": ["ws:analytics"],
  "ws:analytics":   ["ws:partner_ext"],     # re-share (allowed_recipients not pinned)
  "ws:partner_ext": ["recipient:vendor@external.test"],
}
INTERNAL = {"ws:analytics"}
def reachable(start):
    seen, stack = set(), [start]
    while stack:
        n = stack.pop()
        for r in GRAPH.get(n, []):
            if r not in seen:
                seen.add(r); stack.append(r)
    return seen
if __name__ == "__main__":
    leaves = reachable("share:pii_gold")
    external = [r for r in leaves if r.startswith("recipient:") or "ext" in r]
    print(f"share=share:pii_gold direct_grant=ws:analytics(internal)")
    print(f"transitively_reachable={sorted(leaves)}")
    print(f"external_recipients={external}")
    if external:
        print("VULNERABLE: pii share transitively re-shared to external recipient (unapproved fanout)")
    else:
        print("PASS: no transitive external re-share")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: pii share transitively re-shared to external recipient' and lists vendor@external.test as transitively reachable.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only)  
*Safety:* Read-only traversal of an in-memory sample graph; no lakehouse/catalog API calls.

**Research hook.** Build a share-graph mapper that, from a provider vantage, enumerates recipients and infers onward re-share and token exposure across Snowflake/Databricks/BigQuery sharing; demonstrate that a scoped partner grant reaches unauthorized accounts via recipient re-share or a leaked Delta Sharing token, then propose transitive grant provenance and revocation plus provider-visible re-share telemetry.


## 108. Ciphertext-replay decrypt oracle under a stolen KMS grantee identity

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-kms-decrypt-grant-confused-deputy-envelope-reuse` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | Cloud Control Plane + Identity & Federation → Cloud Control Plane + Host / Endpoint |
| **Category** | Credential Access; Cloud Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (high confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1555.006, T1078.004, CWE-639, CWE-863 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** A managed KMS (AWS KMS / GCP Cloud KMS / Azure Key Vault) treats a Decrypt grant as authority bound to the named principal and trusts that whoever holds that principal's identity is the intended caller, so it will unwrap any ciphertext blob presented to it under that key without checking which workload, request context, or data-object the decrypt is for - and because envelope encryption reuses one data-key-encrypting key across thousands of objects, a single granted Decrypt right is implicitly an oracle for the entire envelope-encrypted corpus.

**The attack.** An attacker who obtains the grantee role (a compromised compute identity, an assumed cross-account role, or a confused-deputy service that decrypts on the caller's behalf) replays arbitrary ciphertext blobs from a stolen storage tier to the KMS and harvests plaintext at will, because KMS authorizes on principal identity rather than on the legitimacy of this specific decrypt; encryption-context conditions, where they exist, are unset or attacker-supplied, so one decrypt grant decrypts everything the envelope key ever wrapped rather than only the object the grant was intended for.

**Mechanism (technical).** KMS Decrypt authorization is bound to the calling principal and the key, not to which object/ciphertext is being decrypted. With envelope encryption, one KEK (CMK) typically wraps many data keys across many objects, so any principal (or grantee) allowed to Decrypt under that KEK can decrypt any ciphertext encrypted under it - the key becomes a universal decrypt oracle. AWS KMS offers an optional encryption context (AAD) cryptographically bound to the ciphertext, and grant constraints (EncryptionContextEquals/EncryptionContextSubset) plus the kms:EncryptionContext: policy condition can scope decrypt to a specific object context, but these are commonly left unset. When a grantee identity is stolen or a cross-account grant is over-broad and no encryption-context condition is enforced, the attacker replays captured ciphertext to the Decrypt API and recovers plaintext (a confused-deputy / ciphertext-replay pattern).

**Exploitation primitive.** Assume or steal a KMS grantee/role identity that holds Decrypt on a shared KEK, then submit arbitrary captured ciphertext blobs (data keys / envelope-wrapped objects) to kms:Decrypt - absent an EncryptionContext constraint, KMS returns plaintext for objects the principal was never meant to read.

**MITRE gap.** T1555.006 (Credentials from Cloud Secrets Management Stores) covers pulling secrets out of Secrets Manager/Key Vault, and T1078.004 (Cloud Accounts) covers abusing a cloud identity, but neither models KMS-as-decrypt-oracle: the attacker never reads the key material and may not touch a secrets store - they replay ciphertext against a cryptographic API under a valid-but-misused authorization. ATT&CK lacks a technique for 'cryptographic operation authorized on principal+key but not on object,' for envelope-key reuse blast radius, or for the missing-encryption-context confused deputy. T1550/T1098 partially touch credential misuse and grant manipulation but do not capture the cipher-replay-without-key-exfil primitive.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* AWS KMS - Encryption context — Encryption context is an optional, non-secret AAD set cryptographically bound to the ciphertext; the same context is required to decrypt. Being optional is precisely why object-scoping is frequently absent. — <https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/encrypt_context.html>
- *product:* AWS KMS - Grants (encryption context grant constraints) — EncryptionContextEquals and EncryptionContextSubset constrain a grant's effective permissions to requests whose encryption context matches; these scope Decrypt to specific object contexts when used. — <https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/grants.html>
- *product:* AWS KMS - Least-privilege permissions / Decrypt guidance — Warns that broad IAM Decrypt lets a principal decrypt ciphertext from KMS keys in other accounts if those key policies allow it; recommends key-policy scoping over IAM and limiting to particular keys/accounts. — <https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/least-privilege.html>
- *product:* AWS KMS - AWS global condition keys (aws:SourceArn / aws:SourceAccount for confused-deputy prevention) — Recommends combining aws:SourceArn/aws:SourceAccount with kms:EncryptionContext condition keys when the principal is a service principal, to avoid a confused-deputy scenario. — <https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/conditions-aws.html>
- *product:* AWS Decrypt API reference — Decrypt accepts a ciphertext blob and optional EncryptionContext; without context binding, any principal with Decrypt on the key can decrypt any blob under it. — <https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_Decrypt.html>

**Detection (technical).** Alert on CloudTrail kms:Decrypt / kms:GenerateDataKey events where the requesting principal, encryptionContext, or source IP/role deviates from baseline for that key; flag Decrypt calls lacking the expected encryption-context keys, high-cardinality Decrypt fan-out (one principal decrypting many distinct ciphertexts), and CreateGrant events that omit EncryptionContext constraints or grant cross-account principals. Enforce kms:EncryptionContext:context-key conditions and per-object/per-tenant encryption contexts so a stolen grant cannot decrypt outside its scope.

**References.** [Encryption context - AWS Key Management Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/encrypt_context.html) · [Grants in AWS KMS (encryption context grant constraints) - AWS KMS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/grants.html) · [Least-privilege permissions - AWS Key Management Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/least-privilege.html) · [AWS global condition keys - AWS Key Management Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/conditions-aws.html) · [Decrypt - AWS KMS API Reference](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_Decrypt.html)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Cloud envelope encryption is near-universal for data-at-rest in 2025-2027, and KMS authorization is identity-centric: a Decrypt grant authorizes a principal, not a particular object or workload, so any holder of that principal can present any ciphertext wrapped by the same KEK and get plaintext, turning the KMS into a decryption oracle for the whole envelope corpus. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority decoupled from the data being decrypted: encryption-context binding exists but is widely unset or controlled by the caller, and confused-deputy services that decrypt on a caller's behalf launder the request, so the trust 'this grant unlocks the data this caller is entitled to' silently becomes 'this grant unlocks everything the key wrapped.' This is distinct from the secrets-lease-rotation-window gap (a timing race on lease validity) and from generic stolen-credential use because the trust object is the KMS decrypt grant's implicit scoping to a single intended decrypt, broken by ciphertext replay and KEK reuse. Detection that flags anomalous decrypt volume or ciphertext from outside the grantee's normal object set is nascent, and there is no enforced object-level binding on most envelope schemes.

**How to validate it.** In AWS KMS (or GCP/Azure equivalent), create a KMS key used for envelope encryption across many objects with NO encryption-context condition on the key policy/grant. Assume the grantee role (simulate compromise) and replay arbitrary wrapped data-key ciphertext blobs harvested from the storage tier to kms:Decrypt. Confirm KMS returns plaintext for blobs the grant was never intended to cover, demonstrating principal-only authorization acting as a corpus-wide oracle.

**Refuted if.** The key policy/grants bind decrypt to a non-attacker-controllable EncryptionContext (kms:EncryptionContext condition) tied to the specific object, so replayed blobs from other objects fail authorization - the decrypt is scoped, not a blanket oracle.

**Prerequisites.** Obtaining the grantee identity (compromised compute role, assumed cross-account role, or a confused-deputy service that decrypts on the caller's behalf) plus read access to the ciphertext storage tier.

**Existing controls.** KMS encryption-context conditions, grant constraints, per-tenant/per-object keys, and confused-deputy mitigations (aws:SourceArn/SourceAccount) directly address this; CloudTrail Decrypt monitoring exists. The seam is real where encryption context is unused, which is common.

**Proof-of-concept — KMS decrypt grant confused-deputy: grant authorizes principal not ciphertext (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of an envelope-encryption KMS where a decrypt grant checks only the calling principal, allowing replay of any ciphertext.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-kms-decrypt-grant-confused-deputy-envelope-reuse
# Local STUB. KMS Decrypt grant authorizes a PRINCIPAL but not a specific data key /
# encryption-context. A service holding the grant decrypts ANOTHER tenant's envelope
# (ciphertext replay / confused deputy).
GRANTS = {"svc-report@example.test": {"key":"key-shared","ops":["Decrypt"]}}  # no EncryptionContext constraint
ENVELOPES = {
  "env-tenantA": {"key":"key-shared","ctx":{"tenant":"A"},"ct":"FAKE-CIPHERTEXT-A"},
  "env-tenantB": {"key":"key-shared","ctx":{"tenant":"B"},"ct":"FAKE-CIPHERTEXT-B"},  # not svc's data
}
def kms_decrypt(principal, envelope):
    g = GRANTS.get(principal)
    env = ENVELOPES[envelope]
    if not g or env["key"] != g["key"] or "Decrypt" not in g["ops"]:
        return ("DENY", None)
    # VULN PATTERN: no per-object/encryption-context binding -> any envelope decrypts
    return ("PLAINTEXT", f"decrypted {envelope} ctx={env['ctx']}")
if __name__ == "__main__":
    principal="svc-report@example.test"
    status, pt = kms_decrypt(principal, "env-tenantB")  # service replays tenant B's blob
    print(f"principal={principal} grant_constraints=ops-only(no EncryptionContext)")
    print(f"target_envelope=env-tenantB result={status} :: {pt}")
    if status == "PLAINTEXT":
        print("VULNERABLE: principal-scoped decrypt grant authorized cross-tenant ciphertext (confused deputy)")
    else:
        print("PASS: decrypt bound to encryption-context/object")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: principal-scoped decrypt grant authorized cross-tenant ciphertext' -- svc decrypted env-tenantB it shouldn't own.  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* FAKE-CIPHERTEXT strings, in-process KMS stub; no real KMS/HSM, no real keys.

**Research hook.** Audit real envelope-encryption deployments for unset or attacker-controlled encryption-context and KEK reuse breadth; build a decrypt-oracle harness that, given a grantee role, recovers plaintext from replayed ciphertext across objects the grant never intended, and propose mandatory object-scoped encryption-context binding plus KMS-side anomaly detection on decrypt request provenance.


## 109. Cross-topic subscription via wildcard or shared-principal consumer ACL

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-message-bus-topic-authz-broad-consumer-read` |
| **Primitive** | P2 Identity->Authority |
| **Trust frame** | Network + Identity & Federation → Cloud Control Plane + SOC / Defense |
| **Category** | Collection; Cloud Attacks |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1530, T1078.004, CWE-863, CWE-732 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An event-streaming bus (Kafka / Pub/Sub / Kinesis / EventBridge) trusts that a workload granted consumer access to the cluster will read only the topics its application logic concerns, so authorization is configured coarsely - wildcard topic ACLs, a shared service principal across microservices, or default 'read all groups' grants - on the assumption that producers and consumers are cooperative internal services and that topic-level segregation is a convenience rather than a security boundary.

**The attack.** An attacker who compromises any one consumer workload (or its shared service credential) attaches a consumer to topics far outside that service's purpose and silently tees the firehose of business events - PII, transactional records, internal audit and security events flowing through the same bus - because the broker authorizes the principal for the cluster/wildcard rather than for the specific topic this service legitimately consumes, turning the central nervous system of the architecture into a single-credential collection point that produces no application-visible anomaly.

**Mechanism (technical).** Event-bus brokers authorize a consuming principal against a resource pattern that is coarser than a single topic: a Kafka ACL granted with resource-pattern-type PREFIXED or the LITERAL wildcard '*', a Pub/Sub IAM role bound at the project/subscription tier, or an AWS IAM policy with a wildcard Resource on Kinesis/EventBridge. Because authorization is evaluated on the pattern, not on data sensitivity, a single principal entitled to a prefix or wildcard can subscribe to every topic that matches. A compromised consumer (or its leaked credential) then tees the full firehose - PII streams, audit/CDC events, security telemetry - without ever touching the producers or the data store. The trust boundary that operators believe is 'per-topic' is in reality 'per-pattern'.

**Exploitation primitive.** With a credential holding READ on a PREFIXED ('app.') or wildcard topic ACL plus the consumer-group ACL, attach a new consumer group and read all matching topics from offset 0, draining historical and live records. Equivalently, a Pub/Sub principal with roles/pubsub.subscriber broad at project scope, or a Kinesis IAM principal with stream wildcard, reads streams it was never meant to see.

**MITRE gap.** Closest techniques are T1530 (Data from Cloud Storage) and T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts). Both are insufficient: T1530 models pull from object stores (S3/GCS), not the continuous pub/sub subscription semantics of an event bus where the abuse is a legitimate, authorized read against an over-broad ACL pattern rather than an at-rest object fetch. T1078.004 captures that a valid account is used but says nothing about the resource-pattern granularity gap (wildcard/prefix ACL, shared service principal, consumer-group authz) that is the actual vulnerability. ATT&CK has no technique for streaming-bus topic authorization scope or for 'one principal tees many topics' as a collection primitive.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *standard:* Apache Kafka KIP-290: Support for Prefixed ACLs — Introduces PREFIXED resource-pattern-type alongside LITERAL/wildcard; a prefixed ACL authorizes a principal for every topic sharing the prefix, the root of over-broad consumer read. — <https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-290:+Support+for+Prefixed+ACLs>
- *product:* Confluent Platform - ACL authorization overview — Documents LITERAL/PREFIXED/wildcard resource patterns and that consumers need both topic READ and consumer-group READ; wildcard User:* warned against. — <https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/security/authorization/acls/overview.html>
- *product:* Apache Kafka - Authorization and ACLs — Primary spec for ACL semantics, resource pattern types and wildcard '*' principal/resource grants. — <https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#security_authz>

**Detection (technical).** Alert on creation of a new consumer group / subscription by a principal that then reads across many distinct topics or resets to offset 0 on sensitive topics; audit broker ACL inventory for PREFIXED or '*' literal resource patterns and User:* principals (Kafka kafka-acls --list), and for Pub/Sub/Kinesis IAM bindings granting subscriber/read at project or wildcard-resource scope. Baseline per-principal topic fan-out and flag deviations.

**References.** [KIP-290: Support for Prefixed ACLs - Apache Kafka](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-290:+Support+for+Prefixed+ACLs) · [Use ACLs for authorization - Confluent Platform](https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/security/authorization/acls/overview.html) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1078.004 Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1078/004/) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1530 Data from Cloud Storage](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1530/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Event buses are the backbone of modern data architectures and carry nearly every category of business data in one place, yet ACL practice defaults to coarse grants - Kafka's default consumer-group ACL is the wildcard '*', shared service principals are common, and managed buses often gate at the cluster or project rather than per-topic - so any single consumer foothold can subscribe broadly. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority at the wrong granularity: the bus trusts a consumer principal to self-restrict to its relevant topics, while authorization is enforced at cluster/wildcard scope, so one compromised consumer reads the entire event firehose. This is distinct from cloud-storage collection and from the lakehouse share gap because the trust object is the streaming broker's topic-level consumer authorization and its assumption of cooperative least-privilege consumption, and because the data is in motion - a passive tee leaves no object-access trail and no application error. Per-topic least-privilege enforcement is rarely audited, and detection that a known consumer has begun reading topics outside its historical pattern is essentially absent across the major buses.

**How to validate it.** In a Kafka (or Pub/Sub/Kinesis/EventBridge) cluster configured with a wildcard topic ACL or a shared service principal across microservices, use one consumer's credential to subscribe to topics outside that service's purpose (PII, audit, security-event topics on the same bus). Confirm the broker authorizes the read based on the cluster/wildcard grant rather than the specific topic, and that consuming produces no application-visible anomaly.

**Refuted if.** The broker enforces least-privilege per-topic ACLs per principal (no wildcard, no shared principal), so the compromised consumer is denied topics outside its grant - topic segregation is a real security boundary.

**Prerequisites.** Compromise of any one consumer workload or its (often shared) service credential on a bus that uses wildcard/coarse ACLs.

**Existing controls.** Kafka ACLs/RBAC (Confluent), Pub/Sub IAM per-topic, Kinesis/EventBridge resource policies all support least privilege; the gap is operational coarse configuration. Consumer-lag/new-consumer-group monitoring can detect unexpected subscriptions.

**Proof-of-concept — Message-bus ACL audit: wildcard consumer can read sensitive topics (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. READ-ONLY audit of a sample Kafka/PubSub ACL set for wildcard/broad consumer read grants.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-message-bus-topic-authz-broad-consumer-read
# READ-ONLY ACL audit. Flags consumer principals granted READ on a wildcard topic
# pattern that subsumes sensitive topics (broad consumer read).
ACLS = [
  {"principal":"svc-metrics@example.test","op":"READ","topic":"metrics.*"},
  {"principal":"svc-etl@example.test","op":"READ","topic":"*"},          # wildcard ALL
  {"principal":"svc-orders@example.test","op":"READ","topic":"orders.events"},
]
SENSITIVE = ["payments.card", "pii.profile", "orders.events"]
def covers(pattern, topic):
    if pattern == "*": return True
    if pattern.endswith(".*"): return topic.startswith(pattern[:-1])
    return pattern == topic
if __name__ == "__main__":
    findings = []
    for a in ACLS:
        if a["op"] != "READ": continue
        reached = [t for t in SENSITIVE if covers(a["topic"], t)]
        if a["topic"] in ("*",) or (reached and a["topic"] != reached[0]):
            findings.append((a["principal"], a["topic"], reached))
    for p, pat, reached in findings:
        print(f"principal={p} grant=READ:{pat} reaches_sensitive={reached}")
    if any(pat == "*" or len(r) for _,pat,r in findings):
        print("VULNERABLE: broad/wildcard consumer ACL can read sensitive topics")
    else:
        print("PASS: consumer ACLs scoped to intended topics")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: broad/wildcard consumer ACL can read sensitive topics' -- svc-etl with topic '*' reaches payments.card/pii.profile.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only)  
*Safety:* Read-only evaluation of an in-memory ACL list; no broker connection.

**Research hook.** Build a bus-authorization analyzer that resolves effective per-principal topic reachability across Kafka/Pub-Sub/Kinesis/EventBridge and flags wildcard or shared-principal over-grants; demonstrate firehose collection from a single compromised consumer, and prototype broker-side detection that baselines per-consumer topic sets and alarms on subscription to topics outside the service's historical footprint.


## 110. Offline feature poisoning promoted to online serving via materialization

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-feature-store-offline-to-online-poison-promotion` |
| **Primitive** | P4 Context-Inheritance |
| **Trust frame** | Supply Chain / Build + Vector Store / RAG Index → AI Agent + Cloud Control Plane |
| **Category** | AI / Agent Attacks; Collection |
| **Validity** | plausible (low confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1565.001, CWE-345, CWE-20 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | frontier / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** An ML feature store guarantees offline/online parity precisely so the online serving layer trusts that a feature value materialized from the offline store is the same vetted value the model trained on, so the online path ingests materialized features as already-validated, provenance-clean inputs and applies no independent integrity or anomaly check at serving time - the parity guarantee is treated as proof of trustworthiness, not merely of equality.

**The attack.** An attacker who can write to the offline feature pipeline (a poisoned upstream source table, a tampered batch transform, or a compromised feature-definition job) injects manipulated feature values that the materialization process then faithfully promotes into the online store, so the poison inherits the parity guarantee's trust and is served to production models in real time - steering high-stakes decisions (fraud scoring, eligibility, agent actions) - while every consistency check passes because offline and online agree exactly on the attacker's value.

**Mechanism (technical).** ML feature stores (Feast, Tecton, Databricks Feature Store) maintain an offline store (historical, training/batch) and an online store (low-latency serving), and a materialization job snapshots the latest offline feature values into the online store. Operators treat offline/online parity - that the same feature value appears in both - as evidence of trustworthiness, when parity only proves equality of bytes, not integrity of provenance. An attacker who poisons feature values in the offline store (or in the upstream source feeding it) has that poison faithfully promoted to live online serving by the very materialization pipeline that establishes parity. The poison thus inherits the trust normally conferred by consistency checks and is served to production inference.

**Exploitation primitive.** Write tampered feature values into the offline store table (or the source that materialization reads), then let the scheduled or triggered materialize/materialize-incremental job propagate them into the online store, where they are served at inference and can flip decisions or embed a triggerable backdoor.

**MITRE gap.** Closest is T1565.001 (Stored Data Manipulation) in ATT&CK and AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data) in ATLAS. T1565.001 captures generic at-rest tampering but is blind to the feature-store-specific promotion path: the offline-to-online materialization that launders poison into live serving and the parity-as-trust assumption. AML.T0020 frames poisoning as a training-time/model-corruption attack, whereas here the harm is at serving time on already-trained models via fresh feature values, and ATLAS has no technique for feature-store materialization or online-serving feature integrity. Neither models 'offline/online parity treated as a trust signal' as the exploited control.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *research:* MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020 - Poison Training/AI Data — Nearest ATLAS technique; covers corrupting data to manipulate model behavior but framed at training data, not feature-store online materialization. — <https://atlas.mitre.org/techniques/AML.T0020>
- *product:* Feast documentation - materialization / feature retrieval — Materialize snapshots latest offline feature values into the online store for serving; the promotion channel that propagates offline poison live. — <https://docs.feast.dev/>
- *standard:* MITRE ATT&CK T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation — Generic at-rest data manipulation; closest enterprise technique but not feature-store/materialization aware. — <https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1565/001/>

**Detection (technical).** Compute and alert on distributional drift / value-range and checksum deltas on feature values between successive materialization runs and between offline source and online store; gate materialization on data-validation (e.g., expectation suites) and require provenance/signing on the offline source rather than relying on offline-online equality as proof of integrity. Monitor for out-of-band writes to offline feature tables outside the registered transformation DAG.

**References.** [MITRE ATLAS - AML.T0020 Poison Training Data](https://atlas.mitre.org/techniques/AML.T0020) · [Feast documentation](https://docs.feast.dev/) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1565/001/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Feature stores (Feast, Tecton, Databricks Feature Store) exist to enforce offline/online parity, and 2025-2026 practice treats that parity as the integrity guarantee for serving inputs - the online layer trusts materialized features without re-validating provenance because the store promises they match training. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: the trust earned by a feature in the offline/training context is inherited wholesale into the online serving context through materialization, so a value poisoned upstream is faithfully promoted and served while parity checks - the only checks present - confirm the poison rather than catch it. This is distinct from classic training-data poisoning (which corrupts a model's weights at train time) because here the live online inference path is steered through the materialization promotion gate, and distinct from RAG/embedding poisoning because the trust object is the feature-store parity contract treated as a provenance guarantee. There is no provenance attestation on materialized features and no serving-time anomaly detection that distinguishes a legitimate feature shift from injected poison that satisfies parity.

**How to validate it.** In a Feast/Tecton/Databricks feature store, write a manipulated feature value into the offline store (poison an upstream source table or tamper a batch transform). Trigger materialization to the online store. Query the online serving endpoint and confirm the poisoned value is served to the model in real time, and that offline/online parity checks pass because both stores hold the attacker's identical value - no integrity/anomaly gate fires at serving time.

**Refuted if.** The serving path applies independent integrity/provenance/anomaly validation on materialized features (range checks, drift detection, signed feature provenance) that flags or rejects the poisoned value despite parity - i.e. parity is not treated as proof of trustworthiness.

**Prerequisites.** Write access to the offline feature pipeline: a source table, the batch transform job, or the feature-definition job.

**Existing controls.** Data-quality frameworks (Great Expectations, Deequ), feature drift/anomaly monitoring, and pipeline access controls exist but are typically training-time, not enforced as a serving-time integrity gate. Data/feature poisoning is an established ML-security concern (MITRE ATLAS) though this specific offline-to-online parity-laundering vector lacks a public demo.

**Proof-of-concept — Feature-store offline->online: parity check trusted as integrity gate (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of a feature-store promotion that trusts offline==online parity instead of verifying source provenance.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-feature-store-offline-to-online-poison-promotion
# Local STUB. Promotion gate only checks offline/online VALUE PARITY, not provenance.
# A poisoned offline value promotes to the online store because parity 'passes'.
OFFLINE = {"user_risk_score": {"value": 0.02, "source":"unsigned-batch-job"}}  # poisoned (was 0.95)
def promote(feature):
    f = OFFLINE[feature]
    online = dict(f)              # online materialized from offline
    parity_ok = (f["value"] == online["value"])   # tautological 'integrity' check
    provenance_verified = False    # no signature/lineage check performed
    if parity_ok:
        return ("PROMOTED", f["value"], provenance_verified)
    return ("BLOCKED", None, provenance_verified)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    status, val, prov = promote("user_risk_score")
    print(f"gate=offline/online_parity parity_ok=True provenance_verified={prov}")
    print(f"result={status} online_value={val}")
    if status == "PROMOTED" and not prov:
        print("VULNERABLE: poisoned feature promoted to online store via parity-as-integrity gate")
    else:
        print("PASS: provenance verified before promotion")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: poisoned feature promoted to online store via parity-as-integrity gate' (result=PROMOTED, provenance_verified=False).  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process feature dicts; no Feast/Tecton/online store contacted.

**Research hook.** Demonstrate end-to-end poisoning that enters an offline feature source, survives a materialization job, and biases a live online prediction while all parity checks pass; then prototype feature-provenance attestation carried through materialization and serving-time anomaly detection that scores feature distributions against attested baselines rather than against the (equally-poisoned) offline copy.


## 111. Production-stage alias rebinding to a substituted model artifact

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-model-registry-promotion-gate-artifact-substitution` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Supply Chain / Build + Container / K8s → AI Agent + Cloud Control Plane |
| **Category** | MLOps & Model Supply Chain; CI/CD & Build Pipeline |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1525, T1195.002, CWE-345, CWE-494 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / none |

**The trust assumption.** An MLOps model registry treats a model's promotion to a 'production' stage/alias as an authoritative attestation that this exact artifact passed evaluation and approval, so the serving infrastructure resolves 'the production model' by stage label and loads whatever bytes the registry currently points that label at, trusting that the promoted artifact is byte-identical to the one that was evaluated and that the label binds immutably to that evaluation.

**The attack.** An attacker who can write to the registry or its backing artifact store - a compromised registry credential, a mutable stage alias, or unsigned artifact storage - rebinds the production label to a substituted or backdoored model after evaluation, or swaps the artifact bytes underneath an unchanged label, so the serving layer loads an unevaluated artifact under the trust of a promotion gate it never re-checks; because consumers resolve by label rather than by a signed digest of the evaluated artifact, the malicious model is served as 'the approved production model' with no re-evaluation.

**Mechanism (technical).** Model registries (MLflow Model Registry, SageMaker Model Registry) resolve the production model by a mutable label - a stage ('Production') or, in current MLflow, a version alias - rather than by a signed content digest. Serving infrastructure trusts 'whatever the Production alias points to', and the underlying artifact bytes live in unsigned artifact storage. An attacker who can rebind the alias/stage, or who can overwrite the artifact object after evaluation has passed, substitutes a backdoored model that inherits the promotion gate's blessing. Because MLflow loads many flavors via Python pickle/cloudpickle, the substituted artifact can additionally execute arbitrary code on load (CVE-2024-37052 / CVE-2024-37054), turning artifact substitution into RCE on the serving host.

**Exploitation primitive.** After a clean model passes the evaluation gate, overwrite the stored artifact (e.g., PUT a malicious cloudpickle python_model.pkl to the artifact store) or rebind the 'Production'/champion alias to an attacker-trained version, so the next load_model() serves backdoored bytes - and, with pickle flavors, executes the payload at deserialization.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1525 (Implant Internal Image), T1195.002 (Compromise Software Supply Chain) and ATLAS AML.T0010 (AI Supply Chain Compromise). T1525 targets cloud/container images, not model-registry aliases or serialized model artifacts, and does not model 'serving resolves by mutable label not signed digest'. T1195.002 is build/distribution-time and external-supply-chain oriented, missing the post-evaluation, in-registry alias-rebind / artifact-swap that defeats an internal promotion gate. AML.T0010 covers supply-chain compromise of models but does not capture the registry's stage/alias resolution-by-label weakness or the post-gate time-of-check/time-of-use substitution. None encode 'promotion gate bound to mutable label rather than signed artifact digest'.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *cve:* CVE-2024-37052 — MLflow scikit-learn model deserialization RCE (CWE-502, CVSS 8.8); malicious model artifact executes code on load. — <https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-37052>
- *cve:* CVE-2024-37054 — MLflow PyFunc model pickle deserialization RCE; attacker overwrites python_model.pkl artifact, executes on load_model(). — <https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-37054>
- *product:* MLflow Model Registry - stages deprecated in favor of aliases — MLflow deprecated fixed stages (Staging/Production) for mutable version aliases; serving resolves by label, motivating signed-digest binding. — <https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/ml/model-registry/workflow/>
- *research:* MITRE ATLAS AML.T0010 AI Supply Chain Compromise — Nearest ATLAS technique for compromised models; does not model registry alias resolution or post-gate substitution. — <https://atlas.mitre.org/techniques/AML.T0010>

**Detection (technical).** Require and verify cryptographic signatures/digests on model artifacts at load (e.g., model signing / Sigstore), and alert on any alias/stage transition or artifact-object overwrite that is not produced by the approved promotion pipeline (registry audit log + object-store versioning/immutability). Detect pickle-based flavors and enforce safe-format loading; monitor serving hosts for child-process spawn at model-load time.

**References.** [NVD - CVE-2024-37052 (MLflow sklearn deserialization RCE)](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-37052) · [GitHub Advisory GHSA-ghv6-9r9j-wh4j - CVE-2024-37054](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-ghv6-9r9j-wh4j) · [MLflow Model Registry Workflows (stages -> aliases)](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/ml/model-registry/workflow/) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1525 Implant Internal Image](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1525/) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1195.002 Compromise Software Supply Chain](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1195/002/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Model registries (MLflow, SageMaker, Vertex, Databricks Unity Catalog models) gate production via mutable stage/alias labels, and the dominant 2025-2027 serving pattern resolves the production model by label at deploy/load time, trusting the label as proof the artifact was evaluated and approved. The unmodeled seam is provenance: the promotion event is trusted as an immutable attestation binding label to evaluated bytes, but labels are mutable pointers and backing artifact stores are often unsigned, so the binding between 'evaluated' and 'served' can be broken after the gate without re-evaluation. This is distinct from model-hub deserialization-trust gaps (malicious load-time code) and from leaderboard/eval-overfit gaps because the trust object is specifically the registry promotion gate as a binding attestation - the attacker need not subvert evaluation, only rebind the label or swap bytes afterward. There is little use of signed-digest pinning between evaluation and serving, and detection that a served model's digest diverges from the evaluated artifact is essentially absent.

**How to validate it.** In an MLflow (or SageMaker/Vertex) model registry, evaluate and promote model v1 to the 'production' alias/stage. Then, with registry write access (or write to the backing artifact store), rebind the production alias to a substituted backdoored model, or overwrite the artifact bytes under the unchanged alias. Restart/poll the serving layer that resolves 'production' by label and confirm it loads the unevaluated artifact with no re-evaluation, because consumers resolve by label not by a signed digest.

**Refuted if.** The serving layer pins and verifies a signed digest of the evaluated artifact (e.g. cosign/model signing, immutable versioned artifacts, MLflow model signing), so a swapped artifact under the same label is rejected and never served.

**Prerequisites.** Write access to the registry (compromised credential, mutable alias permission) or to unsigned backing artifact storage.

**Existing controls.** Model signing (Sigstore model-transparency / cosign), immutable model versions, artifact-store integrity, and promotion approval audit exist but signed-digest enforcement at load time is rarely wired up. Supply-chain artifact substitution is a well-understood class.

**Proof-of-concept — Model-registry promotion gate: stage tag trusted, artifact digest unverified (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of a model registry that promotes by stage tag without re-verifying the artifact digest against the signed evaluation.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-model-registry-promotion-gate-artifact-substitution
# Local STUB. 'Production' promotion trusts the stage TAG; the underlying artifact
# digest is swapped after eval (TOCTOU). Gate never re-binds eval->digest.
import hashlib
EVALUATED = {"model:fraud:v7": {"digest":"sha256:GOODGOODGOOD","eval_passed":True}}
REGISTRY  = {"model:fraud:v7": {"stage":"Staging","digest":"sha256:GOODGOODGOOD"}}
def substitute_then_promote(name):
    REGISTRY[name]["digest"] = "sha256:ATTACKERATTACKER"   # artifact swapped post-eval
    ev = EVALUATED[name]
    # VULN PATTERN: promote on eval_passed flag only; do NOT compare current digest
    if ev["eval_passed"]:
        REGISTRY[name]["stage"] = "Production"
        return ("PROMOTED", REGISTRY[name]["digest"], ev["digest"])
    return ("BLOCKED", None, ev["digest"])
if __name__ == "__main__":
    status, served, evaluated = substitute_then_promote("model:fraud:v7")
    print(f"gate=eval_passed_flag evaluated_digest={evaluated} served_digest={served}")
    print(f"result={status}")
    if status == "PROMOTED" and served != evaluated:
        print("VULNERABLE: substituted artifact reached Production; gate did not rebind digest to eval")
    else:
        print("PASS: promotion verified artifact digest matches signed evaluation")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: substituted artifact reached Production; gate did not rebind digest to eval' (served_digest != evaluated_digest).  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process registry stub with fake digests; no MLflow/registry network calls.

**Research hook.** Demonstrate serving a backdoored model by rebinding a production alias or swapping artifact bytes post-evaluation in a popular registry while the promotion record looks clean; propose signed-digest pinning that binds the production label to the exact evaluated artifact and a serving-time admission check that refuses any model whose digest does not match its evaluation attestation.


## 112. Forged lineage edges laundering dataset provenance

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-data-catalog-lineage-spoof-as-provenance` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Supply Chain / Build + Cloud Control Plane → SOC / Defense + Human Operator / User |
| **Category** | Cloud Attacks; Data Governance & Lineage |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1565.001, T1036, CWE-345, CWE-441 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / none |

**The trust assumption.** A data catalog / lineage system (Unity Catalog, Purview, OpenLineage, DataHub) is trusted by governance, compliance, and downstream automation as a faithful record of where each dataset came from and how it was derived, so classification labels, quality/certification badges, PII tags, and lineage edges are consumed as authoritative provenance - access policies, audit attestations, and trust-this-table decisions are made from the catalog's claims rather than from the data plane itself.

**The attack.** An attacker who can emit lineage events or edit catalog metadata (an unauthenticated/over-permissive OpenLineage ingest endpoint, a compromised cataloging agent, or write access to tags) forges lineage and certification - laundering a poisoned or unsanctioned dataset to appear derived from a trusted certified source, or stripping PII/sensitivity tags so a restricted table looks public - so downstream consumers, policy engines, and auditors trust attacker-authored provenance metadata that the data plane never substantiates, decoupling the recorded story of the data from its actual origin.

**Mechanism (technical).** Data catalogs and lineage backends (Unity Catalog, Microsoft Purview, OpenLineage/Marquez, DataHub) accept lineage events and metadata edits over an HTTP ingest API and then present the resulting graph/tags as authoritative provenance and governance (e.g., 'certified', 'no-PII'). The reference OpenLineage backend, Marquez, by default requires no authentication or authorization on its HTTP API, and DataHub's OpenLineage REST endpoint accepts emitted lineage that is translated directly into the metadata model. An attacker with reach to an unauthenticated/over-permissive ingest endpoint, or with write access to tags, can forge lineage edges to launder a dataset's origin, fabricate certification/quality assertions, or strip PII/sensitivity classifications - masquerading untrusted data as trusted. Downstream consumers and access policies that key off these tags are then deceived.

**Exploitation primitive.** POST crafted OpenLineage run/job/dataset events to an unauthenticated Marquez/DataHub lineage endpoint (or write metadata via an over-scoped catalog token) to create fake upstream edges and overwrite classification/certification tags, making poisoned or unauthorized data appear to originate from a trusted, PII-free source.

**MITRE gap.** Closest are T1565.001 (Stored Data Manipulation) and T1036 (Masquerading). T1565.001 treats the catalog metadata as just 'data at rest' and misses that the manipulated object is provenance/governance metadata whose corruption subverts trust and policy decisions, not the dataset itself. T1036 captures making malicious things look benign but is oriented to files/processes/services, not to forging lineage graph edges or classification tags in a metadata-management plane. ATT&CK has no technique for data-catalog/lineage integrity or 'provenance spoofing as a governance-bypass primitive', and the unauthenticated metadata ingest endpoint as an attack surface is unmodeled.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* Marquez (OpenLineage reference impl) - HTTP API auth — README states: 'By default, the HTTP API does not require any form of authentication or authorization'. — <https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/blob/main/README.md>
- *product:* DataHub - OpenLineage REST endpoint — POST /openapi/openlineage/api/v1/lineage ingests OpenLineage events translated into DataHub metadata; ingest surface for forged lineage if auth/authorization is weak. — <https://docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage>
- *standard:* OpenLineage - open standard / API spec — Defines the run/job/dataset event model POSTed to backends; the wire format an attacker forges. — <https://openlineage.io/>

**Detection (technical).** Require authentication and per-namespace authorization on lineage/metadata ingest endpoints; reject or quarantine OpenLineage events whose producing identity/source is not on an allowlist, and alert on lineage edges or classification/certification tag changes not correlated with an actual pipeline run. Monitor for PII/sensitivity tag removals and for catalog metadata writes from unexpected principals; reconcile asserted lineage against observed query/job logs.

**References.** [Marquez README (default no auth on HTTP API)](https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/blob/main/README.md) · [DataHub - OpenLineage integration](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage) · [OpenLineage - open standard for lineage](https://openlineage.io/) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1036 Masquerading](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1036/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Data catalogs and lineage graphs are increasingly the control plane for data governance in 2025-2027 - access policies, certification trust, and compliance attestations are driven off catalog metadata and emitted lineage (OpenLineage events, scanner-populated tags) - yet that metadata is largely an unauthenticated, easily-emitted side channel decoupled from the data plane it describes. The unmodeled seam is provenance forgery: the catalog's lineage and labels are trusted as ground truth about data origin and sensitivity, but they are attacker-writable assertions, so forged lineage can launder a poisoned dataset as certified-derived and tag-stripping can demote a sensitive table out of policy scope. This is distinct from SLSA build-provenance gaps (artifact build attestation) and from feature-store poisoning because the trust object is the data-catalog's provenance/classification record consumed by governance and humans, not the data or model itself. There is little cryptographic binding between lineage claims and actual data-plane operations, and detection of forged or tampered lineage that contradicts observed reads/writes is absent.

**How to validate it.** Against an OpenLineage ingest endpoint (or DataHub/Purview/Unity Catalog metadata API), emit forged lineage events claiming a poisoned/unsanctioned dataset is derived from a trusted certified source, and strip PII/sensitivity tags from a restricted table. Then confirm a downstream policy engine, access decision, or auditor consuming the catalog treats the attacker-authored provenance as authoritative - granting access or trust the data plane never substantiates.

**Refuted if.** The catalog authenticates and authorizes lineage emitters, validates lineage against the data plane, or downstream policy engines verify provenance independently rather than trusting catalog claims - so forged metadata does not change a trust/access decision.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to emit lineage events to an unauthenticated/over-permissive ingest endpoint, write access to catalog tags, or a compromised cataloging agent.

**Existing controls.** Catalog RBAC on tags/classifications, authenticated OpenLineage transports, and policy-engine validation exist but OpenLineage ingest is often unauthenticated by default and metadata writes are loosely governed. No standard control reconciles catalog claims against the data plane.

**Proof-of-concept — Data-catalog lineage spoof: self-asserted lineage trusted as provenance (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub of a data catalog where a producer self-asserts upstream lineage, consumed as a trust/provenance signal.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-data-catalog-lineage-spoof-as-provenance
# Local STUB. A dataset's lineage is SELF-ASSERTED by the producer (no signed
# attestation linking to the real upstream run). A consumer trusts 'derived_from:
# certified_gold' and grants downstream trust to attacker-controlled data.
CATALOG = {
  "ds:risky_export": {
     "owner":"unknown@example.test",
     "lineage_claim":"derived_from: certified_gold",   # self-asserted string
     "attestation_signature": None,                     # no signed provenance
  }
}
def consumer_trust(dataset):
    d = CATALOG[dataset]
    # VULN PATTERN: trust decision keyed on the lineage CLAIM, not a verified attestation
    trusted = "certified_gold" in d["lineage_claim"]
    verified = d["attestation_signature"] is not None
    return trusted, verified
if __name__ == "__main__":
    trusted, verified = consumer_trust("ds:risky_export")
    print(f"lineage_claim='derived_from: certified_gold' attestation_signature=None")
    print(f"consumer_trusted={trusted} provenance_verified={verified}")
    if trusted and not verified:
        print("VULNERABLE: spoofed self-asserted lineage accepted as provenance (no signed attestation)")
    else:
        print("PASS: provenance requires verified attestation")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: spoofed self-asserted lineage accepted as provenance' (consumer_trusted=True, provenance_verified=False).  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* In-process catalog dict; no catalog API (DataHub/Atlas) contacted.

**Research hook.** Probe lineage ingest endpoints (OpenLineage, catalog APIs) for unauthenticated or over-permissive event emission and tag mutation; demonstrate laundering an unsanctioned dataset to certified-derived status and stripping PII classification to bypass a policy engine, then propose data-plane-attested lineage that reconciles emitted lineage against observed reads/writes and signs classification changes.


## 113. Dormant payload pre-staged into protected backup during dwell time

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-backup-snapshot-integrity-prestaged-restore-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P3 Provenance |
| **Trust frame** | Host / Endpoint + Supply Chain / Build → Cloud Control Plane + Host / Endpoint |
| **Category** | Cloud Attacks; Persistence |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1542, T1505, CWE-345, CWE-506 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / none / nascent |

**The trust assumption.** Disaster-recovery doctrine trusts a backup/snapshot as a clean, point-in-time copy of a known-good state, so the restore path treats snapshot contents as inherently trustworthy ground truth - immutability/object-lock guarantees that the bytes were not altered after write are conflated with a guarantee that the bytes were benign when written, and recovery reintroduces snapshot contents into production with little or no re-scanning of what is actually inside the protected copy.

**The attack.** An attacker with pre-encryption dwell time writes a dormant payload - a backdoor, a logic bomb, or staged credentials - into the production state during the backup window, so it is captured into a snapshot that immutability then faithfully and unalterably preserves; when the victim later restores after an incident, the recovery process reintroduces the attacker's pre-staged implant under the full trust of 'restored from clean backup,' so immutability protects the attacker's payload as diligently as the legitimate data and the restore re-arms the compromise.

**Mechanism (technical).** Object-lock / WORM immutability (AWS Backup Vault Lock compliance mode, S3 Object Lock) is designed to guarantee that recovery points cannot be altered or deleted during the retention window. Because the control faithfully preserves byte-for-byte whatever existed at backup time, malware or a dormant implant pre-staged into a system during attacker dwell time is captured and frozen inside the immutable backup. Ransomware commonly dwells for weeks while nightly jobs preserve the implant alongside good data, so the 'protected' backup becomes an artifact of the compromise. On restore the payload is re-armed, and the very immutability that defeats tampering also prevents anyone from cleaning the poisoned copy in place.

**Exploitation primitive.** During dwell, drop a persistence/backdoor artifact onto a host or dataset before a backup cycle, then let object-lock immutability preserve it; recovery from the 'trusted' immutable vault re-establishes the foothold and reverses containment.

**MITRE gap.** Closest IDs are T1490 (Inhibit System Recovery), T1542 (Pre-OS Boot), and T1505 (Server Software Component), but all are poor fits: T1490 covers destroying/encrypting backups, not preserving a clean-looking poisoned one; T1542/T1505 describe the implant itself, not the backup-immutability trust relationship. ATT&CK has no concept of a recovery/backup pipeline as an integrity-laundering channel where a defensive WORM control becomes the carrier of attacker persistence across a restore boundary.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *product:* AWS Backup Vault Lock (compliance mode WORM) — Vendor doc establishing immutable WORM retention that preserves recovery points unchanged, including whatever was present at backup time. — <https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/vault-lock.html>
- *product:* Amazon S3 Object Lock — WORM immutability primitive underpinning ransomware-resilient backups; preserves object bytes (incl. malware) for the lock duration. — <https://aws.amazon.com/s3/features/object-lock/>
- *product:* GuardDuty Malware Protection for AWS Backup — AWS control that scans backups to identify clean recovery points - implicitly acknowledges backups can carry malware that immutability preserves. — <https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/malware-protection.html>
- *standard:* AWS cyber resilience reference (restore re-establishes attacker foothold) — AWS Architecture Blog guidance noting validation (not just successful restore) is required because backups can preserve dormant ransomware. — <https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/cyber-resilience-on-aws-a-reference-approach-for-recovery-from-ransomware-and-destructive-events/>

**Detection (technical).** Scan recovery points (not just live systems) for malware/IoCs before promotion - e.g. GuardDuty Malware Protection for AWS Backup, or restore into an isolated clean-room recovery account and run malware/EDR scans and integrity diffing before reconnecting. Track backup-content provenance: flag recovery points created during a known dwell/compromise window as untrusted and require validation, not just a readable-restore check.

**References.** [AWS Backup Vault Lock](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/vault-lock.html) · [Amazon S3 Object Lock](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/features/object-lock/) · [Malware protection in AWS Backup](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/malware-protection.html) · [Scan backups for malware with Amazon GuardDuty Malware Protection for AWS Backup](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/storage/scan-backups-for-malware-with-amazon-guardduty-malware-protection-for-aws-backup/) · [Cyber resilience on AWS: A reference approach for recovery from ransomware and destructive events](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/architecture/cyber-resilience-on-aws-a-reference-approach-for-recovery-from-ransomware-and-destructive-events/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Immutable and air-gapped backups are the headline 2025-2026 ransomware defense, with 89% of orgs reporting backup repositories targeted, but the entire control protects against post-write tampering, not pre-write malice - and immutability means a payload captured into a snapshot is preserved as faithfully as the real data. The unmodeled seam is provenance: the restore path trusts a snapshot's integrity (bytes unchanged) as if it were authenticity (bytes were benign at capture), so an attacker who pre-stages a dormant implant before the backup window has it locked into the protected copy and re-armed on recovery. This is distinct from generic backup-destruction tactics (which attack availability) and from the keylog-export archival gap because the trust object is the snapshot's assumed cleanliness as a recovery source, weaponizing immutability itself to guarantee the implant's survival across the very event meant to evict the attacker. Pre-restore deep content scanning of immutable copies and detection of dormant payloads inside backups are nascent, and most recovery runbooks restore-then-scan rather than scan-then-restore.

**How to validate it.** During a simulated dwell period, write a dormant payload (backdoor/logic bomb/staged credential) into production state. Let the scheduled backup capture it into an immutable/object-locked snapshot. Later simulate an incident and run the standard restore path. Confirm the restore reintroduces the implant into production without re-scanning snapshot contents, and that immutability preserved the malicious bytes exactly as it preserves legitimate ones.

**Refuted if.** The restore pipeline mandates malware/integrity scanning and clean-room validation of snapshot contents before reintroduction (e.g. Rubrik/Cohesity/Veeam threat-hunting on backups, isolated recovery environment), catching the pre-staged payload - so 'restored from clean backup' is actually verified clean.

**Prerequisites.** Pre-encryption dwell time with write access to production state during a backup window; later, a restore event (attacker need not control the restore).

**Existing controls.** Backup-anomaly/malware scanning (Rubrik, Cohesity, Veeam Secure Restore), isolated recovery environments, and dwell-time detection address this; many orgs restore without re-scanning. Re-arming via restored implants is a recognized ransomware-resilience concern.

**Proof-of-concept — Backup snapshot scan: pre-staged payload trusted by restore-as-integrity (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. READ-ONLY scan of a sample restore set / snapshot manifest (yours) for a pre-staged payload not covered by a verified checksum.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-backup-snapshot-integrity-prestaged-restore-trust
# READ-ONLY scan. A snapshot manifest lists files + a checksum allowlist. The restore
# trusts 'present in snapshot' as integrity. Flags any file in the set NOT covered by
# a verified checksum (a pre-staged payload that would auto-restore).
SNAPSHOT = {
  "files": ["app/config.yaml","app/index.html","app/.startup/payload.sh"],  # last is pre-staged
  "verified_checksums": {"app/config.yaml":"ok","app/index.html":"ok"},      # payload NOT signed
}
def scan(snap):
    unverified = [f for f in snap["files"] if f not in snap["verified_checksums"]]
    suspicious = [f for f in unverified if any(s in f for s in (".startup",".sh",".cron","autorun"))]
    return unverified, suspicious
if __name__ == "__main__":
    unverified, suspicious = scan(SNAPSHOT)
    print(f"files={SNAPSHOT['files']}")
    print(f"unverified_by_checksum={unverified}")
    print(f"executable_prestaged={suspicious}")
    if unverified:
        print("VULNERABLE: snapshot contains unverified (pre-staged) file restore would trust as integral")
    else:
        print("PASS: every restore-set file covered by a verified checksum")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: snapshot contains unverified (pre-staged) file restore would trust as integral' and lists app/.startup/payload.sh.  
*Cleanup:* none (read-only)  
*Safety:* Read-only inspection of an in-memory manifest; no files written/executed, no restore performed.

**Research hook.** Model the dwell-window-to-snapshot capture of a dormant implant and demonstrate that immutability preserves it and restore re-arms it; build a pre-restore introspection layer that mounts and deep-scans immutable snapshots for implants/logic bombs before reintroduction, and quantify how long pre-staged payloads survive across backup-retention and restore cycles.


## 114. Credential and token harvest from inherited notebook kernel context

| | |
|---|---|
| **Seam id** | `GH10-notebook-kernel-credential-inheritance-trust` |
| **Primitive** | P4 Context-Inheritance |
| **Trust frame** | Human Operator / User + Supply Chain / Build → Cloud Control Plane + Vector Store / RAG Index |
| **Category** | Cloud Attacks; Credential Access |
| **Validity** | plausible (medium confidence) |
| **MITRE** | T1552.001, T1552.005, CWE-522, CWE-668 |
| **Maturity / tooling / detection** | emerging / nascent / none |

**The trust assumption.** A managed notebook environment (Databricks/SageMaker/Vertex notebooks, Jupyter/Colab) trusts that any code executed in a kernel is the authoring data scientist's intent, so it gives the kernel ambient inheritance of the user's full data-plane authority - attached workspace credentials, instance/role profiles, mounted lakehouse and warehouse connections, and cached cloud tokens - and treats the running kernel as the user, with no boundary between notebook content and the standing credentials the kernel can reach.

**The attack.** An attacker delivers code into a kernel - a shared/imported notebook, a malicious pip dependency, an %autoreload-triggered module, or an LLM-suggested cell - and that code, running with the kernel's inherited ambient authority, enumerates and exfiltrates the attached credentials and reaches every data system the kernel can touch, because the environment never distinguishes 'the human chose to run this' from 'code in the kernel ran'; the notebook's standing access to lakehouse, warehouse, and secret scopes becomes the blast radius of any code execution, with actions attributed to the data scientist.

**Mechanism (technical).** Managed-notebook kernels (SageMaker notebook instances, Databricks, Vertex/Colab, Jupyter) execute user cells with the ambient data-plane authority of the compute: an attached IAM execution role reachable via the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS), mounted lakehouse storage, and cached/passthrough tokens. Any code that runs in that kernel - a malicious cell in a shared notebook, a typosquatted pip dependency, or a poisoned dependency in a requirements file - inherits this authority transparently and can mint role credentials by querying IMDS (169.254.169.254). The notebook execution role is frequently over-privileged (e.g. AmazonSageMakerFullAccess), so a single rogue cell can read S3 buckets, create VPC endpoints, or exfil tokens. The trust violation is that arbitrary third-party code is run with the user's full cloud identity rather than a sandboxed scope.

**Exploitation primitive.** From within the kernel, query http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role> (or read passthrough/cached tokens) and exfiltrate the temporary credentials, then replay them off-box with the notebook role's permissions.

**MITRE gap.** Closest IDs are T1552.005 (Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API) and T1552.001 (Credentials in Files). These describe IMDS scraping and file-resident secrets generically, but miss the managed-ML-notebook trust model: that a data scientist's interactive kernel silently confers the user's full data-plane identity to any imported dependency or shared cell, that supply-chain (typosquat pip) is the delivery vector, and that the notebook execution role's data access (lakehouse mounts, passthrough tokens) is a distinct privilege surface ATT&CK does not model as a unit.

**Real-world anchors.**
- *incident:* Lightspin: AWS SageMaker Jupyter Notebook Instance Takeover — Documented cross-account technique stealing the notebook execution role token via the IMDS endpoint and abusing AmazonSageMakerFullAccess. — <https://blog.lightspin.io/aws-sagemaker-notebook-takeover-vulnerability>
- *product:* SageMaker Notebook Instances IMDSv2 support — AWS feature/guidance acknowledging IMDS credential exposure risk on notebook instances and adding IMDSv2 restriction. — <https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/amazon-sagemaker-notebook-instances-now-support-configuring-and-restricting-imds-versions/>
- *product:* Databricks IAM credential passthrough (legacy/deprecated) — Passthrough surfaces user cloud creds to the cluster/notebook context; now deprecated (Runtime 15.0+) in favor of Unity Catalog for governance reasons. — <https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/archive/credential-passthrough/>

**Detection (technical).** Alert on IMDS calls (169.254.169.254 / link-local metadata) originating from notebook/kernel processes, especially to the iam/security-credentials path; enforce IMDSv2 with hop-limit 1 to block proxied access. Detect use of notebook-role credentials from an IP/ASN outside the notebook subnet (credential-replay off-box), and monitor pip/conda installs in kernels for typosquatted or newly-published packages. Prefer scoped, short-lived data access (Unity Catalog / least-privilege execution roles) over passthrough or full-access policies.

**References.** [Lightspin - AWS SageMaker Jupyter Notebook Instance Takeover](https://blog.lightspin.io/aws-sagemaker-notebook-takeover-vulnerability) · [Amazon SageMaker Notebook Instances now support configuring and restricting IMDS versions](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/amazon-sagemaker-notebook-instances-now-support-configuring-and-restricting-imds-versions/) · [Databricks Credential passthrough (legacy)](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/archive/credential-passthrough/) · [MITRE ATT&CK T1552.005 - Cloud Instance Metadata API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1552/005/)

**Why it's new (and what MITRE misses).** Managed notebooks are where data scientists hold the widest standing access to the data estate in 2025-2027 - kernels run with attached instance roles, workspace credentials, mounted warehouse/lakehouse connections, and secret scopes - and the kernel is trusted as the user with no separation between notebook code and those ambient credentials. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: any code reaching the kernel (shared notebook, poisoned dependency, autoreloaded module, or AI-suggested cell) inherits the human's full data-plane authority, so a single code-execution event in a high-access notebook is equivalent to handing over the user's entire data reach. This is distinct from CI build-secret sprawl (durable byproducts of an ephemeral job) and from the citizen-dev connector-borrow gap (low-code shared connections) because the trust object is the interactive notebook kernel's ambient inheritance of a high-privilege human's live data credentials, weaponized by arbitrary in-kernel code and now amplified by AI cell-completion that authors code the human merely accepts. Detection that distinguishes notebook code acting on credentials from the human's interactive intent is absent, and kernel-level credential isolation is immature.

**How to validate it.** In a managed notebook (Databricks/SageMaker/Vertex) attached to an instance role and workspace credentials, run a cell containing attacker-delivered code (shared/imported notebook, malicious pip dependency, or %autoreload-triggered module). Have it enumerate ambient credentials - instance metadata role tokens, dbutils.secrets / mounted scopes, cached cloud tokens, attached lakehouse/warehouse connections - and exfiltrate or use them to reach data systems. Confirm actions execute with the kernel's full inherited authority and are attributed to the data scientist.

**Refuted if.** The kernel runs with a scoped, non-ambient identity (no IMDS access, secrets brokered per-call with policy, no standing data-plane mounts) such that arbitrary code cannot enumerate or reuse the user's credentials beyond an explicit, audited boundary.

**Prerequisites.** Ability to get code into a kernel the victim runs: a shared notebook, a poisoned PyPI dependency, an auto-reloaded module, or an LLM-suggested cell.

**Existing controls.** IMDSv2 hop-limit, scoped instance roles, Databricks secret scopes/credential passthrough, and dependency scanning reduce blast radius; ambient credential inheritance in notebooks remains the default. Notebook/CI credential harvest via malicious deps is an established class.

**Proof-of-concept — Notebook kernel credential inheritance: cell inherits full data-plane creds (`python`).**
> ⚠ Authorized lab/non-prod or owned-asset audit only, signed RoE.

*Setup:* python3 stdlib only. Local stub: a notebook kernel started with the data-scientist's ambient creds; an arbitrary cell inherits and uses them.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# GH10-notebook-kernel-credential-inheritance-trust
# Local STUB. The kernel process holds the data scientist's full data-plane creds in
# its environment. ANY cell (incl. a copy-pasted/imported one) inherits them with no
# per-cell scoping/consent. Shows a cell reading the ambient credential.
import os
# kernel launched with broad ambient creds (defanged)
KERNEL_ENV = {
  "WAREHOUSE_TOKEN": "FAKE-DW-TOKEN",      # full read/write to data warehouse
  "S3_ACCESS_KEY":   "FAKE-AKIA",
  "SCOPE":           "warehouse:rw,lake:rw,secrets:read",
}
def arbitrary_cell():
    # an untrusted cell simply reads the inherited environment -- no boundary
    inherited = {k:("<present FAKE>" if v else None) for k,v in KERNEL_ENV.items()}
    return inherited, KERNEL_ENV.get("SCOPE","")
if __name__ == "__main__":
    inherited, scope = arbitrary_cell()
    print(f"kernel_scope={scope}")
    print(f"cell_inherited_creds={list(inherited.keys())}")
    if inherited and "rw" in scope:
        print("VULNERABLE: arbitrary notebook cell inherited data-scientist's full data-plane creds (no per-cell scoping)")
    else:
        print("PASS: cell runs with scoped/short-lived per-cell credentials")
```

*Success:* Prints 'VULNERABLE: arbitrary notebook cell inherited data-scientist's full data-plane creds' listing WAREHOUSE_TOKEN/S3_ACCESS_KEY.  
*Cleanup:* none (in-memory stub)  
*Safety:* FAKE tokens in a local dict, not real env; prints presence not values; no warehouse/S3 contacted.

**Research hook.** Demonstrate credential harvest and cross-system reach from a single malicious cell or imported notebook in a managed environment with attached roles/secret scopes, including via an AI-suggested cell; prototype kernel-side credential isolation that brokers data-plane access per-action with human-presence confirmation, and detection that flags in-kernel credential enumeration distinct from interactive query patterns.
